Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

Section (1) of the Firearms Act, 1925 provides that “The expression ‘prohibited weapon means to include any
weapon of whatever description designed for the discharge of any noxious liquid, noxious gas or other
noxious thing…” Subsection (e) of the definition of Firearms under section 1(1) makes a prohibited weapon
a ‘Firearm’.
The main types of anti-personnel sprays include:
• CN spray: 2-Chloroacetophenone is the active ingredient of the anti-personnel spray known as
“CN” or “Chemical Mace”
• CS spray: 2-Chlorobenzylidenemalonitrile is the active ingredient of the anti-personnel spray
known as “CS”
• Pepper spray: Capsaicin and Dihydrocapsaicin are components of Oleoresin Capsicum, a natural
product extracted from peppers. These and related compounds, known collectively as capsaicinoids,
are the active ingredients in “pepper spray”
• PAVA spray: Pellargonic acid vanillylamide (PAVA) is a synthetic version of the “pepper spray”
described above
According to the scientific literature, the active ingredients in these sprays, when sprayed at or towards
another person, can produce a variety of sensations including intense burning in the skin, temporary blindness
(when dispersed into the eye), severe pain and irritation of the respiratory system.

Potential of the evidence
The contents of suspect canisters are analysed for chemical composition and the effects of the sprays are
included in the laboratory report. These reports will be of assistance to the court in a prosecution under either
of the above Acts.

When should the evidence be considered?
When canisters are thought to contain a noxious liquid and/or used against a person(s).

Samples
When handling canisters it is necessary to be careful not to spray them, especially at oneself.
Secure the canister in a nylon bag if it is leaking or if there is a noxious smell from it.

7.14. Fires and Arson
General
The forensic investigation of fires is a necessary and challenging duty which An Garda Siochana is tasked
with performing.
All fires are investigated to:
• Determine whether they were malicious or accidental
• Determine whether the fire was set to cover up another crime

Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

Fatal fires are investigated:
• To determine if the fire was malicious or accidental
• Determine the cause of the fire for the Coroners inquest
All fires should be treated as suspicious until proven otherwise.

Preservation
It is the duty of the first responder to a fire scene to preserve it for forensic investigation. Access to the scene
should be limited to fire fighters, medical personnel, specialist services such as ESB, Bord Gais and so on whilst
carrying out their fire fighting, rescuing or safety duties.

Fire Scene Investigation
Fire scenes are examined by Crime Scene Examiners who have been trained and equipped to carry out this
function. Due to the destructive nature of fire the cause of an outbreak may not be readily obvious. The
investigator should obtain information relating to the circumstances of the fire, the premises and the occupiers
before the fire scene is examined.
The following sources of information should be considered:
• Members of An Garda Siochana; report of first responder relating to circumstances, statements by
witnesses, injured parties, property details
• Fire Brigade; Including time of arrival, spread of fire, security of property, position of injured parties,
witnesses etc, how fire was fought, potential dangers in the scene, observations and relevant information
• Utility personnel; including Bord Gais, ESB and local authorities
• Electronic systems examination; including fire alarm activation zones, burglar alarm activation zones,
CCTV footage

Health & Safety
Buildings can become structurally unsound following fire damage. Roofs and ceilings damaged by fire can
collapse or loose debris can fall. The crime scene examiner should ascertain the condition of the building
from the Chief Fire Officer or if circumstances warrant it then an engineer should assess the building before
it is entered.
Protective boots, clothing and hard hat should always be worn. The dangers of gas cylinders, chemicals and
noxious gases should be kept in mind. Examiners should ensure the scene is adequately ventilated, well
illuminated and avoid working alone.

Fire Point of Origin
The fire scene is examined to determine as far as possible:
• The point(s) of origin of the fire
• The source of ignition
• The presence of accelerants
• How the fire progressed

Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

Note taking, photography and examination for trace evidence should all be done in accordance with best
practice. Any items removed for further examination or any debris taken for accelerant analysis should be
properly packaged and labelled.
Other evidence such as fingerprints, footprints, glass, paint, blood, fibres and so on may also be present and
may link a possible suspect to the scene.

Expert Assistance
Assistance is available from members of the Technical Bureau (Ballistics Section) and the Forensic Science
Laboratory (Chemistry Section). Advice can be given by phone or experts can visit the scene to assist and
advise. When considered necessary by the District Officer and sanctioned by Detective Superintendent,
Technical Bureau a full Technical Bureau team can be sent to investigate the scene.

Further Examinations
Electrical appliances, equipment or other items such as gas heaters suspected of causing a fire can be
examined at either the Ballistics section or the Forensic Science Laboratory.
Other items, which may be of assistance to the investigation, are samples of clothing, footwear and so on that
can also be tested for the presence of flammable liquids.

Packaging
It is important that the samples are properly packaged for
transportation to the laboratory. All samples should be placed
in nylon bags, which should be folded over and sealed
with Sellotape or tied in a knot at the opening. This should
be done as soon as possible so that any flammable liquid present
is not lost and to ensure that the samples are protected from
outside contamination.
Liquid samples should be sealed in glass vials or metal containers
with screw-cap lids. If the suspect’s clothing is to be examined,
these items should be packed in nylon bags and care should be
taken to ensure that there is no contact between the clothing
and other items from the scene.
The Forensic Science Laboratory’s involvement in suspected arson cases is in the examination of fire debris and
other samples from the seat of the fire. The samples will be checked for the presence of hydrocarbon fire
accelerants such as petrol, diesel oil, paraffin oil or white spirit. The presence of any such material in the
samples taken could point towards arson depending on the circumstances of the case. For example, finding
white spirit in a shed containing a lot of paint products may not be significant.

Indications of Arson
• The scene examination reveals more than one seat of fire
• Evidence of flammable liquids is found
• Containers or other items alien to the scene are found
• Evidence of forced entry is found
• Fire or intruder alarms have been tampered with
• The fire started in an improbable location
• All accidental causes are deemed to be highly unlikely
• The fire forms part of a trend

7.15. Forensic Liaison Office
The Forensic Liaison Office FLO is the central area for acceptance and processing of items of possible evidential
value submitted by Divisional Exhibit Officers, Station Exhibit Officers in the Dublin Metropolitan Region (DMR)
and others, for examination at the Technical Bureau and the Forensic Science Laboratory.
An identifying Technical Bureau number is allocated to all cases prior to delivery to the relevant section for
examination. Following examination, exhibits are returned to FLO. It is the responsibility of Divisional or
Station Exhibit Officers to collect completed exhibits at the FLO and return them to investigators. All sharp
items such as needles and knives must be packaged in special containers issued for this purpose.
All relevant details of the case, the items submitted and the services requested are entered on an Exhibit
Tracking System which tracks exhibits as they are processed in different service areas within the Technical
Bureau. A help line for all investigating members is available in the FLO.

Destruction of Controlled Drugs
The FLO has responsibility for the destruction of seizures of controlled drugs. All controlled drugs sent to the
FLO for destruction must be supported by the appropriate documentation. This will include an accurate list of
case numbers (in numerical order), Technical Bureau numbers, Forensic Science Laboratory numbers and
Station Drugs Register numbers. A description of the drugs in each case will be given; this should include the
number of tablets, number of drugs, the weight of the resin, powder or tablets and so on.

All drugs will be presented in sealed bags and each package will
be clearly identifiable and properly labelled (indicating contents
and reference numbers). Under no circumstances should
needles, sharp instruments, bottles, liquids or any items other
than drugs be submitted. Most importantly each page listing
cases for destruction will include a declaration that these cases
have been checked and are to be destroyed. This declaration
will be signed by a member not below the rank of Inspector.
Members who wish to have controlled drugs destroyed will
make an appointment with a specific member of the FLO in
order to facilitate the administrative process. The number of
cases being accepted in the FLO at any one appointment time
may be limited as necessary. Where the documentation
referred to above or the packages themselves are not in order,
the cases will not be accepted.

7.16. Services available at the Technical Bureau
Major incidents: crime scene call-out procedure
Urgent applications for technical assistance should be made and signed by the District Officer from the
requesting district or an Inspector acting as District Officer and forwarded by fax to Detective Superintendent,
Technical Bureau. Written requests for assistance should include all available information.
Outside office hours, such requests should be forwarded to the Inspector-in-charge, Command and Control
who will on receipt of the request contact the Detective Inspector on duty at the Technical Bureau (or in their
absence, a senior Detective Sergeant acting for the Detective Inspector). The Detective Inspector / Detective
Sergeant acting at the Technical Bureau will consult with the senior member on duty at other core sections to
agree a priority rating and advise the Detective Superintendent of their agreed rating to the call-out.

Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

Where necessary, the Detective Superintendent will consult with Chief Superintendent, Technical Bureau prior
to authorising a team for major incidents. The Inspector in charge, Command and Control will be notified if
attendance is approved and informed of the expected time of arrival of the Technical Bureau team.
Non-urgent applications for technical assistance should be forwarded by fax or ordinary post to Detective
Superintendent, Technical Bureau.
Crime Scene Management
In certain major incidents, a Crime Scene Manager CSM will be appointed by Senior Management of the
Technical Bureau to manage and co-ordinate all operations in the examination of scenes and to liaise with the
Senior Investigating Officer and Incident Room Co-ordinator. It is the responsibility of the CSM to maximise
the potential of the forensic sciences in order to identify the offender(s) in the incident.
This is achieved by utilising a systematic and methodical approach to each identified scene in order to record
forensic intelligence and evidential material and to maintain the integrity and continuity of evidence whilst
negating any risk of contamination. This is done by establishing specific actions to be carried out in order of
priority and in agreement with the Senior Investigating Officer (SIO)
Role of Crime Scene Manager
Prior to arrival at scene:
• Coordinating travel and ensuring that teams leave for scenes as soon as possible
• Liaising with the Local District Officer for briefing purposes
• Selecting an appropriate rendezvous point near the scene before examinations commence
• Determining the integrity of the scene by examining the scene preservation log and so on
At the scene:
• Carrying out preliminary survey of scene
• Prioritisation, after evaluation of the scene, the needs of specialists from the Technical Bureau
and Forensic Science Laboratory
• Contacting and briefing the Detective Superintendent, Technical Bureau on the first day at scene and
delivering regular updates during the course of examination
• Holding regular meetings at location with team members to ensure that their needs are provided for
• Agreeing priorities with team members in relation to best evidence, resolving conflicts or referring
same to Detective Superintendent, Technical Bureau
• Advising the Detective Superintendent, Technical Bureau of the necessity for the services of a Forensic
Scientist at the scene
• Maintaining regular contact with Incident Rooms and advising team members of new developments
and reviewing the progress of the examination of the scene
• Seeking, through the District Officer, the assistance of local Divisional Crime scene Examiner to
assist team as appropriate

Prior to departure:
• Conduct a final survey of the scene prior to departure. When all are satisfied, the Local District Officer
will be invited to the scene, informed of the extent of the examination and invited to sign a hand over
document (incorporated in log)
• Conducting a final walk through and search of scene assisted by local personnel
• The Team Manager will arrange for a debriefing conference between all members of the team at a
convenient time and place prior to departure from a scene
After departure:
• As soon as possible after returning to the Technical Bureau submitting the Crime Scene Log and
Management Report to the Detective Superintendent, Technical Bureau and reporting separately on any
issue arising from the examination of scene
• Thereafter maintaining contact with the Incident Room ensuring that the investigators are kept abreast
of any developments in the examination of items at the Technical Bureau and advising experts who
attended the scene of developments at location
• Coordinating the supply of reports / statements and holding a team conference prior to their delivery
to the Incident Rooms

Chapter 8 The Investigation of Crime in General

8.1. Introduction
While each crime investigation may have its own unique characteristics, these are common procedures to be
followed in all investigations.
This Chapter outlines the standard procedures to be followed in the investigation of crime in general. This is
followed by certain additional procedures applicable to the investigation of the major crimes as detailed.

8.2. Pre-arranged plan of action
Every District should have a pre arranged plan of action which provides in detail for the following:
• Action to be taken when a member receives a report at a station of a suspected crime
• Steps to be taken when a member who is away from their station receives a report of a suspected crime
or when they discover a crime
• The action to be taken on receipt of a report of a suspected crime in regard to the speedy despatch of
personnel to the scene, bearing in mind that preservation measures at the scene should be taken
without delay
• The measures to be put into operation for the purpose of intercepting and searching for the perpetrator(s)
of a crime
• The arrangements to be made with other districts for the setting up of checkpoints and a speedy means
of communication to bring the checkpoints into operation
• The personnel who should be notified when a suspected crime is reported
The plan should be reviewed frequently to ensure that all aspects are adequately dealt with and, in particular,
to ensure that reports of all suspected crimes are investigated efficiently. Every member of a District should be
fully conversant with all details of the plan and be in a position to act without reference to the plan. This may
require regular training seminars and briefings.
The following kits and items should be available at each District Headquarters Station:
• Firearms Residue Kit
• Sexual Offences Kit (issued by the Forensic Science Laboratory [FSL])
• An adequate supply of writing materials including stationery, pencils and pens
• A map of the Division, District and Sub-District
• Measuring tapes
• Early Evidence Kits (issued by the FSL)
• Hair combing kits (issued by the FSL)
• Explosives kits (issued by the FSL)

Chapter 8 The Investigation of Crime in General

A properly stocked Incident Room must be maintained in each District/Division. Adequate stocks of the
following materials must be maintained at all times and a member assigned responsibility for this function:
• Jobs Books
• Books for Scene Preserved
• Camera/Video Equipment
• Tents
• Ground Sheets
• White Suits
• Masks
• Gloves
• Evidence Bags
• Scene of Crime Rolls
• Equipment for Divisional Search Team
• Fingerprinting Kits
The objective of crime scene examination is to try to interpret what has happened in the first instance and to
collect and preserve all available materials that could potentially be used as evidence with a view to bringing
those responsible to Justice. The professional management of the crime scene is crucial.

8.3. Action of first responders to arrive at scene
The success of an investigation that involves a definable crime scene depends primarily on the initial
observations and actions of the first responders to arrive at the scene. This generally applies regardless of the
type of crime.
While the circumstances of the particular case will naturally govern the action taken by the first member(s) to
arrive at the scene, the following matters should be attended to:

Upon arrival:
• Protect the crime scene; see 8.5 below
• Obtain names and addresses of persons present at scene on arrival; this should include all contact details
including mobile phone numbers and so on
• Obtain details of all persons who entered the scene or were observed in the vicinity of the scene prior to
the arrival of the first member
• Locate and interview the victim and witnesses, if present; note names and addresses and record stories
briefly. Where possible have witnesses verify these accounts by signature(s)

• In eye witness cases, secure a description of the perpetrators and vehicles (if any) used and direction of
flight
• Note precise time of arrival and weather conditions

Injured persons:
• If there is a body at the scene, call a doctor immediately either to certify death or to provide medical
assistance
• If injured persons are discovered at scene, arrange for medical attention and identification
• If any person appears to be seriously injured and is being conveyed to hospital, arrange (if possible) to have
them accompanied by a member but the preservation of the scene must take precedence
• If appropriate, be prepared to record a dying declaration
• If a person seriously injured is being removed to hospital (and no member is accompanying him) or if such
a person has already been removed, take steps to have a dying declaration taken
• In rendering medical attention to an injured person, place no restrictions but make a careful written note
of anything disturbed

Recording details and other actions:
• Determine how the crime was
committed, the extent of personal
injuries and the nature and value of
property stolen (if any). Remember to
properly categorise and record the
incidents. Comply with all PULSE
instructions and crime recording rules.
• Seize as soon as possible all copies of
CCTV videos and so on in the immediate
area
• Arrange to have the following details
communicated to the appropriate
station:
• Type and nature of crime and time of
commission
• Description of property stolen (if any)
• Descriptions of suspect(s)
• Description of transport used and
direction of flight
Comply with relevant PULSE instructions and crime recording rules. When two or more members arrive at the
scene, arrangements should be made to divide these duties.
It is absolutely critical to the overall success of the investigation that the above procedures are carried out
intelligently by the first members to arrive at the scene. This will avoid hours of unnecessary enquiry and the
assistance provided will prove invaluable in bringing a case to a successful conclusion.

Chapter 8 The Investigation of Crime in General

8.4. Safety precautions at certain potentially dangerous crime
scenes
In any crime scene involving explosives the assistance of the Defence Forces Explosive Ordinance Disposal team
should immediately be sought.
In all cases where an explosion has occurred, including safe-breakings, an expert should be consulted without
delay to make sure that no danger exists from explosives or gases.
At all scenes of burglary, whether or not an explosion has occurred, any safes on the premises should be
approached with caution because of the possibility that explosives may have been inserted and the criminal
disturbed before the initiation of the explosion.
On no account should any radio transmitter be used in or near such buildings and electric devices such as light
switches, bells, mobile phones, radios and so on should not be operated.
Members are warned to take special care at the scenes of safe breakings or other similar crimes where
oxyacetylene equipment has been used in a room or building. The atmosphere of the room or building could
be extremely dangerous after the use of this equipment.
This danger arises from the following factors either alone or together:
• Oxygen enrichment of the atmosphere;
• Acetylene enrichment of the atmosphere;
• A dangerous mixture of oxygen and acetylene in the atmosphere;
• A flash back in the equipment or cylinder.
Such dangers arise particularly when the oxy acetylene equipment has been used inexpertly by a person in
ignorance or where an expert operator has been disturbed during operations. Alternatively, a maliciously
minded criminal who had opened an empty safe could deliberately leave such a situation.
The following precautions should be taken by those first on the scene:
• Do not smoke or use a naked light, mobile phone or radio equipment near to or adjacent to the actual
scene;
• Shut off oxy acetylene cylinders immediately;
• Feel cylinders to establish if warm or hot;
• If they are hot, clear the building immediately;
• Direct a water hose on warm or hot cylinders from a safe distance and call the fire brigade.
With regard to all of the above there are Health and Safety issues involved and suspected hazards should
properly be dealt with by a Defence Forces Explosives Ordinance Disposal (EOD) team or the Fire Brigade
depending on the circumstances.

Chapter 8 The Investigation of Crime in General

8.5. Preservation of the scene
There is no definite rule or set of rules that can be applied to defining the dimensions of the scene of a crime.
The best physical evidence is normally found at or near the site of the most critical action taken by the criminal
against the property or victim; thus, one is more likely to find important physical evidence in the immediate
area surrounding the body in a homicide case than some distance away.
Similarly, the point of forcible entry into a building or the area immediately surrounding a cracked safe
normally has the greater potential for producing evidence.
While it is quite possible that the dimensions of a crime scene will be large, there will usually be obvious
priority areas that should be given immediate protection. On the other hand, valuable evidence may be
discarded or inadvertently deposited by the criminal at some distance from the immediate scene. Therefore,
the area to be protected may eventually extend far beyond the limits of that considered to have the higher
priority.
Evidence is always present at the scene of a crime.
Whether it is found is another matter. That it is not found does not prove its absence.
Failure to find evidence at the scene of a
crime may be due, among other things, to
faulty crime scene preservation, careless or
inadequate search procedures, or poor
investigation.
Members who protect the scene and the
members who search the scene play a
critical role in determining whether the
scientific expertise available will be utilised
to the best advantage.
The first responders to arrive at the
scene of a suspected crime automatically
incur the responsibility of preserving
the scene until otherwise instructed.
If the first responder to arrive at the scene is in doubt as to whether there has been a crime at all, the worst
should be suspected and the fullest possible precautions taken.
In some cases, valuable traces may well have been obliterated by other persons who were at the scene before
the arrival of the first member; this is unavoidable in the present state of education of the public in these
matters, but after the first members arrive steps should be taken to ensure that no more useful traces are lost.
Responders should be cognisant of the presence of the media as material gathered may be utilised in Court
proceedings regarding professional standards.
Except where they are required to prevent the escape of a criminal still in the vicinity, other members not
directly involved in the crime scene investigation should not encroach on the scene of a suspected crime until it
has been completely examined.
The member(s) preserving the scene of a suspected crime should record in a log book the identity of each

Chapter 8 The Investigation of Crime in General

person (including members of the Force) who enters the scene, and include their time of arrival and reasons
for entering the scene.

This log book becomes an exhibit and each entry will require a statement to
support it from the relevant member. Each person who enters the crime scene
will also be required to make a statement covering their role and presence
there.
All major incident scenes should be recorded on video. Persons who are required to attend at a crime scene
must wear proper apparel.

What Preservation of a Scene Means
• Ensuring that the scene remains unchanged from the time of arrival of the first member until the scene has
been completely examined and evidence collected
• Delimiting the scene as best as possible, i.e., to isolate it from its surroundings and to do so in such a way
that no valuable clue is lost because the boundary has been drawn too narrowly
• Preventing any person (excluding authorised personnel) from entering upon the scene or moving, touching
or interfering with articles at the scene prior to completion of examination of the scene. This may involve
enlisting the aid of reliable civilians at the outset. It may also be necessary to have more than one or two
members preserving the scene, such as in the case of hotels or apartments
• If the scene is in a public thoroughfare, re routing traffic away from the scene, if necessary
• Taking extreme care to avoid the obliteration of such valuable clues as finger-marks, footmarks, tyre marks,
blood, etc which may have been left at the scene
• Taking early action to protect items of possible evidential value that may be destroyed by rain, snow, fire or
other causes before the scene is examined. For example, a raincoat or piece of canvas or plastic may be
used to cover impressions in the earth that are exposed to rain. Buckets and wooden or cardboard boxes
may be placed over obvious traces such as indentations, impressions or stains, whether the crime has
occurred indoors or in the open. Items that will melt should be shielded from the sun or other heat sources
• The member(s) preserving the scene should not interfere with objects with or without gloves as this could
destroy valuable fingerprint evidence. Under no circumstances should the member(s) concerned or anyone
else be permitted to make comparisons by placing shoes or boots into footmarks
• If it appears that there is a likelihood of evidence of tyre marks, tracks or footprints, care should be taken
to keep other vehicles or persons arriving at the scene well clear of these marks
• The member(s) preserving the scene should not allow any other person to add anything to the scene. This
includes cigarette ends

Indoor Scenes
It is generally not a difficult task to preserve an indoor scene as access can easily be restricted, but it must be
remembered that the criminal's possible entry and exit paths from the central scene must also be preserved.
Do not make phone calls from the injured party’s telephone.
All persons who may be within the building should be warned not to touch or disturb anything and
unauthorised persons should be prevented from entering the building. Remove animals from inside.

Chapter 8 The Investigation of Crime in General

In a case of possible homicide, no member should use the toilet, turn on water or use towels at the scene until
it has been examined by authorised personnel.
If the possible exit or entry path of the criminal was via a particular entrance, all authorised personnel entering
the scene should do so by some other entrance, if possible.

Outdoor Scenes
All unauthorised persons should be prevented from entering upon the scene.
An ample area – a minimum of twenty square metres – and including possible entry and exit paths taken by
the criminal should be roped off or barricaded. Persons authorised to enter the scene should do so via the
same path (which should not be the possible entry or exit paths taken by the criminal) and for this purpose
consideration should be given to the laying out of tapes as route markers to the scene.
For each major crime, a separate record should be kept showing particulars of the member(s) responsible for
preserving the scene (giving times and dates as appropriate) from the time of discovery by Gardai until the
examination of the scene has been completed.
The member in charge of the investigation, on arrival at the scene, should see that the preservation of the
scene continues and they should rectify any omission. This will include a check on the precautions already
taken to ensure that the scene is not disturbed except what is necessary to safeguard items of evidence.

If rain or dew is likely, clothing, weapons, bloodstains, and so on should be
covered for protection. Use a screen, tent or ground sheet where necessary and
appropriate.

8.6. Duties of member in charge of investigation
The initial duties of the member in charge of the investigation on arrival at the scene are:

Preparatory:
• Set up an incident room with appropriate facilities and staff
• To check that the procedures set out at 8.3/8.5 have been or are being complied with and rectify any
omissions immediately
• To appoint an Exhibits Officer
• To ascertain if a crime was committed and, if in doubt, suspect the worst. Note the importance of such
in missing persons cases
• To seek out the best immediate informants including Gardai and other witnesses
• To arrange for a complete and thorough examination of the scene

Witnesses, suspects and searches:
• To detail members to locate witnesses in a neighbourhood; house to house enquiries are a vital
component in this regard. See 8.25 below and Chapter 3

8.7. Services of the Technical Bureau
The services provided by the Technical Bureau are available for the investigation of crime of a serious,
complicated or technical nature anywhere in the State. A written application for technical assistance should be
made where appropriate. When a team from Technical Bureau is assigned to a crime scene a Crime Scene
Manager may also be appointed.

particular cases.
Requests for the pathologist and forensic scientist should be made in writing to Assistant Commissioner,

will assist the investigation.

8.9. Services of the Forensic Science Laboratory
Forensic scientists attend scenes where there is mutual agreement that a scientist can add value to the
investigation. The main purpose of their presence is to use their scientific expertise in the selection of samples
that are most likely to yield information on the perpetrator or the sequence of events leading up to, during, or
after the incident. The scientist also provides advice on the best way to preserve and remove these samples
and may apply techniques not available to the Scene of Crime Officer to locate or enhance the scientific
evidence.
The types of scene most frequently attended are those which involve fatalities but scientists also go to
explosive cases and suspect drug factories. Examples of cases where the attendance at a scene by a scientist is
unlikely to be beneficial would be shootings where there has been no contact between victim and assailant.
Another example would be deaths following street violence.

meeting in the laboratory.

8.10. Searching the crime scene
The objective of Crime Scene Search examination is to retrieve the maximum amount of evidence. This can
only be achieved by a professional and methodical approach. The ability to discover and recognise evidence at
the scene of a crime is an essential requirement for successful searching.
The scene of a crime should only be searched by members who have received training in this type of work.
Each crime scene is different according to the physical nature of the scene. The searcher should first of all
survey the scene and endeavour to develop some concept of the nature of the evidence which should be the
objective of the search.
The nature of the crime offers the first clue, since physical evidence can be anything from massive objects to
microscopic traces. Clues may be suggested by the victim, the modus operandi of the offender or other
circumstances that can be observed during the survey. In carrying out a search, it must be kept in mind that
very minute quantities of materials or marks may contain invaluable evidence.
After this preliminary work has been done, a thorough and systematic search should be made in a definite
methodical order whether inside a building or in the open.
Accurate detailed notes should be made of the scene and of its condition by the member conducting the
search. Notes should be supported by a sketch showing the whole lay out of the scene and the exact place
where articles and so on were found. In cases of suspected homicide or serious injury, notes should be made
of the position of the injured person and of any evidence of a struggle.
Where appropriate, ample use should be made of photography before anything is moved. Recording the
scene on videotape is always recommended.

8.11. Search methods
(Refer to Chapter 5, Searches)
A search of the scene of a crime may be carried out by one of the following methods:
(i) A point to point movement following a chain of objects which are obviously evidence;
(ii) The spiral search method normally carried out by a single person involves the searcher walking in circular
fashion from the outermost boundary towards the central point of the protected area. It is advisable not
to operate this system in reverse (i.e. beginning at some central point and working towards the perimeter
of the crime scene) if there is a real danger that some vital evidence may be inadvertently destroyed while
walking to the central point to initiate the search (See Fig. 1);
(iii) The zone method in which the entire scene is divided into zones which are searched individually. This is
the most suitable method to adopt when a search is being made for a small object such as a spent bullet
or shell (See Fig. 2);
(iv) The ‘strip’ method which is the usual method for an outdoor scene, particularly for large areas. The area is
divided into strips about 1 metre wide. The searcher examines each strip separately until a thorough
search has been made of the entire area. Some type of marking such as tape is necessary (See Fig. 3);

(v) The ‘grid’ search method is an advance on the ‘strip’ method – the searchers having completed the latter
search are doubled back perpendicularly across the area being searched. The "grid" system, while being
more time consuming than the "strip" method, has the advantage of being more methodical and
thorough; searched from two different viewpoints, an area is more likely to yield evidence which might
otherwise be overlooked (See Fig. 4).

Searching Indoors
In searching premises, the walls and ceilings as well as the floors should be examined, and it is best to work
from top downwards. It may be necessary to take up floor boards, since liquids such as blood may easily run
between the boards.
Critical evidence may be found even in the most unusual places. For example, one murder case was solved
where the suspect’s fingerprints were found on a light bulb which he had replaced for the deceased before
murdering her.
If the possible exit or entry path of the criminal was via a particular entrance, all authorised personnel entering
the scene should do by some other entrance, if possible.

Careful searches should be made of all places which are likely to have been overlooked by the criminal in
cleaning up after the crime, such as under the edges of tables.

Searching Outdoors
The method of searching an outdoor scene is much the same as that used for an indoor scene, but the nature
of the site will influence the types of materials that will be collected. Because there are far fewer suitable
surfaces outdoors, finger-marks will be found less frequently than in indoor situations. This does not preclude
discovering prints on objects such as weapons, cans, bottles, newspapers or other items which may be found
in or close to the scene. A wall, for example, may contain vital trace evidence.
Special attention should be given to obstacles such as hedges, walls, barbed wire, railings, also down pipes,
window sashes and to anything that has been moved. Members should not lean against them.
Almost all the evidence that will be recovered will be on the ground, and could be overlooked easily or
trampled upon.
Failure to note and take the proper action on each piece of evidence in an outdoor scene will be as detrimental
to a case as it would in a small room.
Members searching outdoor scenes of crime should:
• Pay particular attention to fragile evidence that may be destroyed or contaminated if it is not collected
when discovered
• If any doubt exists as to the value of an item, treat it as evidence until proven otherwise
• Ensure that each item or area where latent finger-marks may be present is closely examined and properly
dealt with
• Carefully protect any impression of evidential value on surfaces conducive to making casts or moulds
• Note stains, spots and pools of liquid within the scene and treat them as evidence
• Note any particular odours emitting from the scene.
When the scene has been searched, the searching member(s) should consult with persons familiar with it to
discover articles foreign to it.

Collecting, Packing, Labelling and Forwarding Evidence
Before taking possession of any evidence full notes should be made of the position and the nature of any
materials removed from a scene as evidence. Each item should be properly packed, sealed and labelled before
proceeding to the next item; packing materials and labels should be available before commencing any search
of a scene.
In deciding which tools or weapons to submit, it is preferable to be guided by the nature of the injuries and to
send all articles which could have inflicted them, even if blood may not be seen on them.
See Chapter 7, Forensic Investigation and Identification for details of procedures to be followed in collecting,
packing and transporting various kinds of evidence. See also Chapter 16, section 16.9 for further information
on packaging and labelling.
In the handling of physical evidence, it is important that it should reach the expert in as near as possible the
same condition in which it was discovered.
Material which may be evidence should be packed so as not to move about in the container while in transit.

Each item of physical evidence uplifted in the course of an investigation must be treated as a potential exhibit
in court and treated as such. Accordingly, the item must be identified and details recorded as to where and

8.12. Circulation of Information
In all cases no information should be released which is unique to the crime under investigation. Such a release
can seriously compromise the future investigation. Notwithstanding this, the following are the details which
should be circulated in the normal course of events:
• Type of crime and particulars of person/property attacked with name of injured party; in sexual cases,
however, the name of injured party should not be circulated
• Day, date, time and place of commission
• Description of property stolen (if applicable) distinguishing between identifiable and non identifiable
• Modus operandi employed - How was the attack carried out? What method was employed to gain access
to the property? Full particulars should be given in each case, and also of any peculiarity or characteristic
of the criminal
• Fullest possible description of suspect(s) (with composite pictures, where appropriate, in serious cases).
Where there is reason to believe that the culprit sustained injuries in the commission of the crime,
consideration should be given to having enquiries carried out at hospitals, clinics and so on
• Full particulars of transport used
Circulation by means of PULSE, fax, e-mail, Fogra Tora or Criminal Intelligence Bulletin (in case of serious
crimes) should be kept in mind.

8.13. Modus Operandi
The expression ‘modus operandi’ means plan of working or mode of operation. The obtaining of all possible
details of the modus operandi employed is an important part of criminal investigation. The primary use of the
modus operandi method is to identify possible suspects.
The modus operandi method of investigation is based on the study of the various patterns of crime and the
methods adopted by different criminals in committing different crimes. Among criminals there is generally a
specialisation in the type of crimes committed; the type of person or premises attacked; the property stolen;
and the method of entry.
In many cases, criminals are likely to follow the same method of committing crime. Thus, where a person has
started their criminal career as a burglar entering houses by forcing the rear door they will be likely to continue
to do so. This does not mean that a criminal would never vary their crime pattern or modus operandi.

8.14. Supervision of Criminals and Criminal Intelligence
The proper supervision of criminals is indispensable to effective criminal investigation. In cities and large towns,
members who are primarily engaged in crime investigation should make it their business to know the criminal
population in their own and adjoining areas as well as the criminals from other areas who are known to
frequent their areas.
Uniformed members in such places should know the principal and most active criminals resident in their areas,
together with those from other areas who have been convicted of committing crime in their areas. Members
with good local knowledge should liaise and meet regularly with the Divisional Criminal Intelligence Officer
and keep PULSE updated as per guidelines.
In areas, other than cities and large towns, members should know the criminal population in their own and
adjoining areas, together with travelling criminals who have been convicted of committing crime in their areas.
Avenues for the disposal of stolen property are essential to the criminal. It is therefore essential for all members
engaged on outdoor duty to be aware of handlers of stolen property in their areas. Profiles should be
completed on all serious criminals with regular briefings held by the Divisional Criminal Intelligence Officer.
A list of criminals recently released from prison is published regularly in Fogra Tora and members should keep
in touch with the particular criminals affecting their areas.

Criminal Intelligence
‘Criminal intelligence’ is information gathered, analysed and recorded with the object of providing assistance
in the investigation of crime and the prosecution of individuals and groups concerned, whether the offenders
are politically motivated or not.
There are four main categories of information, as follows:
• Information which will aid criminal prosecution
• Information which will assist in revealing organisations involved in crime
• Information which will help identify convicted and un-convicted persons and their associates, who may be
engaged in the commission of crime
• Background information and personal histories of such persons

This information can be divided into two further categories - active and passive. Active information, apart
from being stored, requires some action; passive information is merely recorded in case it may be of value in
the future. Members should take account of the provisions of the Data Protection Act in this context.

Collator System for Criminal Intelligence
The objective of the collator system for criminal intelligence is to ensure that all information in possession of
Garda members is used to the maximum extent possible in producing evidence to bring criminals, including
the planners and strategists of crime, before the courts on criminal charges.
This objective can best be achieved if such information is collected, collated, expertly analysed at appropriate
levels and recorded for easy and speedy retrieval by those who will apply it operationally.
The efficiency of the system will be maximised by the sharing and evaluation of all information gained by
individual members. It should be borne in mind that a piece of information which may appear insignificant in
isolation can, when considered with other matters, be of immense value in establishing patterns of crime and
the identities of persons involved.
PULSE now deals with recording intelligence and this is carried out by individual members. It is therefore
important that all information coming to the notice of any member, irrespective of rank or Branch, is passed
without delay to the divisional/district collator. Such information includes:
• Known or suspected criminals checked or sighted, together with any other relevant details, e.g. criminals
spending freely, better dressed than usual, new vehicles, unusual activity
• Vehicles in possession of known criminals or suspected persons giving, where possible, full details of the
vehicle and occupants
• Premises being used for meeting places by criminals including clubs, public houses and cafes
• Any unusual use of property, garages, houses, farm outbuildings etc
• Any other accurate information which may assist in crime detection or in building up background material
on criminals and suspects
The purpose of collecting accurate information is to detect and prevent crime. If success is to be achieved, no
information should be withheld from those investigating criminal offences whether committed by subversives
or otherwise. Every bit of information gleaned or activity observed, no matter how insignificant it may appear,
should be reported so that a general, overall picture may emerge.

Type and Source of Information; Precautionary Measures
All human source intelligence should be handled in accordance with Covert Human Intelligence Sources (CHIS)
guidelines. The code of practice for members involved in the management and use of CHIS has been
circulated for the guidance of all members.
It should be understood that the type of information to be supplied should cover the sighting of all
suspects/criminals whether in the subversive field or otherwise, indicating mode of conveyance and
description, whether accompanied and destination where known and so on.
Callers to the residence or haunts of suspects should be similarly dealt with.
It is appreciated that the feeding in of all information at local level might tend on occasions to reveal the
identity of an informant and thus put the informant, or others, in jeopardy. This is something which cannot be
risked. Accordingly, it is not necessary to supply the information direct to the collator in any case where the
disclosure might tend to identify a source.

8.15. Sources of Information
Crimes, when committed in ways that afford the most concealment, often provide little in the way of
tangible leads in an investigation. Generally, by the time members arrive at the scene of the crime, the
culprits will have left.
Physical evidence discovered during the investigation seldom identifies the perpetrator. Frequently it will only
place the suspect at the scene once they have been brought under suspicion.
Like physical evidence, people are a very important source of information. An informant is very often the most
direct and fastest contact with criminal elements, and the informant might provide information in a few
minutes that would otherwise require an extensive investigation to uncover.
Great assistance can be derived from information received in confidence from individuals, not alone in crime
detection but also in crime prevention.

8.16. Mobile Phone Analysis
Mobile phones are now an everyday communication tool and telephone evidence is becoming increasingly
useful in criminal investigations and prosecutions. Whether profiling the use of a phone or identifying the
location of a handset when it was used, the investigator should fully explore the evidentiary possibilities of
information stored in mobile phones and their associated network systems.
Mobile phone data can be retrieved from the following locations:
• The Subscriber Identity Module (SIM Card)
• The phone’s internal memory
• The network provider

The Subscriber Identity Module (SIM card)
The Subscriber Identity Module (SIM card) is the module that uniquely identifies the subscriber. It also holds
other user-related information such as:

• Customer identification (IMSI)
• Other identification data such as service provider
identification, card identity and language preferences
• List of telephone numbers stored by the user
• List of telephone numbers dialled by the user
• Text messages, including deleted messages that are
impossible to view on phones
• Location information from the position of last usage
• Information about countries visited by the user while in
possession of the phone

In practice the stored text messages and dialled numbers are the items of most value to the investigator. The
use of these storage areas vary with phone types. Most old phones use only the SIM card for the storage of
such items. Modern phones, however, normally use a combination of a SIM card and internal memory for the
storage of text messages and dialled numbers.

Phone Internal Memory
Most modern phones utilise on-board flash memory for storage of information items. From being relatively
small and unimportant only a few years ago, the internal memory has now become the most important source
of digital evidence in mobile phones since this is where most phones store the majority of data.
The following contents of modern mobile phones can have value as evidence:
• Phone identity
• Speed dial numbers
• Text messages and multimedia messages
• Stored location information
• Stored wordlist database
• Settings (language, date/time, tone/volume etc)
• Stored audio recordings
• Images from camera or multimedia messages
• Stored computer files
• Logged incoming calls and dialled numbers
• Stored executable programmes
• Stored calendar events
• GPRS, WAP and Internet settings and cache from such activity
It is also possible to retrieve deleted items. The storage time for deleted items may vary due to the amount of
available memory. Experience shows, however, that in most cases it is possible to recover a large number of the
items deleted by the user before the phone is seized and examined.

Network Provider Information
The network operators retain certain user information in relation to a mobile phone. Call Data Records (CDRs)
are produced every time a user makes a call or sends a text message. The CDRs are produced in the switch
where the call or message originates. CDRs are then gathered in a centralised database and used for billing
and other purposes. Each CDR contains the following:
• Originating Number (A-Number)
• Terminating number (B-Number)
• Originating and terminating mobile equipment number
• Length
• Type of service
• Initial serving base station

CDRs can be filtered on any of the above parameters. This means that one can not only obtain a list of all calls
made to and from a certain SIM, but also to and from a certain phone, regardless of which SIM was used.
The various mobile phone networks operate a cell type system to ensure national coverage. Each cell has a
mast with a number of transmitters and receivers. Mobile phones connect to base stations in different cells as
they are moved around. Details of which base station was used to connect to the network when a call was
made or received makes it possible to identify what area a phone was in when a call was made.

Care and Handling of Seized Mobile Phones
The nature and extent of the analysis of a mobile phone is invariably dependent on the seriousness of the
crime or event under examination and whether the extracted data is for evidential or intelligence purposes.
In serious cases, the Forensic Science Laboratory examination of the mobile phone (including battery and SIM
card) for sources of DNA and finger-marks will take precedence over its technical analysis.

Mobile Phones Seized for Evidential Purposes
• Note if the unit is turned on or off.
• Note the information displayed on the mobile phone screen – attention should be paid to icons such as
envelopes which indicate unread text messages. This allows examiners to be sure if any unread messages
had already been received at the time of seizure.
• Turn off the telephone.
• Do not dismantle the phone as the removal of the battery or SIM can cause important data loss.
• Seek the relevant Password/Security and PIN/PUK code from the owner. If these cannot be ascertained at
the scene then the PIN/PUK codes can be obtained by the single point of contact for mobile service
providers at Crime and Security, Garda Headquarters.
• Check for handset boxes, SIM card holders, phone bills and so on. These can hold very important
information, such as PIN numbers, PUK numbers, account details, account holder details, telephone
numbers and so on.
• Seize the phone charger. This is important in assisting the mobile phone analysis process.
• Place the telephone in a sealed Faraday evidence bag.

Mobiles seized for intelligence purposes
• Inform the mobile phone examiner who will perform the examination that the information is required for
intelligence purposes only.
• Provide as much information regarding make/type/model in advance to the mobile phone examiner
• Once seized, place the handset in a Faraday evidence bag and transport it to the mobile phone examiner
as very quickly as possibly.

Mobile Telephones - Analysis Systems
The technical analysis of mobile telephones that come into the possession of An Garda Siochana will be carried
out at Divisional level by trained personnel using officially approved equipment. Mobile Telephone Analysis
Systems have been deployed on a Divisional and Regional basis and also to specialist sections within National
Support Services.

8.17. Use of Motor Vehicles in the Commission of Crime
An important clue to the identity of a culprit is the identification of a vehicle used in the crime. Even a brief
description of a vehicle can be very helpful. Details of stolen cars recorded on PULSE should be fully utilised in
this context.
A criminal who uses their own car, or a car they have hired, in the commission of a crime and has reason to
believe that the vehicle will be identified with the crime, may abandon it and report it stolen.
Arrangements should be made for members to maintain close contact with car hire firms in their areas, so that
the possible use of hired cars in the commission of crime may be considered.

Duties of Members Operating Checkpoints
It is very important that members operating roadblocks are as fully briefed as possible on the descriptions of
suspects and/or motor vehicles. While the correct particulars of a vehicle sought may be available, it must be
remembered that the wanted persons may abandon the original vehicle and take possession of another before
reaching the roadblock.
Every vehicle which approaches a checkpoint must be stopped and checked. A record should be kept of each
vehicle passing through a roadblock as follows:
• Registered number, make and colour
• Number and sex of occupants

unable to do so
• An owner often has the boot key or a
medallion on a ring with the ignition key. A
thief usually has only a single ignition key.
Bear in mind however that criminals steal car
keys in burglaries
• Old type door handles can be forced with a
hollow bar slipped over the end and used as
a lever. The barrel lock then gives way
• Can the driver repeat the registration
number without checking anywhere?
Thieves sometimes scratch the number on
the dashboard in case they are stopped

Suspicious Pointers
• Absence of ignition key in the vehicle while the engine is running (older vehicles)
• Number plates taped to vehicle, extra screw holes in the number plate
• Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) commonly known as chassis number does not correspond with PULSE
• VIN shows signs of interference with digits erased or ground down
• Visible VIN on windscreen hidden or missing
• VIN Plate or VIN sticker removed from vehicle
• Vehicle Registration documents do not correspond to VIN
• Engine number interfered with or ground down
Helpful Indicators
• Security numbers etched on windows of vehicle (usually the last 6 digits of VIN), in some cases on the
headlights
• Air bags, gearbox, axles all have traceable serial numbers
• Original keys can help in identification by consulting with manufacturer
Assistance in the identification of stolen vehicles can be provided by the Stolen Motor Vehicle Investigation
Unit (SMVIU) at the National Bureau of Criminal Investigation (NBCI).

Examination of Vehicle Suspected of being used in Crime
A motor vehicle suspected of having been used in the commission of a crime must be preserved and examined
in the same way as the actual scene of the crime. In any serious case such as murder or serious crime the
vehicle should be photographed in situ before being moved. Ideally it should be transported thereafter to a
secure warehouse, garage or other enclosed place where it can be examined in a dry environment.
Members should avoid any unnecessary interference with such vehicles (inside or outside) pending
examination by a trained investigator.
In some cases it may be desirable to have the vehicle impounded in order to carry out a detailed examination.
This will have to be decided on by the member carrying out the examination, having consulted the member in

Notification of Incident

Preservation of Life
The first duties of members arriving at the scene traffic accidents involving serious injuries and/or fatalities are
to render assistance to injured persons and, if necessary, to procure spiritual/medical aid and arrange for the
removal of injured persons to hospital.
The first member(s) to arrive at the scene may be involved in performing life saving measures on an injured
victim. They should make mental or written notes (where practical in each situation) about the condition of the
scene as it was on arrival and after the scene has been secured. Notes should be kept on the significant times
involved in responding to a fatal traffic collision report (time dispatched to scene, time of arrival at scene).
An early check should be made to establish that all casualties have been found. This is essential at night when
a person or vehicle might have been thrown off the carriageway or roadway or where persons are trapped in
wreckage. Fire service resources may be required such as cutting equipment, lighting and so on and it may be
necessary to seek the assistance of the local authority for sanding purposes where hazardous spillages
have occurred.

Identify the Scene
Identification of the scene is a priority. All personnel who attend the collision site tend to be drawn to the
obvious location of vehicles and debris. This may only be part of the scene and therefore early consideration
must be given to redefining the scene parameters. This can be achieved by walking beyond the initial scene or
by utilising witness testimony.
There is no definite rule or set of rules that can be applied to defining the dimensions of the scene of a fatal
traffic collision. The best physical evidence is normally found at or near the actual point of collision. While it is
quite possible that the dimensions of a fatal crash scene will be large, there will usually be obvious priority
areas that should be given immediate protection. These include the point of impact and tyre skid marks on the
roadway. However, the scene should encompass the totality of the area in which evidence relating to the
collision may be found, an example of this might be where a motorcycle is found in a field with the body of
the deceased motorcyclist being found some distance away.

Secure the Scene
The purpose of securing the scene is to restrict access and prevent destruction of evidence. The first member(s)
to arrive at the scene of a fatal road traffic collision will assume responsibility for securing and preserving the
scene until otherwise instructed. The use of defined entry and exit points should be considered in order to limit
disturbance. In some cases, valuable traces may well have been obliterated by other persons who were at the
scene before the arrival of the first member; this is unavoidable, but after the first members arrive, no more
useful traces should be lost.
Having regard to the circumstances of each individual case, the following methods can be used in order to
secure a scene;
• Scenes of crime tape
• Individual Gardaí
• Garda or other emergency vehicles
• Road closures and diversions
It should not be necessary, in the absence of any overriding reason, to keep the body of the deceased at the
scene. The location should be noted and the removal of the body of the deceased arranged subject to
compliance with the Coroners Act 1962.

Examination of Scene
The following are matters to be noted at the scene:
• Time of arrival.
• The full names, addresses, ages and telephone numbers (if procurable) of vehicles’ owners, drivers,
passengers, injured/deceased persons, and witnesses.
• Weather conditions; particularly whether visibility or road surfaces were affected.
• The condition of persons involved; the nature of the injuries sustained should be noted and attention given
to the sobriety of persons involved, especially drivers. In this respect it should be borne in mind that severe
shock or concussion may produce a condition resembling that of intoxication.
• The member should note the index mark and registration number, make, and model of each motor vehicle
concerned, and if licensed as a public service vehicle, the plate number. All vehicles, including pedal
bicycles, should be examined for marks or damage. Where necessary, vehicles or their loads, or both, as the
case may be, should be measured.
• The demand for the production of insurance certificates and driving licences should, where possible, be
made at the scene. (Sec. 69, RTA. 1961 requires the demand for certificate of insurance to be made within
three months of any occasion).
• It is important to ascertain the point of impact. If the vehicle or vehicles are not in the position where the
collision actually occurred, damage caused, other evidence such as mud which has fallen off the vehicles
and witness statements will help to establish the point of impact.
• Care should be exercised in regard to the measuring and noting of track, skid and other marks, which
should be done as soon as possible, preferably before any of the vehicles are moved. Compare the thread
of tyres, size and width of wheel tracks with the tracks on the road. The position of broken glass, parts of
damaged vehicles, blood stains, oil, water, luggage and other material on the roadway or adhering to the
respective vehicles should be noted. Anything evidential should be taken possession of and retained by the
member in charge of the investigation.
• The nature and condition of the road surface as well as the main features of the locality should be noted,
particular attention being given to blind corners, bridges, road depressions, fences and other features that
may have a bearing on the causation of the collision. The width of the carriageway, height and width of
the footway and grass margin should be measured.
• All vehicles should be examined as soon as possible to ascertain their fitness for use and to discover any
defects in their equipment or manner of loading that may have contributed to the collision or be an
offence. Bicycles should receive attention with regard to fitness for use. Horse drawn vehicles should be
examined for defects in harness, chains and so on. In the case of motor vehicles, elementary matters for
attention including windscreens and side windows (these, when dirty or defective, interfere with driver's
vision), horn, mirror, tyres, (whether or not deflated) and lighting system. If a collision occurs during
lighting up hours, it should be established whether the lamp or lamps on the vehicle or vehicles involved
were in order and lighting at the time.
• Public Service Vehicles involved in collisions causing death or serious bodily harm shall be examined by a
PSV Inspector as quickly as possible after the occurrence.
• The Name, Rank and Station of the senior member calling to the scene of a traffic collision should be given
to the parties concerned, even if not requested.
• Written and signed statements should be procured as soon as possible after the collision. This will expedite
the investigation and the facts will be fresh in the minds of witnesses. This may also prevent the
subsequent making of false statements which could not otherwise be disproved. It should be noted that
the admission of driving by a person on the occasion is sufficient evidence of driving for the purpose of a
prosecution. (D.P.P. - v - Brendan Leonard, High Court, 1980).

Case Conferences
Upon the initiation of an investigation into a fatal road traffic collision and a collision where available
information indicates that there is a likelihood of a fatality, the District Officer will establish a structured
investigation team and will draw upon the expertise of both uniformed and detective personnel as required.
They should ensure that the investigating members meet formally within 48 hours after the incident and will
hold conferences thereafter as often as they consider necessary.

Investigation File
The District Officer will give every possible assistance, co-operation and advice to the onvestigating
member(s) to ensure the speedy investigation of the collision and the submission of an investigation file at
the earliest opportunity.
Where there is a surviving driver the District Officer should ensure that the file is submitted to the Law Officers
within three months of the collision for the purpose of obtaining directions.
The investigation file should contain the following:
• Statement of investigating member
• Statement of doctor pronouncing death at scene
• Statement of Ambulance Crew
• Statement of Fire Brigade Crew, where involved
• Statement from all civilian witnesses whose names appear on file or explanation as to why there is no
statement from a named witness
• Statement from next of kin
• Identification of body of deceased to member at scene, mortuary or elsewhere
• Statement of member who identified the body of deceased to Pathologist
• Statement of Doctor pronouncing death in hospital
• Copy of Post Mortem Report
• Copy of Toxicology Report
• Forensic collision report
• PSV Report
• C.71
• C.T. 68
• Particulars of Driving Licence including Expiry Date

The primary legislative provisions for investigative purposes are contained within the Misuse of Drugs Acts

enterprises. Members should report relevant intelligence and new modus operandi and trends through the
National Criminal Intelligence Unit for onward transmission to the Garda National Drugs Unit.

the terms of post-conviction confiscation allowed under Section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act 1994.

The Criminal Assets Bureau (CAB) takes a multi-agency approach in its investigations where it identifies the
assets, wherever situated, of persons which derive or are suspected to derive directly or indirectly from criminal

Customs Officials and Officials from the Department of Social and Family Affairs. The Bureau also has
professional expertise in legal, accountancy and IT matters. This expertise can be made available to any
member of An Garda Siochana during the course of their investigations into serious crime.
The Criminal Assets Bureau has a number of trained Divisional Assets Profilers attached to each division. These
Profilers can assist members conducting criminal investigations especially in relation to helping them to identify

8.18.4. Garda National Immigration Bureau (GNIB)
Since the mid-1990s, Ireland has experienced an unprecedented growth in immigration. In 1992 there were 32
asylum applications, in 2000 this increased to 10,938. Between 1998 and 2001, there was a 400% increase in
the number of non-nationals legally registering in the country.
As a response, the Commissioner established the Garda National Immigration Bureau in May, 2000 with
responsibility for all matters relating to immigration on a national basis. The divisional headquarters for GNIB is
located at 13/14 Burgh Quay, Dublin 2.
Functions of GNIB are;
• Control and prevention – control of non-nationals entering the state and prevention of illegal immigration
• Regulation – control of non-nationals within the State
• The removal of non-nationals in respect of whom the Minister has issued deportation orders
• Provide guidance and assistance for members of An Garda Siochana in relation to immigration matters
• Investigate crimes related to immigration including trafficking and smuggling human beings, illegal
employment and illegal working, use of forged, altered and counterfeit travel documents
• Provide expertise and information relating to non-nationals in major investigations, such as murder, where
the victim, witnesses or suspects are believed to be non-national
• Enforce immigration-related legislation
• Carry out enquiries on behalf of the Department of Justice, Equality and Law Reform in relation to nonnationals
• Provide a quality registration service to non-nationals living in the State.

8.19. Responsibilities of Members in Charge of Investigation
The member in charge of the investigation is responsible for ensuring that:
• All aspects of the investigation are thoroughly and fully carried out
• Witnesses' statements are perused and correlated
• Suspects' statements and alibis are fully checked out and enquired into
• The clerical aspects of an investigation such as typing copies of statements, reports and so on receive
adequate attention
• Each item of evidence is evaluated in relation to all other evidence, individually and collectively
• When a case remains undetected it is regularly reviewed and not overlooked

• When persons are arrested for similar type crimes, the possibility of their participation in an undetected
crime is considered and acted upon
• House to house enquiries and indexing results are prioritised
• PULSE regulations are complied with regarding the circulation of information and the recording of offences

8.20. Additional Procedures for Investigation of Major Crimes
Setting up Incident Room
The officer in charge of the investigation should be provided with an office for their use and that of their
assistants. All reports, telephone messages and callers should be directed to this office.
A room fitted with a telephone should be
selected. Arrangements for the installation
of an additional telephone or for the
installation of other telephone lines, if
required, should be made without delay.
This is essential to ensure speedy incoming
and outgoing communication. The
telephone numbers should be noted by
every member concerned in the
investigation and circulated to all stations.

Staffing Incident Room
The officer in charge of the investigation (Senior Investigating Officer or SIO) is responsible for the general
control and direction of the Incident Room. The SIO should immediately place a competent member in charge
of the running of the office, keeping of records, handling of correspondence, preparation of reports,
information and other circulations, processing of statements and messages, filing, and so on, as well as the
keeping of a record of duty assignments made throughout the investigation.
Office personnel should consist of approximately ten per cent of the outdoor staff engaged in the case. The
core team in an Incident Room includes:
• Member in charge of Jobs Book
• Exhibits Officer
• Member in charge of Statements
• Member in charge of Suspects
• Member in charge of Questionnaires

8.21. Records maintained at the Investigation Office
‘Jobs Book’
A Station Diary or ‘Jobs Book’ should be kept. All enquiries to be undertaken should be entered into it under
separate job headings and recorded on the left hand page. The jobs should be numbered in sequence
commencing with Number 1. The right hand page should be used to record particulars of action taken daily

overlooked.
Maintaining an Index Book in conjunction with this record is of great assistance.

Message Pad
All incoming telephone messages should be recorded in duplicate on a special message pad. In addition, any
other information coming to hand by any other means should be recorded on it. One pad should be provided

• Details of search warrants executed showing time, date and place

Exhibits
A member should be appointed as Exhibits Officer. A list should be maintained of all exhibits (including any
taken from the scene by scientific officers) and a record kept of the movement of exhibits. Exhibits should be
labelled as at Chapter 8.11 and a detailed exhibits chart should be established and maintained.

Press Book
A record should be kept of all press cuttings relating to the case. All cuttings (preferably the whole
page) should be Sellotaped in a book in date order and should include the name of the paper and the
date published.
This record should also contain details of any material given out to the press, radio or television and a record
should be maintained of all TV/radio coverage (video tapes, CDs etc) which the case attracted.

8.22. Conferences
The officer in charge of the investigation should arrange for conferences to be held as frequently as they
consider necessary. The conferences should be attended by all the investigating members considered necessary
to deal with the matters at hand. A record of attendance should be kept for each conference.
The officer in charge should conduct each conference personally and have a competent note taker present to
make brief notes on the matters dealt with, decisions arrived at, instructions issued, and so on, for subsequent
consideration and reference as required. The Divisional Officer as well as the other officers concerned in the
investigation should also attend these conferences.
The conferences offer an opportunity for the officer in charge of the investigation, other officers concerned,
and the investigators to get together and review progress and results achieved to date. They also provide a
forum for the discussion of problems requiring attention and difficulties impeding progress. In addition,
officers present can examine fresh lines of enquiry as well as pool and exchange information and put forward
ideas and suggestions.
Furthermore, investigators should be given an appreciation of what remains to be done and any deficiencies
identified in the investigation can be remedied.
Members participating in conferences should be encouraged to listen attentively and contribute unhesitatingly
any information in their possession as well as any ideas and suggestions no matter how unimportant or trifling
they might appear to be. Seemingly unimportant information, when considered in relation to other facts or
facets of the investigation, might well provide a useful clue or, indeed, the vital clue towards a detection.
Apart from this, investigators work better when kept informed on progress generally and are made aware of
the importance and interrelation of the tasks assigned them. Thus, their objectives should be made clear to
them so that they understand and appreciate the importance of their individual contributions to the success of
the investigation.
