8.23. Custody of statements
All original statements should be handed in at the investigation office to the member in charge who would
become responsible for their safe custody. The member in charge should also obtain possession and become
responsible for the safe keeping of any statements taken before the setting up of the investigation office.
Witness statements should be taken in the prescribed Form C8.

Numbering, Indexing and Filing
As statements come to hand from the investigators they should be numbered in sequence, starting with
Number 1, in black lead pencil, and then be indexed. The official issue Index Book is suitable for this purpose.
Statements should be indexed in alphabetical order and with entries containing sufficient particulars to enable
any statement to be picked out readily. The particulars required are:
• Name and address of person who made statement
• Serial number of statement
When two or more statements are made by the same person, they should bear the same serial number and
appear consecutively, e.g. 2, 2 (a), 2 (b), 2 (c), in chronological order downwards on the file of statements.

Checking
Statements should be carefully checked in the
investigation office and notes made of any
matters requiring further attention. If any
further enquiries are needed on a particular
statement, it should be referred for the
immediate attention of the members who took
it with appropriate instructions. The statement
should be annotated on an attached slip of
paper that further enquiries are being made
into other matters.
The statement should not be allowed to leave the office once it has been handed in. Any subsequent
statement taken from another person as a result of the further enquiries necessary on the original statement
should be dealt with as an original statement. Amendments to a statement should be made by way of an
additional statement.
Sufficient copies of statements should be made to provide for eight files and at least three further copies for
proof reading by the investigators. Copies of statements should be checked carefully word for word against
their originals to ensure that they are true copies and free of any discrepancy. Any corrections considered
necessary should be made immediately to ensure they are not overlooked and that true copies of all
statements reach the law officers and prosecutors.

Precis
The purpose of a precis is to provide a valuable and speedy reference for the investigation office staff and
investigators and obviate the necessity to go through statements fully when seeking only basic details.
A precis of each statement should be made as it comes to hand in the investigation office. It should be brief,
indicate the essential facts contained in the statement and be prepared in the following form:
No.

By Whom Made

By Whom Taken

Date of
Precis of Statement

The result of all enquiries made regarding the suspect and all statements pertaining to them should be

8.25. House to House Questionnaire
The aim of a house to house questionnaire is to obtain and record all possible information on the movements
of every motor vehicle and person within a specified area around the scene of the crime or other relevant area
for a defined period before and after a crime was committed, i.e. covering the period when the culprit was
making their approach to and departure from the scene.
It is desirable to establish and put on record the exact position prevailing during the critical period while the
events are fresh in the minds of persons in a position to give information.

Introduction of Questionnaires
Questionnaires should be used in the following circumstances: where the indications in the investigation of a
serious crime are that enquiries are likely to be prolonged and difficult; are likely to necessitate house to house
enquires over a large area; and no particular person is identified as a suspect.
Having decided to introduce questionnaires the aim should be to have them completed as soon as possible.

When to Introduce a Questionnaire
The exact stage at which the questionnaire should be introduced is a matter for the officer in charge of the
investigation to decide.
Consideration should be given to issues such as:
• The date and approximate time of crime
• The location of the scene
• The known facts and circumstances of, and motive for, the crime
• The information, if any, available as to the identity of the culprit(s)
• The necessity to record helpful information while it is fresh in the memories of persons possessing it

Chapter 8 The Investigation of Crime in General

Even in an investigation in which the identity of the culprit is known, the use of the questionnaire could help
to establish their presence at or near the scene at the critical time.
The questionnaire will also help to trace strangers present in the area at that time with a view to establishing
their identity and bona fides as well as their satisfactory elimination in anticipation of a possible suggestion by
the defence that the crime was committed by a stranger.

Preparation and Drafting
In connection with preparing and drafting a questionnaire, the following matters should be considered:
• The area to be covered
• The persons to be included for questioning
• The questions to be asked
• The number of members required
• The appointment of a member in charge of questionnaire(s)

Area to be Covered
The extent of the area to be covered depends on whether it is a rural or urban area and on the density of the
population. Generally it would include the locality of the scene of the crime and the victim's residence, but
such factors as the victim's place of employment, regular haunts and last place known to visit, as well as where
their associates reside, might enlarge the area.

Persons to be Included
All persons in the area at the time should be spoken to. Members cannot afford to be selective in relation to
whom the questionnaires refer to; this can result in vital information being lost.
Questionnaires should not be confined to residents of the area in which the crime was committed. One should
be completed in respect of every person known to have been in the area during the critical period, irrespective
of where they reside.
Generally a questionnaire form should be completed in respect of every person known to be in the area of the
crime over the critical period. However, a number of factors such as the density of population, information
gleaned at the scene and vicinity, the time the crime was committed, and the known circumstances of the
crime might tend to limit the number of persons to be included.

Preparing Questions
A good deal of thought should be given to compiling the questions to be included in a questionnaire. They
should be designed to fit the particular circumstances and to elicit the information desired.
The aim should be to elicit the maximum information through the minimum of clearly put pertinent questions.
The questions should differ from case to case according to the known facts and circumstances of the crime.
The inclusion of too many or badly drawn up questions might lead to confusion in completing the
questionnaire forms and delay the completion of the enquiry.

Publicity
Investigating members should enlist the aid of the Garda Press Office in preparing a media release.

Completion of Forms
A team should be selected to organise the completion of the questionnaire forms. The members of this team

43

Page 44

Chapter 8 The Investigation of Crime in General

should have the advantage of good local knowledge and practical police investigation experience and should
be suitably instructed on their duties.
The team members should explain to each person the purpose of the form, answer any questions or give any
explanation concerning it which might be required. They should also impress on such persons the importance
of accurately answering the questions on the form.
Members should not leave forms in houses for completion by residents. They should always retain the form
and ask the questions of each individual.

Review of Progress
Progress made should be reviewed daily and if it is apparent that the area to be covered is unlikely to be
completed within the scheduled time, additional members should be employed rather than trying to rush
the members already engaged on the work. This can result in forms being completed in a hasty or
haphazard manner.
Similarly, the information being obtained in response to the questions listed should be kept under review, and
the questionnaire amended where considered necessary, or a supplementary questionnaire prepared and used.

Checking, Verification and Filing
An Inspector or Sergeant (independent of the administrative staff) should be detailed to take charge of the
questionnaire team and undertake the administration of house to house questionnaires. They will also be
responsible for the checking, verification and filing of questionnaire forms.
Completed forms should promptly be handed to the member in charge of the questionnaire team by the
collectors. They should then be filed by streets or townlands with the forms for each family being filed
together. They should be checked against the Voters Lists to ensure that no one has been missed out. It is vital
that the members undertaking this important duty be properly briefed beforehand.
Each form should be scrutinised and compared with all other related questionnaires, and any omissions,
inaccuracies, discrepancies and so on noted. This information should be passed on to the members detailed to
verify the questionnaires who should pursue each matter arising to a satisfactory conclusion.
In the event of any person in respect of whom a form has been completed becoming suspect, the form should
be passed to one of the investigation members for attention. A duplicate form should be made and inserted in
the file of completed questionnaire forms on which the name of the verifying member should be noted. The
fact that the original was transferred to the Suspects Statements File and date of transfer should also be noted.
A map should be maintained to show which streets and townlands have been checked and the names of the
members making the enquiries in each street and townland. All documents generated during a criminal
investigation are subject to disclosure to the defence. Therefore it is important that all leads from
questionnaires and so on are fully checked out and enquiries exhausted.
A specimen House-to-House Questionnaire Form is shown at the end of this chapter.

Questionnaire for Licensed Premises and similar public buildings
The purpose of this questionnaire is to establish the identity and other details of persons who, during the
critical period, were present in hotels, public houses, clubs, or at meetings, card games, or in fact at any place
where a number of people assembled.
It should be completed on the first day of the investigation so as to get on record immediately all persons
visiting such places with times of their arrival and departure and particulars of companions, attire, transport
and so on, in so far as can be ascertained. It is vitally important that this information be obtained at the earliest
opportunity and that it be put on permanent record from the outset of the investigation.
A specimen questionnaire form for this purpose is shown at the end of this chapter.

8.26 Final report for the Law Officers
When completing the file for the Law Officers, the relevant statements should be extracted from the day to
day files of statements and arranged in order of the sequence of events. The statements, so arranged, should
be re numbered in sequence, from front to back, commencing at Number 1. In order to complete the file the
following items should be attached:
• Covering report of the officer in charge of the investigation
• Alphabetical index to the statements
• Numerical index to statements
• Copies of relevant documentation – e.g. copies of search warrants, copies of custody records, copies of
paper exhibits and exhibits chart
The indices facilitate a more speedy reference and a more efficient perusal of the contents of the file. The file
may also include:
• Copies of videos showing the scene or suspects
• CCTV footage detailing the suspect’s custody in the Garda station
It should also be noted that phone numbers and home addresses of witnesses and injured parties should not
be submitted to the defence.
Full disclosure of documents and all evidence gathered during the course of the investigation is now the norm.
In this regard it is important that a full copy of all evidence in the possession of the Garda investigation team,
whether favourable or otherwise to the accused, must be disclosed to the Law Officers.
It is a matter for the Gardaí to instruct the Law Officers on any claim of privilege required to be made on any
aspect of the materials assembled during the investigation.

Chapter 9 The Investigation of Murder and Suspicious Death

9.1. Introduction
The investigation of murder is the most important and demanding task which any police force has to
undertake. While the identification and conviction of the murderer is frequently a very difficult task, it will
occasionally be even more difficult to establish the fact that murder has been committed.
The crime of murder can not be treated in isolation from other classes of unnatural death because it is
only by being able to discount death from natural cause, accident or suicide that the crime of murder can
be established.
In many cases, a murder will be obvious from even a cursory examination of the body and scene. However,
there are other instances such as death by poisoning and some blunt force injury deaths, when even after
detailed investigation, the actual manner of death (whether as a result of accident, suicide or murder) may
not be positively established.
Determining the cause and manner of death in these circumstances calls for intelligent and painstaking
investigation. A careful study of the circumstances of death, examination of the body, clothing and scene,
a check on the deceased's background, lifestyle, character and general disposition, and their movements
and actions immediately before prior to death, are all essential.
The objectives of this chapter are:
• To outline the general investigative procedures which should be implemented on receipt of a report of
suspected murder or other unnatural death occurring under suspicious circumstances as well as that of
serious wounding
• To enable all members who may be called upon to investigate an unnatural death to appreciate the
difficulties associated with the more common forms of such deaths
• To create a greater awareness of the need for care and caution when conducting the preliminary
examination and of the potential usefulness of scientific and trace evidence
• To create an awareness of the significance which should be attached to certain conditions and
circumstances encountered
• To make each member more aware of the need for an efficient and adequate investigation of all such
deaths, thereby reducing the risk of murder going undetected, and increasing the chances of securing
the identification and conviction of even the craftiest killer

9.2. Conduct of the Preliminary Investigation
9.2.1. Taking a Report of a Suspicious Death
When a member receives a report of murder, unnatural death, or serious injury to a person which appears to
have occurred in suspicious circumstances, they should make a careful record of the time of the report, the
name and address of the informant, the content of the report, and a note of the circumstances under which
the reporting person secured knowledge of the incident. In addition, if the report is received by telephone the
member taking the call should always ask the reporting person for their telephone number or note the
telephone number displayed on a caller ID device.
The general demeanour, sobriety and state of dress, including marks or traces on the body, clothing, or
footwear of the reporting person should be recorded. If possible, the person making the report should, as the
particular circumstances dictate, be either accompanied back to the alleged scene or be asked to remain at the
station until the report has been investigated.

Occasionally the person who has committed a murder will report the death themselves; in fact they may simply
say "I'm after killing AB." The investigation of such a report is comparatively straightforward once a ‘false
confession’ has been ruled out.

It is vitally important to establish the identity of all persons who entered the
scene or were observed in the vicinity of the scene prior to the arrival of the
first member.
Careful adherence to the procedure outlined above may provide the first essential clue to the part, if any,
played by the informant in the death of the victim.

9.2.2. Action on Receipt of Report
On receipt of the report, all reasonable measures should be taken to arrive at the scene before it has been
disturbed. Promptness of action should not lead to carelessness and members approaching the scene should
be on the alert for persons and vehicles acting in a suspicious manner.
In rural areas, it should be possible to record particulars of any vehicles leaving the general scene area. On
arrival, members should act cautiously and their exact time of arrival at the scene should be recorded.
The instructions set out at Chapter 8.3/8.5 regarding the action to be taken by the first members to arrive at
the scene should be carefully complied with.

9.2.3. Designation of Scene
Once the scene has been located and identified it should be designated formally as a crime scene by the Senior
Investigating Officer (SIO). In this regard, the SIO should consider utilising the powers conferred by Section 5
of the Criminal Justice Act, 2006 if they believe it necessary. See further detail at Appendix II Section 2.

9.2.4. Entering the Scene
If possible, all authorised persons entering the scheme should wear protective clothing approved for the
purpose by the Forensic Science Laboratory. The first members entering the scene area should make a mental
note of the conditions observed; these observations should be recorded at the earliest opportunity. The
utmost care should be taken not to contaminate or destroy any trace evidence.
The first observations should be concentrated on the following conditions, if possible without causing any
interference to the scene:
• Position of the body or injured person
• Position of the head, hands and legs
• Whether the hands hold or contain any object or substance
• State of dress of the body and condition of clothing
• Blood stains - location, size and condition, i.e. whether old or fresh
• Evidence on the ground - location of footmarks, weapons, cartridge cases, etc
• Odours - gas, gun powder, alcohol, perfume, cigar, cigarette smoke, etc
• Doors - open, closed, locked or unlocked
• Lights - on/off
• Windows - open, closed, forced, broken, etc; curtains drawn or open; blinds up or down
• Weather conditions

9.2.5. Removing Victim from Source of Danger
If the victim is still alive, they should be removed from any source of danger. Ligatures should be cut if
possible in order to preserve the knot structure and untied only if necessary, gas supplies turned
off and so on. The removal of a victim from the source of danger is a higher priority than the location of
the source.

9.2.6. Establishing the Victim's Identity
If the victim is able to speak, they will normally reveal their identity and the circumstances of their injuries. If
after all necessary attention has been given to the injured person and they are still unable to speak, a cursory
search of their clothing and personal effects should be made with a view to establishing their identity.
However, if it has been definitely established that the victim is dead and it is suspected that the death may
have been the result of suspicious circumstances, the body should not be interfered with in any way until
the scene is examined by those persons properly trained in this regard.

9.2.7. Removal of Injured Victim to Hospital
When the victim is being removed to hospital, the member in charge at the scene should direct the
movements of the persons moving the victim so as to reduce the risk of destroying or damaging evidence.
If furniture has to be moved, windows opened
or the scene interfered with in some other way,
by depositing fresh footmarks for example, then
a note should be taken of these matters and
this should later be brought to the notice of the
crime scene examiners.
If the scene has been disturbed before the
arrival of the Gardaí, the original conditions
should be ascertained and recorded,
(photographed or video recorded if possible).
A member should always accompany the injured person to hospital, while another member preserves the
scene. The member accompanying the victim should seat themselves close to the injured person and note,
word for word, any comments made.
A seriously wounded person, regaining consciousness even for a short time, could provide valuable
information and in some circumstances such statements might qualify for admission in evidence as a
dying declaration.
The second important reason for accompanying the victim to hospital is to obtain custody of their clothing,
footwear and other effects, thereby ensuring that they are carefully preserved for scientific examination.
In the case of suspected poisoning the member should also ensure the collection and preservation of stomach
washings, blood, urine and other likely evidence of the poison, which may be discovered at the hospital.

9.2.8. Dying Declaration
If the injured person is able to converse without seriously aggravating their condition, the essential facts should
first be obtained. In certain circumstances a dying declaration in the manner set out below should be obtained.
Occasionally, a dying person may simply whisper some remark such as "I'm dying, John Smith killed me", or
"My brother Frank poisoned me". Such statements should be recorded as soon as possible; as should any
circumstances which might permit the statement to qualify for admission in evidence.

Chapter 9 The Investigation of Murder and Suspicious Death

A member taking a dying declaration should, in the presence of witnesses if possible, question the victim to
ascertain if they are fully aware that they are dying and if they have given up all hope of recovery.
Should the replies be in the affirmative, they should be asked to relate the circumstances under which they
sustained their injuries. The questions and replies should be recorded verbatim. Leading questions are not
permissible but questions put to assist the victim in giving an accurate and unambiguous account of the facts
are allowable.
The declaration should be signed by the member taking it and by all those present, but it is not essential that
the declarant (victim) sign.
When the question and answer interview has been recorded and if the victim's condition permits, a statement
in narrative form, with the usual introductory heading, should be taken. The statement proper should
commence with the words: "I, A.B. having the fear of death before me and having no hope of recovery make
the following statement...." When the statement has been read over to the declarant and any necessary
amendments made, it should be signed by the member taking it and by all those present.
It is desirable that the member who took the declaration should view the body at the post mortem so as to
identify positively the body being examined as being the same person who had made the dying declaration.

9.2.9. Dead Body Found on Arrival
Rigor mortis and putrefaction are both specific signs of death and when present the body should not be
moved or touched until a thorough investigation can be made. When the unmistakable signs of death are
present, the premises should be secured or if the body is found outdoors, the area cordoned off.
Only the doctor and clergyman should be allowed enter the scene pending the arrival of the State Pathologist
and crime scene investigators. These persons should be escorted into and out from the scene along a
predetermined route so as to minimise any interference with potential evidence.
In this regard, the Senior Investigating Officer or the member in charge at the scene should identify the most
likely access and exit routes used by the assailant and ensure, if at all possible, that an alternative route is used
by all authorised personnel entering the scene.
The doctor pronouncing the fact of death should be asked to confine themselves to that alone and to refrain
from disturbing the body. Only in the circumstance where neither the State Pathologist nor their deputy is able
to visit the scene should the attending doctor be asked to note the position of the body and the condition of
the clothing. They should also record the extent of rigor mortis and the air and rectal temperatures. Before
inserting the thermometer, they should take anal swabs (high and low on plain swabs).

9.3. Establishing the Cause and Manner of Death
The course of the investigation will be influenced by the mode of death of the victim. Different modes of
death are considered below under separate headings.

9.3.1. Mechanical Asphyxia
The most common causes of death from mechanical asphyxia are those brought about by hanging, strangling
and smothering. Drowning is not a truly asphyxial death but it may be considered with this group.

9.3.2. Death by Drowning
Drowning is the result of inhalation of water following immersion or partial immersion in it. A drunken person
may, if they fall face down in a puddle, drown. A common circumstance requiring Garda investigation is that
of a body found floating in water or retrieved from a river, lake or seabed. However, immersion in domestic
baths or industrial vats is also not uncommon.
The drowning person inhales water which is then absorbed into the blood stream. Death may be due to a
combination of air deprivation and a disturbance of the constituents of the blood due to absorption of the
inhaled fluid. This disturbance is greater in freshwater drowning, causing death more rapidly than in sea
water drowning.
In a small minority of drowning cases, death may result from stoppage of the heart due to the shock of the
inhalation of water. If the deceased suffered from underlying heart disease, or panicked as a result of the
immersion, they would be more likely to die by this means.
A further rare mechanism in drowning is spasm of the air way in the region of the larynx or voice box. The
obstruction of the air way thus produced, in the absence of inhalation of water, gives rise to a true asphyxial
event. Asphyxial signs are not usually found in drowning. If present, evidence of strangulation or hanging
prior to immersion needs to be excluded.
Signs of drowning are most easily seen in a body which has not yet begun to putrefy. Most prominent is the
presence of a mushroom shaped accumulation of thick foam around the mouth. If this is wiped away, more
exudes from the mouth and nostrils. The origin of this foam is the mixture of the mucus lining the air ways
and the pulmonary surfactant or detergent which, when churned up with the inhaled water by the efforts to
breathe, accumulates in the air ways. This foam does not usually persist for more than twenty four hours
except in cold winter weather.
The hands of a drowned person may show scratching and accumulation of mud and sand beneath the finger
nails. Aquatic growth may be grasped in the hands in cadaver spasm.
When the body floats head downwards, the post mortem staining may be confined to the head, neck and
front of the chest. This staining is pink rather than the usual purple, especially when the water is cold. Bodies
cool rapidly in water in temperatures found in the Irish climate. Thus, cooling may well be complete within
twelve hours of submersion, or even earlier.
Various other external appearances on a body found in water are due to immersion rather than drowning. In
particular these are goose pimples and the wash-leather appearance of the soles of the feet and the palms of
the hands. They are not evidence of drowning.
After a protracted period of time in water, the skin becomes very loose and extreme care of the hands must be
taken when recovering and transporting the body so that the fingerprints are not lost. Ideally, and where
practicable, the body should be left undisturbed until it has been examined by the Pathologist.
The investigating member should take control samples of water.
When a body is discovered in water, or other liquid, answers to the following questions must be sought:
• What was the cause of death?
• If death was by drowning, was it accidental, suicide or murder?
• If not by drowning, what was the actual cause and circumstance of death, and how did the body come to
be in the water?

The Pathologist at the post mortem in a straightforward case of drowning, that is one where witnesses
observed the event and it was accidental, may be able to reach a conclusion as to the cause of death by a full
post mortem examination, but may need special tests.
The classic findings in drowning are simplified as follows:
• Foam in the principal air ways
• Excessive expansion of the lungs, so that they almost overlap in the front of the chest, and a spongy feel
due to air trapped in them
• Haemorrhages in the mastoid air cells of the skull
• Sand, mud, plants, or weeds in the air ways and in the stomach
The only acceptable definitive test of drowning, and even it is not absolutely certain, is the diatom test. This
test should be carried out by the Pathologist on any body removed from water in suspicious circumstances.
Diatoms are water plants with acid resistant glass shells. The test involves the breaking down of lung, bone
marrow and possibly other tissues with acid and the microscopic search for diatoms in the residue left behind.
The minimum desirable samples for the diatom test are one complete thigh bone or femur and one
complete lung.
Reliance on the diatom test must not lull the Pathologist into not carrying out a full and thorough
investigation, including histology and toxicological analysis. In the case of suicide, the person may well have
taken an overdose of a drug or poison before entering the water.

9.3.3 Death by Hanging
The main circumstances of hanging are:
• The ‘Long Drop’ (as formerly used in judicial executions)
• Suicide
• Accidental
• Auto erotic or Sexual Asphyxia
• Homicidal
• Suspension of a dead body to conceal homicide

Mechanism of Hanging
Fracture dislocation of cervical spine
This may only be achieved by the ‘long drop’, in which the victim must fall a distance of between one and
three metres depending on their weight, and be brought to a standstill by the noose and rope before the feet
reach the ground.
It is unusual for suicides to adopt this method.

Chapter 9 The Investigation of Murder and Suspicious Death

Decapitation is possible if the drop is too long. Unconsciousness is instantaneous by this method, though the
pulse may continue for up to fifteen minutes or more. Even though the damage in these cases is irreversible
and attempts at resuscitation might seem futile, it is better that resuscitation continue until a medical decision
is made to stop.

Death by constriction of the neck
The mechanism here is a combination of obstruction of the air way and the tourniquet like obstruction of the
arterial supply to the head and brain. If the air way is obstructed while the arterial circulation continues, blue
purple congestion of the head, with petechia haemorrhages on the face and whites of the eyes will be
very marked.
On the other hand, if the noose is tighter and the pull greater, arterial obstruction is achieved and the head
may be ‘white’. Though unconsciousness is rapid (within seconds), death may not ensue for some minutes,
and attempts at resuscitation may be worthwhile.

The Circumstances of Hanging
The noose is usually tied to the neck by means of a running or slip knot suspended at the back of the head,
though behind either ear is also common. Occasionally, suspension at the front beneath the jaw is seen. The
upper end of the noose may be tied directly to the anchorage point from which the body is suspended, or it
may be looped over it and brought down to a fixed point within reach of the deceased person
Investigators must be on their guard with this latter arrangement, as it may have been used to hoist an already
dead body off the ground. In sexual asphyxias, some counter balance arrangement will usually be rigged up so
that the tension of the noose may ease as the person lapses into unconsciousness.
When a body is hanging free, and there is
no stepping off point in the vicinity such as
a chair, table, step, stone and so on, then
there is every reason to suspect murder. In
such circumstances, careful examination
should be made to determine whether it
was possible for anyone to climb up to the
point of attachment of the noose.
When a tree limb has been used, it should
be comparatively easy to detect evidence
of climbing such as broken twigs, leaves,
moss or bark torn away, and similar traces
on the hands, clothing and footwear of
the deceased. It sometimes occurs that an easily removable stepping off point such as a chair has been
accidentally removed by the persons who discovered the body.

External appearances in hanging
The noose or ligature mark on the neck may show an inverted Y shaped area, corresponding to the suspension
point; but more often than not, the suspension point is indicated only by a gap in the noose mark with
convergence towards that point of the mark each side of the gap.
Elsewhere around the neck there may be a deep single groove slightly narrower than the width of the noose.
The groove is deepest at the point opposite suspension, and therefore usually on the front of the neck.
The imprint of the suspending cord may be visible on the skin, which is usually brown and parchment like in
the groove. Marks such as bruises or scratches on either side of the noose are unusual, and, if present,
suggest previous homicidal assault. However, they may have been made by the victim trying to free themselves
or by rescuers in an attempt to loosen the noose.

At the suspension point, the noose mark may either be widened due to contact with the knot, or interrupted
because the weight of the body pulled the skin away from the area.
Other signs of hanging include:
• Protrusion of the tongue, and possible dentures with it
• Dense purple congestion of the face above the noose mark may occur but in other cases the face is pale
due to reflex stoppage of the heart
• Asphyxial haemorrhage may occur though this is the exception rather than the rule
• Distribution of post mortem lividity or staining on the lower part of the body in the dependent areas.
This distribution may be from the waist down in a vertical suspension or it may be modified if the
deceased was found in a semi prone supine or sitting position
• There may be erection of the penis, ejaculation of semen, signs of urination upon clothing, or defecation.

Sexual asphyxia
Sexual asphyxia is a specific form of hanging, confined exclusively to males usually between the ages of
puberty and middle age, though most frequently seen in the teenage to thirty year old group. These victims
are sometimes transvestites, clad in female clothing, who leave erotic literature around the scene of the
hanging and may even set up mirrors or cameras to observe the effect.
There may be some soft piece of cloth between the noose and the neck to avoid discomfort. A plastic bag
may, in addition, be draped over the head. An elaborate mechanism in the noose may be set up so that
relaxation of the pressure on the neck can occur as the victim lapses into unconsciousness.
Such people, when found deceased, may have successfully practised this technique on numerous previous
occasions and it may be obvious that they had no desire to kill themselves. The explanation of the practice
appears to be that a heightened sexual experience is derived from partial asphyxia. Wiring of the genitalia to
an electric apparatus, and various bindings upon the limbs and body may also be found.

Care must be taken in the removal of the noose or ligature in all cases of fatal
hangings, whether they are suicides, homicides or otherwise. It must not be
removed by opening any knots but by cutting the rope at a location where it
is least likely to interfere with any evidence. The actual knot may be significant
as evidence at a later date.
9.3.4. Death by Strangling
Strangulation can be brought about either manually, using one or both hands, or by using a ligature or by
a combination of both. Manual strangulation is invariably murder but suicidal strangulation by ligature
is possible.
In cases of manual strangulation, crescentic marks made by the fingers and fingernails on the neck of the
deceased may occasionally be clearly visible after death.
The variety of neck marks in manual strangulation is such that it is inadvisable to draw any conclusions as to
the nature of the assailant's grip. Oval bruises on the skin may correspond to fingertips, while thumb pressure

is usually greater and more localised than pressure from the other fingers. The picture of skin markings may
be confused by the victim's own finger nails scratching the skin in an effort to free themselves from the
assailant’s grip or the ligature.
The most important distinguishing features of strangulation by ligature from hanging are the associated
injuries on the neck and elsewhere, indicative of struggle and the presence of a suspension point. In suicidal
hangings, associated injuries are virtually non existent.
If a ligature is wound several times around the neck and doubly knotted at each turn, homicide is more likely.
Multiple turns and more than one knot are rarely achieved by an intending suicide.
While death by strangulation is usually murder, it should be remembered that self strangulation could be
accomplished with a ligature or tourniquet type device. A person cannot strangle themselves with their own
hands, since at the instant of unconsciousness the hands will relax and breathing will resume. As in the case
of hanging, an important determination in identifying an accidental strangulation is establishing what the
victim was doing when the accident occurred.
The ligature mark is usually best sampled for hair and fibres by ‘Sellotape lifts’. This involves the application of
strips of transparent Sellotape to the groove with the adhesive side next to the skin, peeling the tape off and
mounting the strips on plastic cards for microscopic examination.
In the general examination of the clothing and the body of the strangled victim, particular attention must be
paid to evidence of sexual assault.
Homicidal strangulation is usually preceded by a struggle which often results in injury to the body and clothing
of the deceased and attacker as well as general disturbance of the scene. Personal injuries usually consist of
scratches on the face, neck and hands, as well as bruising on areas of the body.
Damage to clothing may consist of tears in the fabric and dislocation of attachments, such as buttons.
Frequently, strands of hair, fabrics and fibres are discovered in the hands of persons murdered in this manner.
Similarly, nail scrapings from the hands of the deceased and of the culprit (when located at an early stage) are
important as they could possibly contain trace evidence.
For this reason, the hands of the deceased should be kept intact by placing them in plastic bags (with each
hand loosely secured so as not to mark the body) with the object of having them examined at the mortuary by
the Scenes of Crimes Examiner under the supervision of the State Pathologist.

9.3.5. Death by Smothering
Smothering occurs when the air passages are obstructed; it is either accidental or murder. Most accidental
deaths occur in infants under six months of age, for example, when the child turns on its face and smothers.
With older children and adults, such deaths usually occur while eating (choking) or, as in the case of drunken
or mentally disabled persons, when regurgitated food blocks the air passages.
Infanticide, the killing of the old, seriously incapacitated, and drugged persons, and the victims of rape, are the
most common forms of murder by smothering. Although the intention of the assailant in a case of rape is
often only to silence the victim, death sometimes results.
In this type of murder, asphyxial signs (facial congestion and petechial haemorrhages in the whites of the eyes
and on the lips) are usually obvious.
Death by smothering can also occur where the victim is gagged in the course of a robbery or other crime and
is asphyxiated due to swelling of the lining of the nasal cavity so that both mouth and nose are blocked.

9.3.6. Wounds
In the examination of a dead body, it should never be assumed that any particular wound was inflicted with a
particular weapon or in a particular manner. The examination and interpretation of wounds is, and always
must be, the responsibility of the Pathologist.
In the evaluation of wounds generally, considerable significance is attached to the discovery on a body of
either ‘hesitation’ or ‘defence’ injuries.
Hesitation or tentative wounds when discovered on a body are commonly interpreted as being strongly
indicative of suicide; however, they are not present in all suicidal deaths by wounding. Tentative wounds are
characteristically seen on the neck in cut throat cases where they are invariably multiple and parallel to the
main wound. In suicidal stabbing, they may also be present on the front of the chest over the heart as
multiple shallow repetitive wounds, all at the same angle. Wrist slashes may also have a tentative component.
Defence wounds are generally on the hands or arms, and are received either when warding off knife thrusts,
when they tend to be on the back of the hand or arm, or when attempting to seize the blade of an assailant's
knife, when they are on the palm and inner surfaces of the fingers.

Stab Wounds
A stab wound results when a narrow instrument, such as a knife, is thrust into the body; the resulting wound
in the skin is often small while damage to the deeper tissue and organs is extensive. The cause of death in
stabbing cases is usually profuse bleeding, most frequently within the body.
The shape and size of the wound in the skin is dependent upon several factors: shape of the weapon;
direction of penetration; movement of the weapon while in the body; and direction of withdrawal. Limb or
trunk position, skin tension, and the relationship of the axis of the wound to skin folds will affect the degree of
stretching or gaping of the wound.
As most stab wounds involve cutting, either
while being pushed in or being drawn out,
the elongation of the wound makes the task
of estimating the width of the blade of the
weapon more difficult. Penetration of bony
surfaces such as the skull, sternum or spine
presents the shape of the part of the weapon
that passed through the bone.
Occasionally, the blade of the stabbing
instrument may break off and remain in the
body, in which case the broken part and the
remainder of the weapon can be matched for
positive identification.
Stab wounds on hairy surfaces such as on the head or in the eye itself, which display very little or no outward
evidence of bleeding, may easily be overlooked unless specifically searched for at the post mortem
examination by inspection and by exploration, i.e. by feeling the scalp. Surrounding hair should be shaved for
‘wound photography’.
While not being evident from a surface examination, the weapon may be plunged in and out of the tissue
without being completely withdrawn between thrusts. This can result in multiple wound tracks beneath one
skin wound. The depth and shape of the wounds, as determined during post mortem examination, may give a
clue to the nature of the weapon used.

The track of the weapon may well be confined to fleshy areas; however, in those cases where the penetration
involves internal organs, the track may not be accurately represented because of changes in the shape and
position of the organs after death relative to their position in life.
Equally, a powerful stabbing force against a soft area like the abdomen might depress the area and thus
make the wound appear deeper than the actual length of the weapon; likewise, a blade may not penetrate
to its full length, leaving a wound track shorter than the blade. Therefore, a careful and complete post
mortem is essential in all cases.
Suicidal stab wounds are inflicted on those areas of the body which can be reached easily by the victim, the
most frequent site being over the heart. As with suicidal shootings, the victim usually opens up the clothing
to uncover the area selected to stab. Occasionally they will leave the knife sticking in the wound. Hesitation
stabs, which barely penetrate the chest may be noticeable close to the selected site. Cuts on the insides of
the wrists or thigh may also be present in a stabbing suicide, and are a good indication of the suicidal intent
of the victim.
Frequently in cases of murder there is only one stab wound which penetrates a vital organ and causes death.
Stab wounds may be inflicted on any part of the victim's body but are usually located on the back, neck and
upper chest.
Defence injuries of the hands and arms and injuries on the back and other areas not easily reached by the
deceased, are strongly indicative of murder.
Also indicative of murder are:
• Multiple wounds of different parts of the body
• Multiple wounds of uniform depth
• Wounds of non vital as well as vital areas
• Multiple stab wounds of the breasts and genitals are symptomatic of sex murder
In cases of murder, the stab wounds often penetrate the clothing (a factor usually absent in suicides) and for
this reason particular care should be exercised when removing and examining the victim's clothing. In many
instances, the clothing presents a more accurate correlation to the actual width of the weapon than does the
wound. This applies where the clothing is comprised of woven but not knitted material.
Photographs of the slits in the clothing, prior to removal from the body, may help in the reconstruction of the
event; photographs of white cards beneath the slits will highlight them if the cloth is dark or bloodstained.

Cut Wounds
A cut is an incised wound inflicted with a knife, razor, piece of glass, or other sharp edged instrument. As
with stab wounds, extensive bleeding is the usual cause of death in cutting wounds. With cut wounds the
bleeding usually occurs from the body surface. The edges of cutting wounds are clearly incised with no
evidence of bruising or markings around the skin surface. By definition, these wounds are longer than they are
deep while stab wounds are the reverse.
Fatal suicidal cutting wounds usually involve the throat, and the knife or other cutting instrument may
occasionally be found clenched in the hand of the dead person.

Gun Shot Wounds
Unlike some other forms of violent or unnatural death, firearm fatalities are often attended by important trace
evidence. The sources of this trace evidence from the weapon include the wounds, the empty cartridge cases,

the spent bullets, the shotgun wadding and the powder residues on the clothing and body of the victim.
Injuries caused by shooting may be classified as either bullet or shotgun wounds.

Bullet Wounds and Shotgun Wounds
A bullet, if it passes through the body, produces entry and exit wounds. Examination of these wounds may
reveal information concerning the type of weapon, the kind of ammunition, the range, direction and angle of
fire, number of shots striking the body and an indication of the disabling effects of each such wound.
Because most ammunition used in shotguns delivers multiple rather than single projectiles, the injuries
produced require a different interpretation. Similarity to bullet wounds is only at near or contact ranges unless
single ball shot is used or ‘balling’ - the sticking together of pellets - occurs.

Entrance Wound
The typical bullet entrance wound is a round regular hole and minimal blood is produced. Frequently it may
be smaller than the bullet itself and may be so inconspicuous as to be hidden in clothing, in hair, in body folds
and orifices, or behind closed eye lids.
Entry wounds are normally distinguishable from exit wounds because the latter are larger and appear as
jagged irregular lacerations. Exit wound size depends on bullet velocity, bone fragments, state of bullet on exit
and so on. A single bullet can account for multiple entrance and exit wounds, while there have been cases
where more than one bullet entered via the same wound.
A narrow ring of greyish soiling from the bullet, the ‘grease collar’, and a reddish brown ‘abrasion collar’ are
usually visible around the wound. The ‘abrasion collar’ may display an oval shape in entry wounds where the
bullet has struck the skin obliquely.
It is very important to be able to estimate the distance of discharge. The distance from which bullets and
shotgun ammunition were discharged can be conveniently classified as being one of:
• Contact entry wounds
• Close range wounds
• Long range wounds

Contact Entry Wounds - Bullet
These are produced when the gun is discharged with the muzzle
in contact with the victim's body, or less than 50 mm from the
victim. The edges of the hole and bullet track are burned. In
true contact wounds over bone, especially the skull, the skin and
tissue are torn, producing ragged lacerations radiating from the
hole. Particles of un-burnt powder and other debris are blown
into the bullet track for a distance below the surface.
Contact wounds of the head may cause massive fracture of
the skull with scattering of brain and bone fragments around
the body.
A patterned bruise is occasionally produced at the wound; this
results from the recoil of the weapon and duplicates the end of
the muzzle, sights or retractor spring rod.

Contact Entry Wounds - Shot
In this case the actual contact entry wound is often destroyed by the blast where the weapon is placed against
the head or in the mouth. Over soft tissues elsewhere on the body, a neat round or oval entry wound is
produced, but blast destruction of underlying tissues is on a large scale. The wound is similar to a bullet entry
wound with an "abrasion collar" but is wider-up to 25 mm in diameter. Powder blackening and burning may
be present over a thin rim up to 6 mm. wide.
The wound bears a punched out appearance due to the cutting effect of the mass of shot pellets. Slight
raggedness of the rim of the wound may result.

Close Range Entry Wounds - Bullet
The wounds which are produced when the muzzle is held from 5 to 60 centimetres from the victim are
normally circular. They are most readily distinguishable from contact or long range wounds by the presence of
burns and powder tattooing in the skin surrounding the hole.
The recognition of powder marks and residues is an important factor in early differentiation of entrance and
exit wounds. The pattern and composition are useful in deducing the range of fire and kind of ammunition
used. Accurate range-of-fire tests can be made by laboratory test firing the same weapon and ammunition at
test targets. Types of powder residues can also be differentiated by chemical, photographic, radiographic and
spectrographic tests.

Close Range Entry Wounds - Shot
For a distance of up to a metre, the mass of pellets does not scatter and produces the punched out entry
wound as described for contact wounds up to a diameter of 3 cm. The rim of powder burning is wider - up to
a maximum of about 30 cm. Tattooing of the skin may occur up to a range of 60 cm. Cards and wads may
enter a wound at ranges of up to 180 cm or may produce a circular mark or bruise adjacent to the entry
wound. Head wounds in this category, unlike those at contact range, do not produce blast disruption of
the skull.

Long range wounds - Bullet
These wounds are produced when the weapon is
discharged with the muzzle at a greater distance
than 60 cm from the body and are, characteristically,
a round hole with a circular abrasion collar without
burns or powder tattooing.

Long range wounds - Shot
Beyond one metre, individual pellets leave the main
mass, causing small subsidiary entry wounds around
the main one. The large central wound persists in
ranges up to 4 metres. As the range increases, the
pellets spread in an ever widening circle or pattern,
the diameter of which is roughly proportional to
the range.
Given the pattern diameter, the ballistics experts at
the Technical Bureau will accurately assess the range
of fire by a set formula appropriate to the particular
barrel and type of shotgun.
X rays are invaluable for assessing pattern diameter in bodies. They are also useful for pellet counts,
determining the direction of shot travel and detection of obstruction between gun and victim.

Exit Wounds - Bullet
A bullet normally takes a straight path through a body and will exit if the speed and momentum are great
enough and no object, such as bone, is struck. Exit wounds are normally larger than the bullet or the entrance
wound. They are ragged and irregular in shape, and show more damage than the entrance wound.

Exit Wounds - Shot
Pellets, because of their small weight and low velocity relative to bullets, have poor penetrating power.
Therefore, other than at close range, or in a limb, there is usually no exit wound. In contact or close range
wounds, the ribs and spine may deflect pellets in all directions and a few may emerge in haphazard patterns.
Exit wounds in the head are unusual, unless in contact blast injuries, because the bone of the skull absorbs the
shot energy.
In the absence of a firearm at the scene, members should bear in mind that gunshot wounds, especially blast
injuries to the head, might look like injuries due to a blunt weapon. Thus, for example, removal of a gun by
well intentioned relatives to conceal suicide might give the impression of homicide.

Location and character of the wounds
An important consideration for both the Gardaí and Pathologist is whether the victim’s wounds could
conceivably have been self-inflicted when their location and character of the entrance wounds and bullet are
taken into consideration.
Unless some special contrivance is employed, self inflicted gunshot wounds require that the trigger of the gun
be held within an arms length of the body - usually not more than 75cm. In cases involving long barrelled
weapons, contrivances such as a string hooked around the trigger, or activation of the trigger with a toe, must
be employed to discharge the gun.

Position of the weapon
The position of the weapon at the scene of a fatal shooting may be important in determining the nature of the
death. Where a firearm is found grasped in the hand of the dead person, it is preferable that the gun be
removed from that hand by the Pathologist. Only the Pathologist is competent to distinguish true cadaver
spasm from that of a gun placed in the hand by a murderer to simulate a suicide.

9.3.7. Death as a Result of Injuries Caused by Vehicles
When a body is discovered on a roadway, it is important to consider whether the victim was:
• Murdered by means of a vehicle
• Murdered by some other means and later placed where found in an effort to simulate a traffic accident
• Killed in a hit and run accident. In such cases, scenes should be preserved and examined in the same
way as other serious crimes
Post mortem examination will distinguish between injuries received before and after death.
When foul play is suspected, the general investigative procedures for murder, as already outlined, should be
adhered to. As most road traffic deaths are subject to autopsy by hospital pathologists, it is important that the
pathologist concerned is informed before he or she carries out the examination, of the possibility of having to
distinguish ante mortem from post mortem injuries. Gardaí attending a post mortem should ensure that the
pathologist is thoroughly briefed beforehand.

9.3.8. Death as a Result of Beating
Deaths as a result of beatings are invariably murder and must always be treated as such from the outset.
The significant outward indications of a beating are extensive bruises caused by assaults with fists, feet or a
weapon. When a weapon is used, it may leave distinct impressions on the body and be identified accordingly.
Similarly, when a person has been kicked or stamped upon, shoe impressions may be found on the body
or clothing.
This aspect must be given great consideration when dealing with the person who claims to have found
the body and made the report to the Gardai as that person’s hands, clothing and footwear may hold
vital evidence.
If weapons were used, most offenders will attempt to dispose of their implements sooner rather than later.
This means that they may have disposed of them close to the scene of the beating. This should be kept in
mind when deciding what amount of area to cordon off around a scene. For instance, if the body is found at
the side of a road, do not just cordon off the area around the body, but cordon off the complete roadway and
surrounding ditches until a full and satisfactory search of the area has been carried out.
Beatings of, or non accidental injuries to, children are sometimes only detected when fatal consequences
ensue. A full skeletal survey is mandatory in every case.

9.3.9. Death by Burning
Burned bodies normally lie in a distorted position – referred to as the ‘pugilistic attitude’. This is caused by
contraction of the muscles under the action of heat. Blisters of varying sizes develop on the skin. The skin and
soft tissues crack gradually and the cracks sometimes have even edges, which might easily be confused with
cut or stab wounds. The bones become brittle and breakages may occur.
The body is charred gradually by the heat and reduced to a torso outline. The body of a newborn baby can be
burned in an ordinary stove fire in two hours but a temperature of 1,250 degrees Celsius is required to
consume an adult body in the same time. Only a few bones will remain.
In the investigation of a death involving fire, the following questions must be asked:
• Did death occur before, during or after the fire?
• What was the cause of death?
• Did death occur due to natural causes, accident, suicide or murder?
• What is the identity of the victim?
Indications that the victim was alive at some stage during the fire include:
• The discovery of smoke and soot in the air passages
• The discovery of significant levels of carbon monoxide in the blood or tissues
• The presence of cyanide in blood where the victim inhaled fumes from the burning of certain types
of plastic
The cause of death and injuries discovered can only be determined by the Pathologist. When only bones or
parts of a body remain, the pathologist should examine the remains in situ.

In the identification of severely burned victims, when personal effects, face and fingerprints are completely
destroyed, the single most important investigation is the dental one. The teeth are the part of the body most
resistant to fire. Most people have been to a dentist at some time in their life and have had their teeth
charted. Even dentures may survive a fire and provide vital clues.
Just as severely burned bones elsewhere are fragile, so the jaws are no exception. Therefore in the
investigation of any fire disaster, the Forensic Odontologist has a vital part to play and should be called in
with the State Pathologist, to the scene if necessary, to help with identification of victims. It should be
noted that teeth may be also a source of DNA.

9.3.10. Death by Poisoning
Death may result when the body ingests food, drugs, chemicals or noxious gases which destroy the balance
of metabolism, paralyse the central nervous system, destroy or injure the digestive tract, or cut off the
supply of oxygen to the blood.
Poisons may generally be classified as follows:

Gases
The majority of ‘gassing’ deaths consist of fatal poisoning by either carbon monoxide or natural gas.

Anaesthetics
Anaesthetics can be administered preliminary to such crimes as robbery, rape or murder. The principal
symptoms are unconsciousness and respiratory failure.

Corrosives
This group of poisons produce a lethal effect principally by means of a destructive local action in the mouth,
throat and stomach. Frequently, some of the corrosive is regurgitated from the mouth and nose, burning the
skin around these areas.

Metallics
Arsenic was once the most common metallic poison used in murder. The principal symptoms of metallic
poisoning are burning of the mouth and throat, abdominal pain, vomiting and severe diarrhoea. These
poisons are usually slow acting and rarely cause death within a few hours. Frequently it requires several days
and often two or three weeks before death occurs.

Organic
These poisons, which are extracted from various plants, are conveniently classified as being either alkaloid or
non alkaloid. The alkaloids include strychnine, nicotine, atropine, scopolamine, opium and its derivatives such
as morphine, heroin, codeine, papaverine, paregoric and laudanum.
The most common characteristic of the alkaloids are that they kill quickly, only small quantities are necessary,
and they principally affect the nervous system. Strychnine causes convulsions; the corners of the mouth are
drawn up and the face fixed in a grin, the arms and legs are drawn together and the back is severely
bent backwards.
Sedative and hypnotic drugs include alcohol, barbiturates, the benzodiazepines and the tricyclic
antidepressants. Stimulant drugs include cocaine and the amphetamine related drugs.

Food poisoning
Food poisoning is a term which is conventionally applied to contamination of food by various micro organisms,
mostly bacteria, but also some viruses (enteroviruses). The common bacteria are staphylococci, salmonellae,
and clostridium welchii. A rare but lethal form is due to clostridium botulinum, present in incorrectly preserved
meat products.
All types may cause vomiting and diarrhoea while in botulism paralysis can prove rapidly fatal.

General
The presence of certain poisons in the human body can sometimes be confirmed a long time after death.
Metallic poisons do not disappear with putrefaction, and arsenic can be detected in hair and bones even
centuries afterwards. Lead also remains in the bone tissue for a long time. Scopolamine, strychnine and
morphine can still be detected after several years, and carbon monoxide up to six months after death.
Hypnotics decompose and disappear quickly - some even in the time which elapses between the
administration and occurrence of death. An exception is barbitone, which can be detected eighteen months
after death.
When exhuming the body of a person suspected of having died from metallic poisoning, especially arsenic,
samples of soil from the grave should always be taken, for the soil itself may contain the poison as a result
of seepage.
The investigation of a suspected poisoning death may conveniently be broken down into five general areas:
• Examination and investigation of the scene
• Collection of physical evidence
• Establishing the source and origin of the poison;
• Interviewing witnesses and suspects
• Post mortem examination

Investigation of Poisoning
Examination of the scene
If the examination of the scene does not disclose the type of poison used, the toxicologist may fail to identify
it, especially if it is a substance not in common use. The search must therefore be meticulous.
This initial search should be carried out by members of the Garda Technical Bureau, and following their
examination of the scene, a further search should be carried out by the Divisional Search Team so as to ensure
no evidence has been lost or missed.
Prior to the arrival of the Technical Bureau, when the preliminary examination is being made, nothing should
be disturbed, thereby ensuring that the scene is kept intact for technical examination.

Collection of physical evidence
The collection of physical evidence should never be limited to known, or suspected poisonous substances.
Whenever possible the following materials and objects should be secured:
• Samples of any beverages or substances suspected as being poisonous including vomit, faecal matter,
urine, and blood from the victim
• Samples of food, medicines, beverages, drugs or similar materials that the victim may have consumed.
• Samples of foods in which the poison might easily be concealed, and liquids, spilled or otherwise
should be collected
• Sinks, pipes and drain traps, dust bins, dumping areas in the vicinity of the house, as well as cupboards
and refrigerators which may contain evidence of the poisonous substances involved.
• Samples of any stomach contents collected through the use of a gastric lavage
• Containers, vessels and utensils that might have held any liquid, food or medicine
• Hypodermic needles, syringes and so on
• Samples of fuel or chemicals
• Soiled linen or clothing
• Samples of cosmetics, antiseptics, household sprays, paint and paint solvents, insecticides,
weedkillers, and liquid fuels
Investigating members should establish details of the heating, cooking or industrial fuels such as gas, oil, or
diesel used on the premises and the manner in which they are supplied to and around the building.
Establishing the source of the poison
When endeavouring to determine the source of a particular poison, its availability and the class of person who
would have easy access to it are essential considerations to be borne in mind. Sources are numerous and vary
from pharmacies to illicit drug channels to the local corner shop or supermarket.
Interviewing witnesses and suspects
The facts and circumstances of an individual investigation will dictate the line of questioning to be followed.
See Chapters 3 and 4 for general guide lines in regard to the interviewing of witnesses and suspects.
Post Mortem examination
To assist in carrying out the post mortem examination, the Gardaí should supply the Pathologist with the
fullest information possible, including:
• Known facts as to the circumstances of death
• Medical history of deceased and name of any doctor who recently attended the deceased
• Attending doctor's observations and treatment of symptoms exhibited by the victim prior to death
• Particulars of any poison or drug, including narcotics, believed to have been taken by deceased
• Information regarding the last movements of the deceased, their place and type of work, and any other
information that the investigation may uncover that may be of assistance

• Vomits - entire quantity should be taken by the Scenes of Crimes Examiner

• What was the cause of death?

• Was the poison contained in any food or beverage? (This may assist in determining the location where

9.4. Organisation of Murder Investigation
The intensity of the investigation into a suspicious death will subside or develop as the cause of death becomes
known. Though at the outset there may be indications of death other than by murder, it is always essential
to commence the investigation on the basis that foul play is involved.
Always preserve the designated scene intact for expert examination. The procedures applicable to the
investigation of major crimes should be implemented in all murder or suspicious death investigations. (See also
Chapters 7 and 8.).

9.4.1. Duties of the Senior Investigating Officer
In addition to the duties outlined at Chapter 8.6, the following are applicable:
• Have the body identified as soon as possible and utilise the media if necessary for this purpose
• When identity has been established, detail members to carry out the following courses of action:
• Visit deceased's home and interview occupants regarding his/her habits, associates, vehicles,
movements prior to death, when last seen, and obtain particulars of his/her clothing, jewellery,
personal effects, location and time of last meal
• If possible, examine deceased's room and take possession of photographs, documents, diaries, etc.,
for potential use in the investigation. The member/s carrying out this duty will have been allocated the
specific function and title of ‘Family Liaison Officer/s’, whose function will be to keep in constant
contact with the close relatives and family of the deceased for the duration of the investigation, for
both investigative and compassionate reasons

9.4.2. Identification of Deceased
It is essential that a dead body be identified without delay and there is no objection to searching clothing
providing the position of the body is not disturbed. Caution should be exercised in basing a decision on
identity solely on documents found on a body - these could be stolen, faked, or planted. As a precaution, at
the post-mortem, fingerprints should be taken, the body photographed and described (including dental data)
and a record made of clothing and objects found.

Recording Fingerprints of Deceased
The purpose of taking fingerprints in this case is that
they may be compared with latent prints discovered in
the home or place of work of the individual and thus
lead to their positive identification, and possibly as
proof of the deceased’s movements prior to their
death. Identification may also be made where the
victim's fingerprints are on Garda files.

Taking Photographs of Deceased
If a body remains unidentified after the post mortem
examination, the face should be ‘naturalised’ so as to
obtain a more life like photograph. The simplest
measure is for the mortician to wash the face, clean and comb the hair, colour the lips with a suitable colour

In addition to recording the external physical features, a note should be made of amputations, bodily

If there is a case of facial disfigurement through, for example injury or skeletal state, a Dental expert may be

Decomposed, Mutilated or Skeletal Remains
DNA is usually the best means of identification in these cases. Samples should be taken at the post mortem
for DNA comparison with putative relatives. In addition, the pathologist may be able to give an indication of
the sex and age of the deceased.

9.4.3. Establishing the Time of Death
The time of death can be established by Garda investigations and pathological examination. In the latter case,
body temperature, stomach contents, rigor mortis and putrefaction are the principal determining factors.
• Body temperature: The body temperature during life is 98 degrees F. Although the rate of cooling
depends on age and health factors and also on the environmental temperature, a body will feel cold
after 8 to 12 hours and the air temperature will be attained after 18 to 20 hours.
• Rigor mortis: Rigor mortis is the contraction and stiffening of the muscles and commences three to five
hours after death in the face and jaws and extends downwards to the feet. Overall rigidity is usually
achieved after eight to twelve hours and the condition remains from twelve to twenty four hours. The
rigidity leaves the body in the same order in which it develops.
• Stomach contents: An entirely empty stomach is indicative of death taking place at least four to six
hours after the last meal. If the small intestine is also empty it is probable that death occurred twelve or
more hours after the last meal. If the stomach is found to be well filled with food, death most likely
took place within one hour after the last meal. Establishing what kind of food has been consumed can
be very important to an investigation to establish the last movements of the deceased, and could also
be useful in corroborating, or otherwise, statements of witnesses or potential suspects in the case.
• Putrefaction: The development of putrefaction is mainly influenced by environmental air temperature;
the warmer it is, the more rapid the decomposition is. In warm water, the skin of the fingers and toes
become white and puckered after about two hours; and after two or three weeks of immersion, the skin
will peel off with nails attached. The process, termed ‘maceration’, is much slower in cold water.
The assistance of an Entomologist can also help in establishing the time of death by correlating the growth of
insect larvae on the body remains.

Post Mortem Lividity
When death occurs, the blood begins to settle in the lowest parts of the body and a blue or reddish violet
discolouration appears there. This is known as lividity. While lividity is not an indication as to the time of
death, it will usually indicate if the body has been moved or disturbed after death.

9.4.4. Post Mortem Examination
Employment of the State Pathologist
As a general rule, a post mortem examination by the State Pathologist should be sought in any case where
there are suspicious circumstances attaching to the death. In appropriate cases, the Senior Investigating
Officer of the investigation should utilise the procedures outlined in section 33 of The Coroners Act, 1962.
Removal of Body from Scene
The trace evidence on the body at the scene may be crucial to solving the crime and every effort should be
made to preserve it before moving the body. This involves sellotape lifting exposed areas of skin and, if
necessary, the deceased’s clothing. If there is heavy bleeding the clothes should be removed prior to
transporting the body as the movement of wet blood could obscure other evidence.
It is important that the Pathologist view the body in situ at the scene, and carry out a preliminary examination.
Clean plastic bag coverings should be placed on the head, hands and feet with the whole body then being
enclosed or wrapped in plastic covering.
The body should be transported in the position in which found, preferably in an undertaker's ‘shell’ to avoid
undue disturbance. A member should always accompany the body to the mortuary and remain with it until
the post mortem examination commences.
Identification of Body at Post Mortem Examination
The member who accompanied the body to the mortuary must identify it to the Pathologist - this is essential
to maintain the continuity of evidence. The formal identification of the body to the Pathologist by a relative
should be done at the mortuary. It is undesirable to have this done at the scene, as a relative will be likely to
disturb the body and interfere with the scene.
The Senior Investigating Officer, or someone delegated by them, should be present at the post mortem so that
they can be apprised of the medical findings, as well as to supply any information required by the pathologist.
Results of Post Mortem Examination
The opinion of the Pathologist will be based on the following factors:
• Age and sex of deceased
• Probable time of death
• Age of injury and whether ante or post mortem
• Cause of death
• Description of weapon or substance used
• Time interval between wounds received and death
• Drug or alcohol content of the blood
• Evidence of molestation, virginity, pregnancy or venereal disease
• Whether death resulted from natural causes, accident, suicide or murder
The body should not be released for burial until the approval of the Pathologist has been obtained.

9.5. Murder Reported after Burial of the Body
Investigation
If an allegation of foul play is made subsequent to the interment of a body, the State Pathologist should be
consulted and, if it is then considered appropriate, an exhumation order should be obtained.

Exhuming the Body
Arrangements should be made with the State Pathologist, Forensic Scientists in appropriate cases, Garda
Technical Bureau, cemetery authorities and witnesses necessary for the identification of the body, as to the
date and time when the exhumation will be carried out.
The collection of the following material should be considered in cases where poisoning is suspected:
• Sample of soil from surface above the coffin
• Sample of soil from a position six inches above the coffin
• Samples of soil from each side of the coffin
• Samples of soil from beneath the coffin, preferably from near the seat of the stomach and liver.
If the grave is waterlogged, samples of the water should also be taken
• Control samples of soil from other parts of the cemetery, especially the area adjacent to the grave.
• All samples should be enclosed in sterile, airtight containers, which should be sealed and labelled.

Identification of Body
In the absence of a relative, identification may be established by the undertaker or the cemetery employee
who made and closed the grave.

Establishing the Cause of Death
In some cases, a full x ray examination may be required if there is reason to suspect violence. It is preferable
before, but may be conducted after the post mortem examination. The body should be photographed. Post
mortem examination should be carried out and tissues removed for scientific examination. Samples of coffin
wood, caulking, sawdust, lining and portions of the shroud should be taken. From a medico legal point of
view, the exhumation of a dead body can never be as satisfactory as a primary post mortem.

9.6. Motive for Murder
The likelihood of a successful investigation is considerably enhanced once the motive is established. It is
imperative at all times, and especially where the initial evidence does not fix suspicion on a particular person,
that the motive be established as soon as possible.
