Chapter 10 Infanticide, Unlawful Abortion and Concealment of Birth

10.1. Infanticide
The wilful killing of a baby by its mother is a crime which has evoked the compassion of legislators in many
countries. This compassion is expressed in the relevant legislation taking specifically into account the mental
condition and circumstances of the offender.
The killing is murder and investigated as such, but has been designated as the specific crime of Infanticide by
the Infanticide Act, 1949. Under this Act, a woman who would be liable to a charge of murdering her own
child shall be guilty of Infanticide if, at the time of the act or omission causing the death of that child, being a
child under the age of 12 months, "the balance of her mind was disturbed by reason of her not having fully
recovered from the effect of giving birth to the child or by reason of the effect of lactation consequent upon
the birth of the child, and may for that offence be tried and punished as for manslaughter".
The charge, in practice, is likely to succeed only on the clearest evidence of deliberate killing. The mother is
given the benefit of the doubt to the fullest extent, the child being presumed to have been still born until the
contrary is proved. The onus of proving that the child was born alive rests on the prosecution. The principal
evidence to support a charge of Infanticide is that of the Pathologist who should be in a position to answer the
following questions:
• Did the infant have a separate existence?
• If so, how did it die?
• When did these things happen?

Separate Existence
Before an Infanticide charge can be made, it must be clear that the infant had a separate existence. Viability is
the ability to acquire a separate existence which is accepted in law as being after the 28th week of gestation
though modern intensive care enables infants as early as 23 weeks gestation to survive. The question of
viability does not, strictly speaking, arise in Infanticide but it may be an indication of the vigour with which the
child might be expected to have striven to maintain a separate existence.
A separate existence requires complete expulsion from the body of the mother, but not severance of the
umbilical cord. A child can have a separate existence when its body has been completely delivered, but is still
attached to the placenta (after birth) within the mother's womb.
The presence of food, such as partly digested milk, would point to the fact that a child had survived its birth,
although its absence would not point to the opposite conclusion. It has been established too that proof of
breathing is not proof of live birth as this may occur within the mother’s womb after the membranes burst.
The presence of milk in the stomach is regarded as evidence of separate existence as are certain changes in the
umbilical cord. A state of maceration means that death occurred ‘in utero’ (in the womb) and precludes the
possibility of a separate existence.
Foetal membranes enclosing the head also preclude a separate existence; this phenomenon is described as a
‘caul’. Apart from maceration, there is no means of judging by external appearance alone, whether there was
a separate existence. Only an autopsy can decide this. At autopsy, reliance on flotation of pieces of lung (‘the
hydrostatic test’) is only possible when gas due to bacterial growth in decomposition is excluded. In addition,
microscopic confirmation of lung expansion must be carried out.

Cause of Death
The Infanticide Act, 1949 requires that the child, having attained a separate existence, shall have met its death
from some wilful act or omission. The most common wilful acts causing death are:

Chapter 10 Infanticide, Unlawful Abortion and Concealment of Birth

• Suffocation or strangling
• Drowning
• Injury - usually to the head
• Stabbing
Proving that certain acts are, in fact, wilful acts depends primarily on autopsy reports and statements. The
difficulty here relates specifically to suffocation or strangling. While clear evidence of the marks or imprints of
hands may be visible in either case, these should not be mistaken for marks and scratches caused by strenuous
attempts at self delivery by the mother.
Suffocation may be caused by a pillow or other similar object; it may also be caused by stuffing the baby's
mouth with paper, mud, cloth or other similar material. It may also happen that a mother may use such plugs
on a baby which has been still born, believing it to have been a live birth. Unless this act is followed by
concealment, there is no offence.
A newly born infant may have sustained injury from several causes during delivery, but these would bear little
resemblance to injuries deliberately inflicted upon such an infant, this will be determined by the Pathologist.

10.2. Unlawful Abortion
Abortion can be procured by means of violence, abortifacient drugs and/or instruments. It is difficult to obtain
information and/or evidence of an unlawful abortion because of the secrecy in which the operation would
generally be carried out. Sometimes the fact that an unlawful abortion was carried out or attempted may only
come to light as the result of the death of the mother. Death occurring in such circumstances is treated and
investigated as murder.
Physical evidence is of particular importance where an unlawful abortion has been carried out. Where
syringes, instruments or other articles are found, they should first be examined for fingerprints by a competent
and qualified examiner.
In searching the house or premises of an abortionist, care must be taken not to overlook relevant evidence.
The member in charge should request the assistance of a doctor or other qualified person whilst making the
search. Anything suspicious found at the scene should be shown to them for their opinion as to whether or
not it might have been used in an abortion, or be of evidential value in bringing a case.

10.3. Concealment of Birth
In a charge of concealment of birth, there is no need to prove live birth as it is immaterial whether the child
died before, at, or after its birth. It is sufficient that the child was dead at the time of concealment. The three
most important investigative factors in a suspected concealment of birth are:
• Delivery
• Disposition
• Relationship

Chapter 10 Infanticide, Unlawful Abortion and Concealment of Birth

Delivery
It is necessary to obtain medical evidence
to prove that the woman actually had a
baby. The necessary examination for this
purpose can only be made with the
woman’s consent. In the absence of such
medical evidence, very discreet enquiries
may unearth witnesses to the delivery
from whom statements can be taken.
It is worth noting that anyone aiding and
abetting in concealment is equally culpable
and their statement must comply with the
necessary procedure applying to
statements from suspects and offenders.

Disposition
The concealment must be from the world at large, not just from a particular person; there may be sufficient
concealment even when made known to a number of people who are all pledged to secrecy. It must be
established if there was a secret disposition or if there was the possibility of the body being found. If the baby
was placed where there was a possibility of its being found, e.g. in a public park, there is no concealment as
such; if there is no such possibility, it amounts to concealment.

Relationship
If it is established that there has been a secret disposition, the connection between the body and the woman
who gave birth to it will have to be determined. The scientific use of DNA examination may be an important
tool in this regard, and it must be appreciated that every person shall, by any secret disposition of the dead
body of the said child, whether such child died before, at or after its birth is guilty of the offence of
concealment (Offences Against the Person Act 1861, S.60 refers).
An exhaustive examination of the body and the scene where it was found may provide the necessary clues.
The clothing or other object or container in which the body is wrapped or placed may provide useful evidence
whereby the necessary connection can be established. It is imperative, therefore, that these are handled with
expertise and care.
The relationship between an infant and its mother may be positively established by DNA typing; this method
requires a blood or muscle sample from the infant's body as well as a blood sample from the person suspected
of being the mother. The use of DNA typing is more relevant where Infanticide is suspected. However, in
concealment cases, the transfer of blood and bodily fluids from the child to any other person can be
established through DNA examination, which in turn could be evidence of that other person’s culpability in
the crime.

Chapter 11 Sexual Offences

11.1. Introduction
In this chapter, the term ‘sexual offences’ includes rape, rape under Section 4 Criminal Law (Rape)
(Amendment) Act 1990, aggravated sexual assault, sexual assault, unlawful sexual acts, defilement, buggery,
incest and attempts to commit any of the foregoing offences. Members should also be aware of the offences
created by the Child Trafficking and Pornography Act, 1998 concerning the possession and distribution of
child pornography, child trafficking and taking and so on of a child for purposes of sexual exploitation. The
offences under the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) (Amendment) Act 2007 of soliciting or importuning for the
purposes of the commission of sexual offences (Section 2) or of meeting a child for the purpose of sexual
exploitation (Section 6) should also be kept in mind.
Any investigation of these offences is particularly demanding of the investigator because of the discretion and
tact which should be employed. It is vital that members show sensitivity in their dealings with victims,
particularly when the victim displays behaviour that seems strange or uncharacteristic.
Sexual offence allegations are often difficult to either prove or disprove; thus the investigator has the dual role
of bringing the offender to justice and, on occasion, the responsibility of protecting the reputation of a falsely
accused innocent subject. However, it must be borne in mind that false allegations are not common.

11.2. Rape and Sexual Assault
On receipt of a complaint of a sexual offence a first consideration should be the necessity of immediate
medical assistance for the victim.
The allegations would be supported if the complainant is known to have reported the offence without delay
either to the Gardaí or another person. However, there is often a valid reason for a delay in reporting such
offences and the victim should be asked to account for the delay while making their statement.
The question of consent is often one of the most difficult factors to prove in the investigation.
While it is the responsibility of the appropriate court to adjudicate upon the degree to which consent was or
was not present in a given case, it is the duty of the investigator to unearth all the facts and circumstances
which surround the occurrence, so that the court can be fully informed.

The scene, occasion and background of the alleged incident are of prime
importance.
Obviously where a victim was asleep or unconscious at the time of the occurrence or was under the influence
of drink or drugs that rendered them incapable of knowingly consenting to the act alleged, ‘consent’ or lack
of it would then have to be carefully considered by the investigator.
Such incidences may involve the impersonation of a victim's partner or the pretence of carrying out a medical
examination. Another example of doubtful ‘consent’ would be where the victim was in a vulnerable state
while undergoing therapy or counselling.
‘Consent’ induced by fear or threats of immediate physical violence is not a true consent; for example, where
there is a fear of death or bodily harm, either to the victim or to their children, it is unlikely that ‘consent’
would then be withheld. The extent to which this fear was understood to exist should be fully investigated.
The need for a full exposition of the background story needs to be stressed.
Defence provisions are provided for in the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act, 2006 in cases of defilement of
children under 15 and 17 years and this Act creates two new offences which are gender neutral. While it is
not a defence to prove that the child consented to the act there is a defence of honest belief that the child
had attained the age of 15 or 17.

11.3. Interviewing the Victim
The victim of an alleged rape or sexual assault should be interviewed as soon as possible after the occurrence.
Whether the interviewers are male or female should be decided by the victim unless, in the circumstances, this
is not practicable.

It is important that the victim should not be interviewed in the company of any
potential witnesses.
A victim of rape or sexual assault may feel embarrassed at being asked to relate the details of the incident(s)
and may be suffering from post traumatic stress.
The investigating members must understand that the existence of these factors could affect their ability to
answer effectively questions relating to the crime. The attitude of the interviewers should always be one of
sympathy and understanding; however, the importance of obtaining a full and consistent statement must be
pointed out to the victim.

The victim’s statement is the most important evidence in every file as it decides
the extent of the crime and the gravity of the alleged offence. The fullest and
most detailed statement that the victim is capable of making should always be
obtained even if this has to be done over a long period of time or several
periods of time. The necessity for such detail should be explained to the victim
in every case.
Preliminary Information
The first member to meet the victim should observe and note:
• Time and date of complaint
• Full particulars of the complaint
• The general state of the victim - signs of mental shock or distress, state of hair, etc
• Any evidence of injury or marks, intoxication or drugs
• The state of clothing - torn or disarranged; buttons or jewellery missing; stains of mud, earth, blood or
semen on clothing
• Detailed description of the scene
• The member dealing with the victim should not have physical contact with any suspect prior to forensic
samples, clothing and so on (other items) being taken from the victim or suspect for fear of cross
contamination of vital forensic evidence
• No refreshments should be offered to the victim until it is established that oral sex has been eliminated as
an alleged offence.
• If there is a delay in getting to the medical examiner, consider using the Early Evidence Kit.
Victims should be allowed to tell their stories without interruption. At this stage particular notes should be
taken of the scene of the crime. A full description should also be taken of the alleged perpetrator, if not
identified by the victim, and this description should immediately be circulated.

Visiting the Scene of the Crime
The victim should be prepared by the investigating member to visit the scene of the crime. It should be
explained to the victim the importance of pointing out the scene. For example, it should be pointed out that
forensic evidence may be found there and that the fact of going there may help jog their memory. The scene
of the crime should only be visited by the victim with their consent taking extreme care that the scene is
not contaminated.

11.4. Possession of clothing and medical examination of
the victim
It is to be expected that the victim, having reported the crime or having had it reported on their behalf or with
their consent and approval will likewise consent to medical examination. This should be carried out at the
earliest opportunity.
Before making arrangements for such an examination, the investigator should determine if the clothes being
worn at the time of the complaint were the same ones as those worn on the occasion of the attack. If not,
the relevant clothing should be secured from the complainant for laboratory examination.
If the clothing is unchanged, arrangements should be made for a change of clothing so that the necessary
possession of those worn at the relevant time can be facilitated. This can easily be achieved at the time of
medical examination.

It is of critical importance to obtain the clothing, especially the underclothing,
without delay following the incident. It must be remembered that all clothing
worn at the time and immediately after the incident is relevant. It is advisable
that each garment be placed in individual exhibits bags sealed and properly
labelled. Gloves should be worn to avoid contamination
The presence of sperm in the injured party's vagina provides a certain proof; in children it may constitute
evidence of rape, whereas in adults, it merely indicates recent intercourse.

Medical Examination
The victim should be prepared by the
investigating member for the medical
examination, and apprised of its relevance
to the investigation. The written consent of
a parent or guardian will be required by the
doctor before carrying out an examination
of a person under 16 years of age.
The victim should be given the option of
being examined by a doctor of their choice
or, where there is one available, referred to a
Sexual Assault Treatment Unit.
The medical examination is a multipurpose one and may provide evidence of penetration, physical trauma and
also provides for the taking of samples required for examination in the Forensic Science Laboratory.
The Sexual Offences Examination Kit issued by the Forensic Science Laboratory must be used. This kit ensures
that the correct samples are taken in an appropriate manner. The samples taken should include plain swabs of

the relevant orifices, hair samples, blood samples and nail scrapings. All samples should be labelled clearly
with the complainant’s name. The site swabbed must also be included on the swabs. The kit should be sealed
immediately on receipt of the relevant samples.
The Early Evidence Kit should be used where there is an allegation of oral sex or where a toxicological
examination may be required in cases where there is going to be a delay between the offence and the Forensic
Clinical Examination
Semen is detectable for some days after vaginal intercourse whereas the detection time in respect of the anus
and mouth is considerably less. However the possibility of forensic evidence being found at any stage cannot
be ruled out and procedures should be carried out in an effort to recover the best evidence. (See Chapter
16.21 for more information on persistence of semen.)
These samples may be vital evidence linking the victim with a suspect from whom DNA and other samples
are taken.
Where arrangements are being made to have the victim medically examined at a hospital, advance notification
should be sent to that hospital in order to avoid any unnecessary delay. Injuries received by the victim should
be photographed at the hospital.
It should be remembered that medical evidence that a female victim had sexual intercourse, even though
amounting to corroboration of the fact of intercourse, is not in itself proof that the victim was raped.
Conversely, rape can be committed merely by a partial act of penetration and it is possible to commit the
offence without emission of semen or injury to the genital area.
If the victim wishes to have a bath or shower after the examination, they should be facilitated to so do.
The presence of venereal disease in the victim after the occurrence of rape can provide corroborative evidence
of association or of sexual relationship. If the culprit is suffering from a specific venereal disease, they may be
likely to transmit it to the victim during the commission of the offence.
Tests for venereal disease are not performed in the Forensic Science Laboratory and should be arranged by the
doctor examining the victim.

11.5. Formal Interview of the Victim
In all cases consideration must be given to having the victim interviewed by specialist interviewers.
Before a formal interview, the victim should be given a choice as to whether the interviewer should be male or
female and this choice should be acceded to save in exceptional or unusual circumstances.
In the course of the interview, the investigating member should obtain the following information in the exact
words of the victim:
Before the Crime:
• Detailed description of events leading up to the assault from the moment they met the assailant
• Whether they knew the assailant. If not, get full description; (see below under The Offender)
• How they got to scene of the rape/sexual assault
• Did anyone see them?
• What was said prior to, during, and after intercourse/assault?

The investigator should try to determine the victim's reliability as a witness. They should check the victim's

Where relevant, the hints given at Chapter 3.17: Testing a witness for credibility should be followed, ensuring
at all times that the victim should not be made to feel guilty or re victimised.

Description of Vehicle
If a vehicle was used, the investigator should secure a description of it, including the make, model, type and
registration number.
The victim may recall:
• The colour and general appearance of the vehicle
• The type of grill, bumpers, lamps, warning device, radio, cassette, DVD, etc
• The stickers on windshields, windows or bumpers; the presence of dangling items such as baby shoes, toy
animals, etc
• The type, colour and arrangement of doors, seats, floor mats and upholstery
• The details of the dashboard or the presence of unusual gadgets, accessories, keyholders, ashtrays and
interior lights
• The presence of packages, tools, clothing or other identifiable objects
• Whether injured party lost any item

Use of Camera/Video Equipment by Offender
It should be borne in mind that a number of culprits may photograph and/or video the commission of a sexual
offence. Such photographs and videos have an obvious evidential value but, depending on the age of the
victim, they may also constitute the production of ‘child pornography’ contrary to Section 5 of the Child
Trafficking & Pornography Act, 1998.

11.6. Interviewing a Child Victim
In all cases the child victim should be interviewed by Specialist Child Interviewers, unless there are urgent
extenuating circumstances.
In the course of interviewing a child
victim, the protection of the child's
innocence should be ensured. Here
the co operation of the parents and
guardians is particularly essential.
The child should not be made to feel
any degree of guilt as a result of the
reactions of others around them. The
child, therefore, must not be allowed
to think that they are in trouble or
that people think them to be at fault.
Investigators should be mindful that a
parent or guardian may be a suspect
in a case and adopt an appropriate
alternative interviewing strategy.

Chapter 11 Sexual Offences

The child should be interviewed in their home, or in other familiar surroundings, usually in the presence of a
parent or guardian.
The questions must be designed to elicit the fullest particulars of the occurrence and to make possible an
accurate assessment of the child's sense of truthfulness and concept of time and dates and so on. Questions
relating to parents, other members of the family (if any), school attendance, school and other friends,
television schedules and programmes, all provide the child with an outlet for discussion. As a general rule,
children like to discuss themselves and show off their knowledge; therefore, the greatest requirement on the
part of the interviewer is an ability to listen.
It would be very wrong to reveal to the child anything sexual that could extend beyond the scope of the
information seeking goal of the interview.
Because of the nature of the offence, parents and guardians of child victims are likely to be sensitive and
emotional. This is particularly so because sexual offences involving children are frequently found to be a series
of recurring offences over a period of time.
The interviewer should gain their confidence by making them aware of the determination to investigate the
case successfully, with the minimum of publicity. Stressing an appreciation of the seriousness of the complaint,
the interviewer should appeal for their co operation explaining that the tendency to commit sex offences
against children is indicative of a specific character trait on the part of the offender and it should be explained
that this trait poses immediate danger to other potential victims.

11.7. Examination of Scene
Investigators should spare no effort in locating and searching the scene of the crime. The victim may have only
a hazy or general idea of the location, particularly if a vehicle is involved. If at all possible, the injured party
should be requested to accompany the investigating member to the scene; always ensuring that the scene is
not contaminated by the victim. (See ‘Visiting the Scene of the Crime’ at section 11.3)
A logical starting point, if the location of the crime scene can not immediately be ascertained, is the place
where the victim was found. A search should be initiated from this point to locate the exact scene.
On location of the scene, the procedures referred to in Chapter 8 with regard to the preservation of the scene
should be complied with.
Where corroborative evidence of the crime exists at the scene or other location, it should be photographed in
situ. It should also be remembered that the scene of a reported rape or sexual assault, which was
accompanied by serious violence, may become a murder scene. Ideally, Divisional Scenes of Crime Examiners or
Technical Bureau personnel should examine all rape/sexual assault scenes, record them photographically and
gather all evidence.

Outdoors
The scene should be searched for shoe marks of both the victim and culprit(s) and vehicle tyre marks (if any)
involved. The exact spot should be scrutinised carefully for evidence of a struggle; it might also contain certain
personal belongings of either the victim or the offender, and, possibly, contraceptives or contraceptive
containers, tampons or other clothing.
Blood and semen stains should also be looked for on the ground or on nearby vegetation or objects. If the
victim was assaulted, bound, gagged or drugged, the scene might also contain evidence to substantiate such
claims. Accordingly, weapons, sticks, rope, cord, wire, drugs or drug containers, should be sought. Samples of
soil and vegetation should also be taken for comparison with materials likely to be found on the shoes, clothes
and/or vehicle of the suspect. The use of tents to protect scenes at this time should be borne in mind.

House or other Building
An examination should be made of the entry and exit points. Couches, beds, rugs, chairs, and other objects
should be searched for evidence of the offence and identity of the offender. Bathrooms, too, should be
searched carefully. Dust samples and samples of bedding, drapes, rugs, and upholstery should be taken for
comparison with foreign materials on the suspect.
Where a rape is alleged to have taken place in a bed, all bed clothing should be seized for laboratory
examination together with any clothing the victim might have been wearing at the time of, or which they put
on after, the alleged offence.

Vehicle
If it is alleged that the crime was committed in a vehicle, and the vehicle is located, a search should be made
therein for blood and semen stains, smudges of cosmetics, clothing, palm or finger-marks, contraceptives and
their containers. The exterior, including the undercarriage and tyres, should be examined for evidence that the
vehicle has been at the crime scene.

11.8. Arrest of Suspect
Whenever the elements of the alleged crime have been established, the suspect should be arrested as soon as
possible. A person arrested on suspicion of having committed ‘a sexual offence’ may be taken to and detained
in a Garda Síochána Station in accordance with the provisions of Section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 if
the provisions of that section apply.
Where there is no immediate likelihood of an arrest, information should be prepared and circulated. In sex
crimes, as in all other crimes, the modus operandi of the culprit is an important element of any such
information.
It is a mistake for the investigators to have preconceived conventional ideas of how the alleged sexual acts
were performed. Exact details of the precise deed(s) and manner in which perpetrated (e.g. whether an act of
intercourse, oral sex, one of innumerable incidents of sex play, or sadistic behaviour) should be established to
determine the sexual aspects of the culprit's modus operandi.
These informative factors can prove and have proven vital when comparison of sex crimes and suspects are
called for, particularly where these involve travelling (sex) criminals. Aspects of the modus operandi, other
than its sexual aspects, are likewise important and should not be overlooked.
In many circumstances the injured party may get a close up view of the culprit and the description may
facilitate the compilation of a composite sketch. Where the descriptions so warrant, the use of composite
sketches should be requested from the Technical Bureau. (See Chapter 7.4 under CD Fit Composite.)
Note: The directions of the Director of Public Prosecutions should be obtained before charging a suspect with a
sexual offence.

11.9. Obtaining Evidence from the Suspect
Where an arrest of the suspect has been made, their person and clothing might provide worthwhile additional
evidence, corroborating not only the allegation made but the evidence already to hand.
Where a person is detained pursuant to Section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 (see 11.8 above), evidence
may be obtained from them in accordance with the provisions of Section 6 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984
and Section 2 of the Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence) Act, 1990 as amended by Section 14 of the Criminal
Justice Act, 2006. See further detail at Appendix II, Section 3.

Chapter 11 Sexual Offences

If possible, the suspect's clothing should be examined for blood, semen and other stains and foreign particles
likely to connect them with the crime or the crime scene. After lawful arrest, the suspect's clothing can be
confiscated for such examination. (See also Section 7 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2006 in this context.)
The general condition of the suspect, if apprehended soon after the occurrence, should be noted. The
presence of marks indicating a struggle; especially bruises, scratches, teeth bites, torn clothes, and so on can
counter any possible plea of consent.
The provisions of Sections 18 and 19 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 as amended by Part 4 of the Criminal
Justice Act, 2007 (inferences that may be drawn from the suspect's failure to account for certain matters)
should, where relevant, be complied with in anticipation of possible corroborative evidence - see further detail
at Appendix II Section 4.

Medical Examination of Suspect
Arrangements should be made to have suspect medically examined, though this can only be done with their
consent and then only after proper caution. Usually the caution should be given by the member in charge of the
investigation in the presence of the doctor. Where the suspect refuses to consent to an examination, it will be
necessary to invoke the provisions of the Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence) Act, 1990 as amended - see
further detail at Appendix II Section 3.
Even where it is likely that the suspect will refuse to be medically examined, providing them with the
opportunity to consent to one is a necessary exercise as this eliminates any subsequent defensive plea that, if
afforded a medical examination, innocence could have been proven.
The medical examination may reveal evidence of:
• Recent emission of semen
• Injury to the penis
• Presence of blood on the penis
• Victim DNA on the penis
• Presence of blood, semen, faeces, or lubricant in the pubic hairs or at the penis base
• Presence of venereal disease
These, together with such other observations as might be available from the fingernail scrapings, local bruising
and injuries are worthwhile corroborative evidence. The Sexual Offences Kit is designed for use with suspects
as well as victims and should be made available to the doctor. The samples taken should include penile swabs,
blood, saliva and hair and any others the doctor and investigator may deem relevant. The underpants worn to
the medical examination should also be taken as the victim’s DNA may have transferred to it from the penis.
The provisions of the Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence) Act, 1990 as amended should be availed of to
acquire DNA and other relevant samples and evidence.

11.10. Contentious Allegations of Rape
On occasion it may come to light that a complainant has omitted certain facts or has provided inconsistent
information in relation to the offence alleged. Members must not immediately assume that the basis of such
allegations are false. It may be the case that the victim felt too embarrassed to mention certain facts, or
omitted information that did not seem relevant to the victim at the time of making their statement.
Where the victim delayed the reporting of a sexual offence for a period of time, the cause of the delay should
be established. It is here that the evidence of the first person who met the victim after the occurrence is
important. The first person that the victim told about the occurrence is also significant.

Chapter 11 Sexual Offences

Where there is a doubt about the allegation, evidence of resistance or any other information contained in the
witness statement (if any) assumes greater importance.
Possible witnesses should be identified who reside or have passed near the scene or along the route of
the alleged escape and they should be interviewed; their evidence, whether negative or positive, could
be important.
Generally speaking, the victim's evidence should, where possible, be corroborated by other evidence
implicating the accused even though such corroboration is not necessary to secure a conviction.
Medical evidence of rape or assault confirms only the commission of the crime and does not, by itself, prove
that the crime was in fact committed by the particular person accused.

False Accusations
While there may be false allegations of sexual offences on occasion, they are uncommon. It is essential that
victims are not made to feel that their allegations are not believed. Motives for making a false allegation of
rape or other sexual offences vary. Where a member believes that a false allegation has been made, the onus
is on that member to obtain all the relevant facts pertaining to the case and to double check every aspect of
what is alleged by the victim or what witnesses have said in their statements. The complaint must be
investigated fully and fairly in the interests of all concerned. (See also Chapter 3 in this context.)

11.11. Child Pornography
The Child Trafficking and Pornography Act, 1998 covers the sexual exploitation of a child and allowing them to
be used for the possession, production or distribution of child pornography. Members requiring assistance in
the investigation of a child pornography related offence should revert to the Paedophile Investigation Unit of
the Domestic Violence and Sexual Assault Investigation Unit (DVSAIU) at NBCI, Harcourt Square, Dublin 2.
Members should be aware that evidence of
this type of crime can be found on various
electronic storage media, such as laptops,
PCs, hard drives, floppy disks, CDs, DVDs
and so on. The background shown in some
of these images may actually identify the
crime scene itself. The establishment of the
location of the offence may require the
analysis of internet and telephone logs
which should be sourced in the appropriate
manner through the National Criminal
Intelligence Unit at Garda Headquarters.
When investigating a crime of this nature it is essential that at least one of the investigators has a good
working knowledge of computers in order to capture all the relevant evidence stored on computer equipment
as well as to allow for comprehensive questioning of the suspect involved.
It should be noted that prior to seizing electronic equipment the advice of the Paedophile Investigation Unit
and the Computer Crime Investigation Unit at the Garda Bureau of Fraud Investigation should be sought and
under no circumstances should the suspect be allowed access to any of this equipment even on the pretext of
assisting the members as the suspect could delete valuable evidence. (See also Chapter 13, ‘Computers and
Crime’ for further information on this topic.)
The same is true for mobile phones, cameras and camcorders; all of which should be seized and subsequently
examined and all data downloaded. Similarly, telephone records should be applied for through the National
Criminal Intelligence Unit to establish the various contacts made by the suspect.

Chapter 11 Sexual Offences

During an investigation of this nature it may be important to establish the identity and user of any credit card
available to the suspect.
Section 3 of the 1998 Act creates offences in regard to child trafficking and various other acts for the purposes
of the sexual exploitation of a child. A child is defined as a person under the age of 17.

11.12. Sexual Exploitation of Children
Section 6 of the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) (Amendment) Act, 2007 amends Section 3 of the Child
Trafficking and Pornography Act, 1998 to create two new offences. The first offence is where a person who
intentionally meets or travels with the intention of meeting a child within the State for the purpose of doing
anything that would constitute the sexual exploitation of that child. The second introduces a partial extraterritorial offence where a citizen (or a person ordinarily resident) in the State intentionally meets or travels
with the intention of meeting a child outside the State for the purpose of doing anything that would
constitute the sexual exploitation of the child.
‘Sexual exploitation’ is broadly defined under the section and includes:
• Inviting or using the child to engage in prostitution or the production of child pornography
• Inviting the child to participate in any sexual activity which is an offence under any enactment
• Inviting the child to participate in or observe any activity of a sexual or indecent nature
• The commission of any such offences against the child.
In each case the suspect must be proven to have met or communicated with the child on at least two
previous occasions but it is not necessary to establish that these meetings or communications were in any
way sexual.
A strict interpretation of the section does not require the conduct outside the State to be illegal in the other
country, but this is a matter for interpretation by the Irish courts in any given case.
These crimes apply to both the online and the offline environments and the terms ‘meet’ and ‘communicate’
therefore include telephone contact, written communications, electronic communications and face to face
meetings. The prompt uplifting of evidence of communication by way of the technical examination of
documents, recovery of CCTV/video footage or the recovery of telephone or computer data will be of primary
importance in such cases. Further information on these topics is set out at Chapter 13 under ‘Computers and
Crime’ and Chapter 14 under ‘Examination of Handwriting and Documents’.

11.13. Soliciting or Importuning a Child for the Purpose of the
Commission of a Sexual Offence
Section 2 of the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) (Amendment) Act, 2007 creates the offence of soliciting or
importuning a child or a person who is mentally impaired for the purpose of the commission of an offence of
defilement under Section 2 or Section 3 of the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act, 2006 (children under 15
years and 17 years respectively) or an offence of sexual assault under Section 2 of the Criminal Law (Rape)
(Amendment) Act, 1990. These offences apply regardless of whether the purposes were for prostitution
or not.

Chapter 12 Bombs - The Garda Response

12.1. Introduction
The aim of this chapter is to assist Gardaí manage the response to bomb related incidents by:
• Developing a greater understanding of the terrorist threat, together with an awareness of the potential
effects of an explosion and related incidents
• Minimising the risk to Garda personnel and members of the public
• Minimising damage to property
• Increasing members’ knowledge of the various issues involved
• Increasing the confidence and competence of members involved with cordons, rendezvous points, initial
action, scene preservation, liaison and communication
The procedures and advice contained in this chapter should be read in conjunction with the current Major
Emergency Plan and information issued in relation to Chemical, Biological, Radiological and Nuclear Incidents
(CBRN). See also section 16.15 in regard to the role of the Forensic Science Laboratory.
Remember:
• Complacency is the enemy of safety
• Beware of secondary devices
• The scene of an explosion is the scene of a serious crime
• An explosion may involve a CBRN component
• Shattered glass causes most injuries at bomb scenes
The procedures in place in each Division to respond to bomb threats are an essential aspect of Major
Emergency Planning. Each local Divisional Officer should ensure that regular liaison is maintained with all local
stakeholders in the emergency response services. Regular simulations and table top exercises with appropriate
follow up reviews should be carried out. Training deficiencies identified as a result of these exercises should be
addressed as appropriate (See also 12.3 ‘Preventative Measures).

12.2. Receipt of Bomb Reports
Bomb reports usually come to the notice of the Gardai in a number of ways:
• The person who notifies the Gardai knows that the report is without basis in fact, but makes the
notification for some malicious purpose. These notifications are usually made by telephone or note and
the caller or writer remains anonymous. Such reports are termed ‘Hoax Bomb Calls’.
• The person who notifies the Gardai believes or knows that a bomb may actually be present. Such persons
usually identify themselves voluntarily and will assist the Gardai in the subsequent investigation.
• The person who notifies the Gardai is the person responsible for planting the bomb and is calling for the
purposes of giving a warning. Certain terrorist groups have traditionally used code words to ‘authenticate’
a bomb warning and such warnings may be channelled through any organisation or person. Code words

may become public knowledge and have been associated with hoax calls, so while full details should be
noted they should not be taken as a guarantee of authenticity.
• Written bomb reports. These are less frequently encountered than telephone reports but they should be
treated with equal care and attention with the original document being preserved carefully and
immediately placed in a cellophane envelope. Further handling must be avoided to preserve fingerprints,
handwriting, postmarks or any other form of trace evidence.
The initial action of the Gardai should result in one of the following:
• It may be established that the call is a hoax by the investigating member(s) being completely satisfied that
no suspect device as reported is present at the site identified. This decision is often taken in consultation
with the Head of Security of the relevant building who may have advance procedures in place to deal with
such reports and is in a position to speedily confirm that no threat exists. In this case the follow up
investigation will be directed towards identifying the person who made the false report.
• A suspect device may be located. Subsequent investigation may disclose the contents to be harmless or to
contain a bomb or an object or device which has the appearance of a bomb. In this case, members should
proceed as set out in this chapter.

Anonymous reports
The fact that a report is made anonymously should never minimise the seriousness with which the report is
viewed. Garda action must always proceed on the possibility of a bomb being present bearing in mind the
serious loss of life and damage to property that even a small device can cause.
The bomb threat places grave responsibilities on members once such a report is received.

12.3. Preventative measures
District and Divisional Officers should ensure that regular consultation is maintained with the local community
in all aspects of crime prevention. High risk areas or premises such as oil installations or chemical plants should
be identified and appropriate prevention plans and strategies put in place for them.
This will usually be achieved through consultation with the management and security personnel of the
companies or other organisations involved in these high risk areas or premises.
The relevant local authorities should also be encouraged to put the necessary contingency plans in place in
respect of public areas or buildings which may be the target of a terrorist or similar attack. Such buildings and
public areas include airports, railway stations, bus terminuses, busy motorways or other centres where large
groups of people tend to congregate.
Local trade associations and leading businesses should be consulted in respect of shopping centres or other
commercial venues.
The expert advice of the Garda Technical Bureau is available on request in developing local prevention and
contingency plans and the local Defence Forces Explosive Ordinance Disposal (EOD) Commander may also
be consulted.
Contingency plans should include Chemical, Biological, Radiological and Nuclear (CBRN) incidents and
consultation with the specialist emergency services personnel trained in this area. The services of the local
Crime Prevention Officer and specialist staff at Community Relations Section, Harcourt Square may also be
consulted as necessary.
Controlled access and other measures
In essence this means making access to likely targets, either within buildings or in public places, as difficult as
possible. Maximum security and controlled entry will assist in this objective.

Other measures which may be taken include:
Prevention
• Identifying and eliminating risks presented by potential hiding places for improvised explosive devices
• The development of procedures to ensure the timely identification of suspect correspondence/parcels
• Controlled access to confidential information
• Clear policies on access control and privileges to IT, keys, passwords, restricted areas and so on
Aiding Response
• Maintaining all exits free of obstruction
• Installation and maintenance of emergency power facilities
• Installation and maintenance of appropriate CCTV coverage
• Periodic inspection of fire fighting equipment, first aid kits and emergency exits
• Regular performance of fire drills and emergency evacuation plans
• Maintenance of up to date maps of the building
• Display of maps showing exit routes at frequent intervals throughout the building
• Continuous ongoing training and review

12.4. Action Following Advance Warning of a Bomb Explosion
All bomb threats communicated by telephone or other means of communication must be taken seriously.
Terrorist groups may make reports when Improvised Explosive Devices (IEDs) with timing devices have already
been planted.
Experience to date has shown less likelihood of advance warning to the authorities in cases where the bomb
has been detonated by remote control, where the bomb was fixed under a vehicle or where the purpose of
the explosion was to kill or injure military personnel or an attack directed at a military establishment.
Decisions made in response to calls can be complicated by:
• Deciding on the authenticity of the call in view of the number of hoax calls received
• Not having sufficient time to react
• Receiving misleading information about the location and timing of the alleged bomb
Bear in mind that terrorist groups do not normally contact the premises or site to be attacked. The advance
warning is usually passed through some third party.

Response to bomb warnings
The precise text of an advance bomb warning must be passed immediately to the local Garda Communication
Centre. Unless a bomb warning can be conclusively graded as a hoax by the Senior Garda Officer in charge,
the following response will immediately be put in place;
• An immediate full-scale response should be initiated.
• The senior operational Garda available in the District will immediately attend the scene and take charge.
The local District/Divisional Officer will be informed immediately.
• The first priority will be to identify the location of a suspect device in order to co-ordinate an evacuation. In
this context, the possibility of secondary devices should not be overlooked.
• The presence of a specific type of device should be confirmed, if possible.
• Everyone in the vicinity of the potential explosion should be immediately alerted to the risk.
• It should be established quickly whether existing search/security arrangements, in place at the site where
the threat relates, effectively eliminate the risk of an explosion.
• It should be established if the site is one where a search can readily identify that no IED is present.
• As assessment of the above criteria or any other specific features present will determine whether
evacuation, road closures and the implementation of a cordon are warranted.
• Responsibility for such actions rests with the senior Garda member in charge who will maintain a real time
decision log in this context.
Whenever a suspect device is found, Garda action must be clear. Immediate efforts must be made
to cordon, evacuate and contact the local Defence Forces Explosive Ordinance Disposal (EOD)
personnel.

Contacting the EOD Personnel
Every Defence Forces Command area maintains a specialist Explosive Ordinance Disposal (EOD) Team. EOD
support should only be sought when a suspect device has been located. In certain circumstances, however, it
may be prudent to advice the EOD Team of an impending request. Gardai are also free to seek the advice of
an Army EOD Officer when considered appropriate.
Local arrangements should be in place in every Garda Communications Centre for contacting the local EOD
team including arrangements for Garda escort of the Army team to the scene. (See also 12.11 below)

Incidents in a public place
The first Garda member on the scene is required to make an initial threat assessment. Consideration should
be given to the following:
• The identification of a suspect device
• The content of the message
• The location given
These factors must be evaluated quickly. This evaluation becomes critical when there is limited time left to the
time given for a threatened explosion. Remember, the time given may not be relied upon as being accurate.

6

Chapter 12 Bombs - The Garda Response

In some circumstances, local knowledge or special instructions will help when a specific threat exists. Advice
can be sought from the EOD, Ballistics Section or Technical Bureau to assist the decision making process in
determining whether an identified suspect device represents a threat.
The Senior Garda at the scene will co-ordinate the response. Garda vehicles must not park near the suspect
location and should if possible be located at an arranged Rendezvous Point (RVP).

Incidents at Private Premises
The decision to evacuate private premises lies with the Senior Garda at the scene and the occupier has a
responsibility to comply with that member’s directions.
When dealing with an advance bomb warning (prior to any suspect device being identified), the Gardaí will
immediately take responsibility for the public area(s) of the place to which the threat relates. A risk assessment
must be made before making a decision regarding whether or not to evacuate the premises.
It is therefore essential that when Garda members are responding to an advance bomb warning, the head of
security or occupier of private premises MUST be informed as to the origin of the threat as advised to the
Gardai and the Garda action in response. This is to enable a risk assessment to be made and to facilitate
further Garda action.
Gardaí are unlikely to know the layout of the premises and the various places in which a device could be
concealed. The assistance of staff working in the building, who know the layout, should be secured to ensure
a quick and thorough search is conducted. In addition, Gardaí will not know what items of property,
machinery and furniture should be present and without staff assistance will not as easily identify anything out
of place.
The head of security will retain responsibility for implementing their organisation’s security precautions. This
person will be involved in all activities connected with search and evacuation from a ‘control point’, and will
advise Gardaí of the precautions taken within the premises. On discovery of a suspected improvised explosive
device (IED) or on being advised of a suspected IED, they may order an internal evacuation, a partial evacuation
of the premises or a total evacuation to a pre designated safe area.
The mnemonic/acronym ‘BOMB ALERT’ has been devised to help as an Aide Memoir. This stands for:
Building; Occupiers; Method of Search; Back Off; Accurate Information; Locate Suspects; Evacuate;
Rendezvous Point; Tape-off Area.

Building
• Type of building?
• Is it controversial or political?
• What is known or can be found out?

Occupiers
• Who lives or works there?
• Is the occupant listed in any voter register or Thom’s directory?

12.5. Suspect objects and packages
Attention may be drawn to a suspect object or packages for any of the following reasons:
• The object or parcel is marked ‘Bomb’, ‘Danger’, ‘Do not open’, etc
• The object or parcel is similar to that described in the initial report
• The object or parcel is foreign to its position or to the premises
• The reason for the object or parcel being there is questionable or
cannot be readily determined
• Due to the size, shape, weight or availability of the object or parcel,
it is suspicious

Bomb Devices
Devices may be divided into three classes:
Time or delayed action devices
These are so designed that they detonate after a given interval. The delaying component can be chemical,
electrical or mechanical or a combination of all three. It is important to note that a time-delay bomb is nearly
always well concealed so that it is not likely to be discovered prior to detonation.
Manual, anti-disturbance or anti-handling devices
These are so constructed that they detonate instantaneously on being interfered with. This type of device is
usually meant to be found by the person for whom it is intended.
Remote control device
This is one which can be detonated at a distance.

Precautions
The following precautions shall be followed by all members:
• Do not smoke
• Do not accept identification markings as legitimate
• Do not take for granted that it is an ordinary explosive device; it may be of high explosive or an incendiary
device
• Do not permit radio transmission within close range of the suspect device
Particular attention is directed to the following points in relation to suspect devices:
• Do not place in water. Water is a conductor of electricity and it may cause the device to explode. If the
device contains chemicals such as metallic sodium, metallic potassium, calcium phosphate, sodium
peroxide or sulphuric acid, the water may cause ignition or detonation.
• Do not lift or disturb the device. It may be constructed with an anti-lifting or anti-handling ignition.
• Do not cause any vibrations. Vibrations created by forcing doors or banging windows may cause the
device to ignite or detonate.

9

12:31

Page 10

Chapter 12 Bombs - The Garda Response

• Do not attempt to open a suspicious package, object or container. This is strictly the duty of the
EOD personnel.
• Do not cut any ribbon, string or band on the object. This may release the tension of an ignition device
thereby resulting in detonation.
• Do not move the device. Keep the object in the same position as when found.
• Do not remove anything to inspect the contents.

General Actions
• Cut off main electricity supply
• Cut off main gas supply
• Cut off main oil supply, if any
• Remove flammable materials, if possible, from the vicinity

12.6. Radio Communications
During any search for a device all Garda radios and mobile phones will be kept on. Members should be aware
that if radios are operated near to an explosive device there is some risk the device may detonate.
Handheld personal radios and mobile phones must not be used to transmit information within 50 metres of
any suspected explosive device. Radios fitted in vehicles must not be used to transmit information within 100
metres of any suspected explosive device. Radio pagers that receive only, present no risk.
The danger posed by use of communication equipment is limited to the area of the suspect device and a
balance should be maintained between this limited risk and the requirement for communications.
No communications system is absolutely secure from ‘eavesdroppers’. This should be borne in mind when
wording any transmission, including cellular telephone conversations that may contain sensitive information.

Inhibition of radio systems
Some devices are designed to function in response to a radio signal. EOD personnel may be equipped with a
means to inhibit radio signals in the area of a suspect object. This equipment will not normally inhibit Garda
communications but mobile phones and other public access system may be inhibited for a distance of 300
metres around the control point.

12.7. Action When a Device is Discovered
Suspected IEDs when found by a Garda must be brought to the attention of the Senior Garda in charge at
the scene.

Evaluations
Not every object found is to be treated as suspicious. The first member at the scene, after consideration
should make this decision, even if a member of the public already considers it suspicious. It is not an easy
decision and requires the assessment of all available information. The following should be considered in
arriving at such a decision:

• The source of the information, including a full description of the object
• The location
• The time the object/vehicle is discovered
• Anyone who may be on the premises or about to visit, especially VIPs
Question to be asked:
• Are sensitive, vulnerable or diplomatic premises involved?
• Have steps been taken to identify the owner of the property?
• Has a PULSE check been carried out on any vehicle involved?
• Has the person reporting incident or any witnesses been questioned?
• Local knowledge. Did or does a prominent person live or work nearby?
• Is the date of the incident significant? For example, a particular anniversary, part of a campaign, high
profile criminal trials in process?
• Has there been a specific threat to that location or premises?
• Does it follow a recent trend of genuine devices and locations?
• Have there been recent hoax threats to the premises?
• Has any attempt been made to hide the item?
• What is the current terrorist threat level?
• Is this a distraction and/or an element of a crisis elsewhere?
For some of the above information the member in charge will rely on the Communications Centre for advice.

Vehicles
If a vehicle is involved, the following points should be considered as a matter of urgency, particularly if a time is
given for detonation:
• Can anything suspicious be seen in the vehicle?
• Is it apparently overweight?
• Who owns the vehicle?
• What is the age of the vehicle?
• Is it a terrorist profile type of vehicle?
• If a commercial vehicle, are there any unusual wires from the cab to the back?
• Does it show any sign of structural alterations?
• Has it been adapted in any way for a gas or spray?

When, after a quick but thorough investigation, Gardaí cannot confirm that an object is innocent then the only
option is to assume the article is an IED and inform the Senior Garda on duty in the District and the
Communications Centre. No other viewing of the item should take place once that decision is made.

Senior Garda Member
The Senior Garda present at the scene will assume the role of Scene Commander and co-ordinate the
following actions:
• Requesting the attendance of the Army EOD Team
• Establish a Command Centre
• Establish a Rendezvous Point (RVP)
• Request Communications Centre to alert the other emergency services and local authority services
• Consult existing contingency plans, including the Major Emergency Plan
• Set up cordons with appropriate authority
• Consider setting up press assembly points / media centre in liaison with the Garda Press Office

Reporting Results
It is a matter for the EOD Commander at the scene to declare whether an object is safe. In this event, entries
will be made in the relevant logs and records. The PULSE Incident Record should be updated accordingly.

12.8. Evacuation
The decision regarding whether or not to evacuate will be made by the senior Garda present in consultation
with a responsible person within the premises or the head of security. Equally, it may be necessary for Gardaí
to advise that the premises should not be fully evacuated, for example, where Gardaí have reason to believe
that there may be an explosive device outside the building and full evacuation could place people at
greater risk.
It is vital that all Gardaí are aware that evacuation can mean the movement of persons from one area in a
building to another and does not necessarily mean the emptying of people from a building to beyond a
cordoned area. The purpose of evacuation is to move people from an area where they might be at risk to a
place where that risk is reduced.
When the decision to evacuate has been taken, everyone should be evacuated to a safe place as soon as
possible. This place should be checked in advance to ensure that it is safe. All individuals should be cleared to
a distance which is deemed to be safe; this may range from about 150 to 300 metres depending on the size of
the anticipated device and other factors. Even at this distance, serious injury may be caused by flying debris
and glass. Once evacuated, the area must be cordoned off and no unauthorised persons permitted to enter.

Stages of Evacuation
• Internal evacuation to a safe area
• Partial evacuation where large premises are involved and a small device is found
• Full evacuation

Members directing evacuation should exercise great care when the precise location of the suspect device is
unknown. Remember, you could be evacuating people directly into the site of an explosion!
In addition, if there is a CBRN element then wind direction will be a factor in the direction of the evacuation.
Making the public aware of a threat may include:
• Gardaí going from door to door
• Gardaí speaking to people in the street
• Use of a public address (PA) system on official vehicles
• Telephoning vulnerable premises
• Any other means of communication that individual circumstances may require or dictate such as local radio
or the Internet
All traffic and pedestrians should be diverted at a safe distance from the suspect device. The dangers posed to
local public transport services such as rail, aircraft and water transport should not be overlooked in this
context. The establishment of a safe access and egress route to facilitate emergency vehicles should also be a
priority. Emergency vehicles will include:
• Fire brigade
• Ambulance
• EOD team
• ESB
• Gas company
• Water works

Safe Areas
It is for the head of security, who is responsible for safety within a private building, to make the decision to use
the safe areas within that structure. Only in exceptional circumstances, and for the most cogent reasons,
should a Garda order people to leave the safe area against the advice of the head of security for those
premises. Where a Garda encounters a head of security who has evacuated people to a bomb shelter, that
member should note:
• The name and position of the head of security within the organisation
• The number of persons remaining in the building and the reasons why these persons remain
• The briefing of members attending the scene

12.9. Evacuation Plans (planning advice for private heads of
security)
The head of security should have up-to-date contingency plans for evacuation. This is especially necessary
when there are a number of exits from the building. Where the public are present, the head of security should
consider using members of staff as ‘evacuation marshals’ to ensure that the public can leave quickly and
without panic by the pre-selected routes. Evacuation marshals need to be selected in advance and trained in
their role.

When an evacuation is initiated, doors and windows, whenever possible, should be left unlocked,
especially in the vicinity of a suspect object. Lights should be left on, but plant and machinery shut down
where practicable.
A method should be devised to check that everyone has left the building after the evacuation, and that all
personnel, staff and visitors are accounted for. They should be instructed not to re-enter until the building is
declared safe. Whenever an evacuation takes place, Gardaí and security personnel should bear in mind the
following useful points:
• Evacuate to an area which has already been checked or at least is considered safe (for example, no
vehicles parked, minimal street furniture and has shelter from flying glass/debris)
• Clear people out and away from the suspect item
• Do not evacuate past the suspect item
• Clear those at most risk first
• Evacuate with a sense of urgency
• Use cover such as rear alleyways and so on
• Special arrangements may need to be made for the disabled
• Wind and weather conditions may be a factor

12.10. The decision to re-occupy
Following an evacuation, the head of security following discussions with the senior Garda at scene will at some
stage have to decide when the building can be re-occupied. Following the discovery of a suspicious object
Gardaí will have assumed control until the object has been declared safe. Thereafter, control will revert to the
head of security.

The head of security should be reminded that there may be another suspicious object somewhere in the
building which remains undiscovered because the search was stopped and the building evacuated due to the
discovery of the first suspect object. The head of security should, therefore, have the rest of the building
searched before considering re-occupation.
It also follows that, where the evacuation was carried out before any search, the head of security will have to
initiate a search before considering the question of re-occupation. Where the time has been given for an
explosion, but no explosion occurs, the advice to the head of security should be to ensure that at least one
hour is allowed to lapse after that time before search procedures are initiated or re-commenced.

The EOD officer will only attend following a request of the Senior Garda Officer in the District concerned. This
member will make that decision after consideration of all information.
When the EOD callout is requested the contacting member must be able to relay the following information:

• The exact location of the RVP and approach route. (In rural areas, particularly, a map should be employed
to pinpoint the location)
• Routes and alternative routes to the immediate area
• Rendezvous point for Garda/Army - minimum 50 metres away.
• Exact positions of members of An Garda Siochana
• Suggested time for meeting
• Is the site a commercial premises/private residence? Obtain details of the ownership or tenancy if
available. Is the premises occupied or unoccupied?
The Device/Threat:
• The time of any threat calls
• The time notified to Garda
• Time of discovery
• The exact location and a description of the suspected improvised explosive devise (IED). For example, size,
description and make of any case, bag or box
• Whether the device has been moved or touched
Measures Taken:
• Safety measures taken - cordoning, evacuation etc
• Instructions given by Gardaí to civilians and if these instructions have been carried out
• Evacuation details (i.e. cordon requested - minimum 100 metres)

Other Relevant Information:
• Special circumstances surrounding the incident (e.g. sensitive premises / VIPs nearby) or particular historical

• Suspects, giving any useful background information

• Level of Garda involvement i.e. armed/plain clothes/uniformed

Traffic Control and Scene Logistics
The Senior Officer from the EOD Team will take charge of the immediate scene when an object has been
identified or when an explosion has occurred.
Although the Senior Garda member retains overall command of the scene the Senior EOD Officer present,
while acting in aid to the civil power (ATCP), retains responsibility for the operations and the safe disposal of
item(s) until the incident is declared safe.
The Senior Garda Officer at the scene will make appropriate arrangements concerning the arrival of the EOD
team to the RVP. The optimum location should be selected for the RVP which may be some distance away
from the suspect site.
It is important that persons capable of giving accurate descriptions of the suspect device remain at or near the
Control Point to speak to the EOD Team. A civilian witness who actually saw the item can also be included in
the decision making process.
It is recommended that a diagram or drawing be prepared in advance, so that the EOD Commander quickly
receives the best available briefing on arrival. The diagram should, if possible, include the location of the
device, the route to it, its exact position, its appearance and any other relevant information.
If the suspect device is inside a building, all approach doors should be kept open to provide direct access for
the EOD, particularly if there is a need to use specialist equipment. Do not interfere with light switches, and
do not touch anything in the room. Obstructions such as loose carpet, which may hinder specialist equipment
to be used by the EOD Team, should be pointed out.
The Senior Garda Officer should remain at the Command Control Centre when the EOD team arrives at
the scene.
Requests by the EOD to alter cordon distances must be acted upon immediately. The EOD Team may request a
Garda or a witness familiar with the location to view the IED or the approach to the device on close circuit
television to assist them in the performance of their role.

12.12. Cordons
It is the duty of An Garda Síochána to designate and establish cordons within any area they deem fit.
Pedestrians and motorists may be excluded from this area if it appears expedient to do so. The setting up of
any cordon must be attended to as soon as is reasonably practicable.

16

Chapter 12 Bombs - The Garda Response

Cordons are best effected with the standard issued plastic tape and are required to:
• Keep people out of danger before detonation and to minimise the risk after detonation
• Protect emergency service resources from unauthorised access
• Exercise control, for the purpose of preserving the crime scene for example
• Account for personnel (under Health and Safety legislation) by use of a cordon control kit

Pre-Detonation
Make the cordons:
• Clear to all, both Gardaí and public
• Realistic in their management, but at a safe distance
Inner cordons should be erected, using the following minimum distances subject to the size of the device:
• Small items up to briefcase size

- 100 metres minimum

• Large items up to and including cars

- 200 metres minimum

• Vans and heavy goods vehicles (HGVs)

- 400 metres minimum

• Gas or smoke plume

- 1,000 meters in upwind direction

Gardaí are advised, at all times, to stand behind the shelter of a solid building to maximise the protective cover
available and minimise injury from blast, flying glass, projectiles and debris.
Beware of a secondary device
Cordons must be attended by sufficient members to achieve their purpose. Each member should have a
personal radio set, kept on at all times. Gardaí should also be aware of secondary devices which could be left
at cordon locations. If possible, obvious junctions should be avoided until first searched.

Post Detonation - Inner Cordon
If not already in place, put a cordon around the bomb scene at least 150 metres radius from the apparent seat
of the explosion as this must become a ‘sterile area’ for rescue, evidence gathering and safety from dangerous
structures. This cordon may be reduced on the advice of the Technical Bureau Bomb Scene Manager following
consultation with the army EOD Commander on site.

Cordon Control Points
Gardaí are responsible for logging the attendance of their own personnel as well as representatives of other
agencies within the inner cordon/rescue zone, this includes engineers from the utilities agencies and other
investigators. However, Fire and Ambulance Service personnel have their own established system and their
access and egress must be facilitated rapidly.
The Gardaí will appoint a cordon control officer who, on the directions of the senior investigating member and
Communications Centre, will authorise all personnel entering the inner cordon/rescue zone.

Outer Cordon
A second cordon of at least 200 metres radius from the seat of the explosion should be implemented. An area

Chapter 12 Bombs - The Garda Response

between these two cordons will then be identified and designated as the marshalling/holding area and Control
Point to accommodate the emergency and essential services as well as additional Garda personnel.

Traffic Cordon
A third or traffic cordon may be deployed beyond the outer cordon to prevent vehicular access to the area
surrounding the scene. The member designated to co-ordinate traffic management will be responsible for its
implementation.

Access and egress routes
Whenever possible a ‘one-way’ system of access and egress should be implemented to ensure the rapid
attendance of emergency vehicles to the scene and to facilitate the unimpeded removal of casualties to
hospital.

Evacuating the cordon areas
Gardaí must control evacuation from inside the cordon area. Some large buildings have pre-planned
evacuation procedures which must be followed whenever possible. When all injured people have been
removed from the scene and any fire extinguished, everyone must withdraw outside the cordons. The urgency
of this cannot be stressed enough in view of the possibility of secondary devices being present. A stand off
period of at least one hour should follow. All Gardaí must be aware that suspects may still be in the area,
especially if a remote control device has been activated.

Preliminary Survey of Scene by Senior Member
The distances for positioning the cordons are, in the first instance, the responsibility of the first senior member
on scene. It is impossible to give precise distances for installation as each scene will differ and officers must
use their initial scene examination to ensure that, as far as possible, the first cordon encompasses the complete
contaminated/debris area.
After an assessment by members from the Ballistics Section and/or in consultation with the army EOD Team
the extent of the cordon may be varied. It may also be necessary to extend or reduce the first 150 metre
cordon but this should not generally occur until the Senior Garda member has carried out an accurate initial
scene assessment.

Mortar Attack
In the event of a mortar attack, the following minimum distances should be maintained.
Base Plate Danger Area
A cordon of at least 300 metres in radius from the launch site/base plate area should be implemented.
Flight Path Danger Area
A cordon of at least 100 metres each side of the flight path, to include 100 metres in radius to the rear of and
100 metres beyond the flight path.
Impact Zone Danger Area
A cordon of at least 200 metres in radius from the point of impact.

An explosion is a major incident requiring the implementation of special arrangements by one or all of the
emergency services.
In the case of a major incident, the priority is to assess the situation and inform the Communications Centre
immediately. The mnemonic ‘CHALET’ has been devised to help the senior member assess the incident.

12.14. Rendezvous Points (RVPs)
A Rendezvous Point (RVP), under the control of a Garda member, should be established near to the scene
within the outer cordon. All emergency, specialist and voluntary services attending the incident should be
directed to the RVP in the first instance. The most important aspect of setting up the location of an RVP is
ensuring its safety.
The following matters require consideration:
• Is it far enough away from a suspected explosive device?
• Is it out of direct line of sight from the object/scene?
• Is it in such an obvious position that the terrorist may have planted a secondary device there?
• Consider the services of Garda Explosive Search Dogs to ensure that the RVP is safe
• Is it easily accessible to essential personnel?
• Is it easily identifiable as the RVP?
Arriving resources/assistance will be directed to either the Marshalling Area/Holding Area or Control Point
(see below).
Other functions of the RVP include:
• Maintaining a log of incoming Garda resources
• Briefing the Operational Commander upon arrival
• Directing Garda Reserves on standby to the Marshalling Area / Holding Area

12.15. Marshalling Area/Holding Area
This should be located near to the RVP and be actively policed to maintain security. All incoming resources
will initially arrive at the RVP. They will then be directed to the scene, cordons or marshalling area/holding
area, as appropriate. The marshalling area/holding area can also be used to park ‘in use’ vehicles (which
should not be locked), accommodate waiting resources, or be used as a ‘relieving point’ for Gardaí waiting to
be re-deployed.

Vehicles not in use must not be allowed to remain at the incident.
Other functions of the marshalling area / holding area include:
• The briefing of members attending the scene
• Issuing equipment as necessary
Important: Care should be taken to ensure that the locations of the RVP and marshalling area / holding area
do not conflict thereby preventing the efficient deployment of resources.

12.16. Control Point
The emergency services control/command units will form the focus from which the incident will be managed.
These units, together with those of the public utilities and local authority, will be located close to one another
and be known collectively as the Control Point. The Operational Commander will jointly exercise authority
from this point in a co-ordinated manner.
To avoid confusion, where a service mobilises more than one control/command vehicle to the scene, only one
of these will perform the control function at the Control Point. It should be clearly distinguishable from other
similar vehicles and be the only reference point from which the Operational Commander will operate.
Other functions of the Control Point include:
• Logging details of resources deployed to the scene
• Co-ordinating tactical deployments
• Passing resource requests to Communications Centre
• Managing information on behalf of the Operational Commander
• Managing all on scene emergency service communications
