12.18. Immediate Responsibilities of the Operational
Commander
Information and preparation:
• Establish that the EOD officer has sufficient information for his team.
• Consult and utilise any existing contingency plan.
• Establish an RVP.
• Establish a control point known as the Joint Emergency Services Control Centre.
• Establish a co-ordinating group at the scene.
• Establish a vehicle marshalling area / holding area.
• Establish a temporary morgue where there is a large number of fatalities.
• Appoint a member with responsibility for the identification of the dead and injured.
• Establish a reception area where relatives of victims can be met and spoken with by properly
skilled Gardai.
Cordons:
• Implement cordons.
• Establish cordons access control points.
• Appoint a cordon control officer.
Emergency services:
• Liaise with all services such as Gas, ESB and Telecom regarding initiation of Service Mobilisation Plan and
implement Casualty Bureau Procedure if necessary.
Media:
• Contact the Press and Public Relations Office at Garda Headquarters and request a Press Officer be
deployed to the scene.
• Establish a press assembly point / media centre.
Remember: Gardaí may be injured and/or shocked and may need to be evacuated themselves.
Many people and agencies will volunteer assistance following an explosion. Their attendance must be
organised properly for reasons of both personal safety and the necessity to preserve the integrity of the scene.
Only those units that are tasked to attend should be directed to a RVP to receive clear instructions
from the Operational Commander.
A log, if not already started, must be started at once.
Clear, positive and audible instructions by the Operational Commander will greatly help the responding units
to carry out their tasks. In prolonged serious situations, however, Gardaí will only remain effective for a short
time before the severity of the situation affects them. Relief, mindful of the need for continuity of evidence,
must therefore be considered.

Those that are obviously dead must not be moved by anyone and as far as possible no attempt should be

screens are not readily available, are ideal for this purpose. Full details of officers carrying out this task and the
items used must be recorded in the log.
It must always be remembered that the location of an IED is the scene of a serious crime. It is most important
to preserve the legal integrity of all exhibits which must be preserved or vital evidence will be lost.

12.19. Command Structure
As the incident develops the following command structure must be established:

Strategic Commander
This is the senior member in overall command who sets the strategy for dealing with the incident and will
usually be either at Assistant Commissioner or Chief Superintendent rank. The Strategic Commander will not
become personally involved with activities close to the incident but remain detached.

Scene Commander - Senior Garda Officer at Scene
The Scene Commander or Tactical Commander, appointed by the Strategic Commander, will control and
deploy the resources. They will attend the scene, take charge and be responsible for formulating the tactics to
be adopted to achieve the desired goal.
In addition to the EOD Team tasked to the tactical level other EOD Advisors can be requested for the other
command levels if required. This is especially the case for a CBRN Incident.

12.20. Witnesses
If witnesses are asked to remain, they should be separated, if possible, to preserve the validity of evidence.
Likewise, any suspect(s) should be isolated.

12.21. Attendance of Media
Media representatives will obviously attend the scene quickly. They should not be allowed to photograph the
dead or injured or the scene itself in an uncoordinated and unsupervised fashion. A press assembly point /
media centre should be established outside the outer cordon and a member appointed to ensure they remain
at that location until allowed supervised access.
A representative from the Garda Press and Public Relations Office will attend as soon as possible, but Gardaí at
the scene should make no comment regarding the numbers of injured or Garda action in conversation with
press or TV representatives without the prior authority of the Operational Commander.
It should be borne in mind that the probability of television cameras transmitting live pictures may cause
undue distress to the public and may alert other terrorist groups of the precise scene.
In this regard, it is critical to compile an accurate list of the dead and injured. The identification of the dead
and injured is a role which must be attended to immediately and the information passed to the

met/interviewed and a team of Gardaí who have skills in dealing with traumatised persons assigned to

The Operational Commander, in consultation with the Senior Technical Bureau Management, will request the
appointment of a crime scene manager.
The Scene Commander, in consultation with the Army EOD Officer at the scene, will normally request the
following resources unless, because of the time or location of the incident, this has already been done:
• Forensic Medical Examiner, State Pathologist
• Explosive search dogs
• Search teams
• Attendance of Forensic Scientists
• Full Technical Bureau team
• Local Authority surveyors and engineers
• Senior Health and Safety Adviser
• Any other resource considered necessary

12.23. Specific action at the scene of suspected terrorist,
chemical, biological, radiological and nuclear incidents
Lessons learned as a result of the 1995 Tokyo sarin gas attack, as well as a variety of other incidents abroad,
have shaped the response to Chemical, Biological, Radiological and Nuclear (CBRN) incidents. There is now
an accepted multi-agency response to CBRN incidents involving personnel from all branches of the
Emergency Services.

Indicators
Although chemical, biological, radiological and nuclear incidents have been linked together their immediate
effects are quite different. However, these effects could include some of the following:
• Groups of people suddenly collapsing
• Groups of people suddenly feeling unwell
• Strong smell of noxious fumes
• Groups of people experiencing sudden skin blisters
• No logical explanation for casualties, (e.g. nearby chemical plant, accidental spillage)

Initial Action
The primary aim of any suspected CBRN terrorist incident is the protection of life. Gardaí must not endanger
themselves but should call for appropriately equipped assistance through the Communications Centre. The
following Garda and Emergency Service personnel have been specially trained and are equipped to deal with
terrorist related CBRN incidents:
• Gardaí from selected city centre and rural stations
• Technical Bureau
• Fire Brigade
• Ambulance Service
The role of the Gardaí will be the same as at any other major incident.
The Fire Service has the primary responsibility and the equipment for emergency rescue, safety management
and environmental monitoring within the inner cordon until the arrival of other specialist responders. Gardaí
are responsible for co-ordinating the multi-agency response, protection and preservation of the scene,
investigation and evidence gathering. Where safe to do so, Gardaí may assist fire fighters in local evacuation.
Gardaí who are not protected must not enter a potentially contaminated area.
EOD Teams tasked to a CBRN Incident are equipped to deal with limited incidents and would have
to request additional military resources.

12.24. Action at the Scene of Suspected Contaminated Mail
As a result of contaminated mail incidents abroad and at home the following guidelines have been developed
in order that Gardaí respond in a proportionate manner. Most frequently, these incidents have involved
packages purporting to contain anthrax.

Strategy
The overall intention must be to manage the scene safely and obtain effective medical support for victims,
while minimising impact and disproportionate alarm to the local community. This will be delivered through
intelligent and informed leadership, dynamic risk assessment and close liaison with emergency service partners.

Indicators
Anthrax is a bacterial infection of the skin, lungs or intestinal tract. In the most serious instances, it may cause
respiratory and cardiac problems which, if untreated, could prove fatal. Staff must be aware that although
threats have referred to the presence of anthrax, there are a wide range of biological hazards which could be
used to contaminate items of mail.

Considerations of Gardaí attending
The first member at the scene should make the decision on whether it is a contaminated mail incident, even if
a member of the public already considers it suspicious. This is not an easy decision and requires Gardaí to
assess all of the available information. In arriving at this decision, all of the attendant circumstances should
be considered.

Army EOD Units
The Army EOD Units are not the lead agency for a CBRN incident. However, for an initial response every EOD

• To decontaminate a very limited area.
• To decontaminate themselves in conjunction with the Fire Services.
• To evacuate and prepare for safe transportation of small items or samples.
• To draw down additional military resources within six hours.
Note: The Army EOD Teams are not equipped for major decontamination tasks.
In dealing with these incidents, members should consider the following checklist:
• Do not overreact.
• Physical protection, such as personal issue latex gloves and the covering of broken skin will help protect
against skin infection.
• The room in which the item is located should be closed and isolated.

Affected persons:
• Persons who have come into contact with any suspicious substance should be reassured that they are
extremely unlikely to feel any immediate effects if at all and that medical aid will be provided.
• They may be allowed to wash their hands in a weak bleach solution (one teaspoonful of domestic bleach
to one litre of water) and then rinse in ordinary soap and water.
• Try to ascertain how many others may have been exposed to the material – keep a detailed list.
• Victims should be removed to a comfortable room as close to the isolated room as possible in order to
avoid a further spread of potential contamination.
• If either room is connected to an integrated/recycled air conditioning ventilation system, that room or local
system must be shut down.
• All victims or potential victims may be decontaminated or treated with antibiotics, as appropriate.
• There should be no need for further evacuation from the building once the room has been physically and
technically isolated.
About anthrax:
• Anthrax is difficult for criminals/terrorists to deploy efficiently.
• Anthrax is not transmissible between humans.
• It can be easily treated with antibiotics.
• It can appear like many innocent items e.g. brick dust.

12.25. Suicide Bombers
A suicide attack is an attack in which the attacker or attackers intend and expect to die. It may be carried out
by an individual carrying or wearing an IED or driving a vehicle containing an IED. It is important to distinguish
this type of attack from an actual suicide attempt where the individual may try to use explosives or a related
material such as fuel or gas. It is also important to distinguish between a suicide incident and a ‘proxy bomb’
when the individual is an unwilling participant.
Every attempt should be made to contain the incident to as small an area as possible while maintaining a
standoff distance by retreating as necessary.
The Garda members present need to note any behavioural characteristics that may indicate a suicide bomber.
In cases where a suspected suicide bomber has been identified, there may be little time for a co-ordinated
response. There may be circumstances whereby lethal force is justified so an armed response should be
requested immediately.
If the circumstances are such that some members have to hold down the bomber then every effort must be
made to secure the individual in such a fashion that prevents any movement which could detonate the device.
Under no circumstances are tasers or other electrical discharge devices to be utilised against a bomber, as the
charge delivered may detonate the explosive device.
If the individual is suspected of carrying an IED then only the EOD Team should approach to deal with the
suspect device. All the precautions that apply to an IED must also be applied in these cases (see above).

12.26. Pipe Bombs
Pipe bombs are regularly used by Criminals. They
are generally deployed against windows of houses
or cars of persons associated with the drugs trade
in an effort at intimidation or extortion. In all cases
where a suspected pipe bomb is located EOD
should be tasked to render it safe and the area
should be evacuated. The Ballistics Section should
also be notified and advice sought from them.
The safe device should immediately be brought to the Technical Bureau for forensic examination. Advice or
assistance should be sought from Ballistics Section, Garda Technical Bureau. If the Technical Bureau were not
deployed to the scene then the made safe device should be packaged, labelled and submitted to them by
hand for examination.
Any pipe bomb explosion scene should be preserved for examination. If there is any suspicion of a further
device being present then EOD should be tasked to clear the scene and declare it safe.

12.27. Incendiaries
An incendiary bomb is a device which sets fire to property by an intense and sustained heat and flame
concentration. It may ignite spontaneously or after a pre-selected period of time.
These devices are directed towards the destruction of property by burning. They are placed in locations where
they will cause maximum damage. Prime selection points would be clothing and soft furnishing stores, oil or
gas heating systems, main electric supply or areas where flammable chemicals or gases are stored.
These devices contain their own means of ignition, do not require a supply of air and can be very small so they
can be placed in any place and are easily concealed. They are therefore difficult to detect and this should be
borne in mind when searching for them.

The burning element can be liquid, gel, solid or gaseous. Containers of all shapes and sizes are used.
These include:
• Cigarette boxes
• Cassette tape / CD holders
• Plastic lunch boxes
• Small envelopes
• Tin cans
The general guidelines given under Bomb Reports should be adhered to in relation to incendiary devices.

12.28. Investigation of a Bomb Explosion
Members should exercise care in approaching areas where explosives or booby-trapped objects are likely to be
located. EOD personnel should be informed that technical examination for finger-marks and so on will be
necessary after an area has been cleared. They should handle objects only insofar as it is absolutely necessary
to do so, and then with rubber gloves, if possible, in order to minimise damage or erasure of material clues.
The scene should be examined as soon as possible after the explosion. Photographs should be taken and/or
video films made immediately to record the relative effects of personal injury and property damage. A sketch
of the scene should be made and accurate measurements taken to furnish necessary information about
distances and details represented by the photographs and/or video. The sketch should be drawn to scale.
The examination of the scene of the explosion will often
assist the investigating members to arrive at a reliable
conclusion as to the nature of the explosive which caused
the damage. That is whether or not the material was a
high or low explosive type, the quantity of explosive used
and so on. Interviewing witnesses as to the sound of the
explosion, the amount and colour of the smoke and the
impact as evidenced by the buzzing of the ears, may be of
value in corroborating the results of the physical
examination of the scene.
The ‘seat’ of the explosion should be carefully and
methodically examined. The seat is the area where the
explosive was set off. This area is readily ascertained by
the fact that the greatest amount of damage will have
taken place at and around this point.
Fragments or traces of the explosive substance may be found in or near the seat of the explosion. This can
occur, for example, where damp dynamite was used or where discolouration caused by acid or the residue
from burned explosives is present.
Fragments of the bomb may be found in the crater or may be scattered about the immediate vicinity.
The debris should be searched carefully for physical evidence which may give clues as to the type of container

Chapter 12 Bombs - The Garda Response

or exploding mechanism was used. It should be possible to ascertain from the debris if it was mechanically,
electrically or remotely controlled. The evidence may consist of exploded pieces of pipe, a can or other
container, wrapping of cloth, electrician’s tape, rope, wire fragments of burned fuse, fragments of blasting
caps, batteries, clocks, watches and so on.
Anything which is foreign to the immediate surroundings should carefully be collected, marked, wrapped or
tagged with the name and registration number of the member who discovered it. The time, date, exact
position, and other relevant and pertinent facts should be noted on the wrapping or tag.
There should be a diligent search for evidence.
• The investigating members should be able to establish the reason for the explosion from the evidence
at the scene. Was it intended for a person or persons, a physical object or a particular activity? Data
should be collected concerning the victims, personnel regularly in that vicinity, or passing it, or other
persons who would be expected to be in that area at the material time.
• Information concerning the financial position of the persons or property should be gathered. In
particular, it should be established if the persons or property were heavily insured, and if this insurance
was recently introduced. The annual audit time should be ascertained as there may be an attempt to
destroy the books of account in advance of this.
• Individuals who had any form of relationship with the victims or property, or who were seen in the
area, should be investigated. Persons who should have been there and were not should receive special
attention and their explanations checked out. This applies also to persons recently dismissed from a
firm or organisation.

12.29. Suspects
For this particular type of crime a suspect is a person who had the capability of perpetrating it. Such a
person would have the means of committing this crime, the necessary knowledge to utilise the means,
and finally, the opportunity and knowledge of the scene to place the device in position.

Means
The nature and design of the components of the device will establish where they initially came from.

Knowledge of explosives
A suspect’s background should be investigated to establish if they had ever been trained in the use of
explosives or employed by a firm where they were used, or has a close friend who had a similar background.
It should also be established if they possess any special mechanical or electrical knowledge.

Opportunity
The suspect must have access to the area where the device was placed. The ‘opening times’ of the area
will assist the investigating member to establish the period when the device was placed. The nature of the
device will further be of assistance. Was it placed where the public had access or was it placed in an area
where only certain personnel had access to or had knowledge of?
The activities of the suspect should be checked thoroughly in relation to location and time before, during
and after the explosion.

12.30. Search of Suspects
To connect the suspect with this form of crime, a search should be made for evidence of explosive materials
and mechanisms as well as other traces which would establish the presence of the suspect at the scene.
• The person of the suspect should be examined minutely for any form of trace evidence.
• The clothing of the suspect should also receive minute scrutiny for trace evidence or clue materials.
• The suspect’s home, workshop / place of work should be searched. Items that should be sought are:
• Chemicals such as acids and nitrates
• Detonating devices such as fuses and caps
• Explosive materials
• Construction materials such as electric wire, wood, nails, paper, twine, glue and friction tape
• Instruments such as wire cutters, pliers, shears, screw drivers, hack saws, planes and lathes. All dust
and scrapping from these instruments should be individually packaged, labelled, dated, signed by the
finding member for forwarding to the Technical Bureau for examination.
• The suspect’s vehicle should also be examined for any evidence as already outlined above.
The above procedures for searching the scene of an explosion, basic precautions to be observed in respect of
suspect bombs and aids to obtaining evidence are general outlines. Although the investigation or this type of
crime may vary with each case, these general guidelines should be adhered to as closely as possible.

Chapter 13 Theft and Fraud

13.1. An overview of the Criminal Justice (Theft & Fraud
Offences) Act, 2001
On August 1st 2002 the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act, 2001 came into force. This Act
represents the most fundamental reform of the law in relation to property offences since the foundation of the
State. The Act reflects in large part the recommendations of the Law Reform Commission in their report on the
Law Relating to Dishonesty 1992 (LRC 43-1992). An examination of that report is recommended for anyone
wishing to understand better the structure and philosophy of the Act. It can be accessed at the Commission’s
web page (www.lawreform.ie). The following discussion is intended as a general overview of the principal
offences created by the 2001 Act which frequently arise in the investigation of fraud-related complaints.
The overview given below is intended to assist members in investigating crimes created by the Act and offers
some guidance on the ingredients necessary to establish the various offences based on the existing case law
available. The precise scope of many of the offences will not become clear until there are relevant decisions
handed down by the Irish Superior Courts.
Members should consult the various sections for
guidance on powers of arrest and detention where a
penalty of less than five years imprisonment is provided
and accordingly outside the definition of an ‘arrestable
offence’ within the meaning of the Criminal Law Act,
1997 and not subject to the powers of detention as
created by the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 as amended.
It should be noted that the definition of an arrestable
offence within the Criminal Law Act, 1997 includes an
attempt to commit the substantive offence intended, for
example attempted theft or attempted deception.
Accordingly where such an attempt is suspected members may resort to the powers of arrest and detention
available for the substantive offence. It should be remembered, however, that attempts to commit crimes are
usually charged as offences contrary to the common law. It should also be noted that the definition of
arrestable offence as amended by the Criminal Justice Act, 2006 extends to common law offences which carry
the prescribed minimum penalty.

13.1.1. Important Definitions
“Appropriates” in relation to property, means usurps or adversely interferes with the proprietary rights of the
owner of the property.
“Depriving” means temporarily or permanently depriving.
“Dishonestly” means without a claim of right made in good faith.
“Document” includes: A map, plan, graph, drawing, photograph or record, or a reproduction in permanent
legible form, by a computer or other means (including enlarging), of information in non-legible form.
“Property” means money and all other property, real or personal, including things in action and other
intangible property.
“Unlawfully obtained” means obtained in circumstances constituting an offence, and cognate words shall
be construed accordingly.

A person “deceives” for the purposes of the Act if they;
• Create or reinforce a false impression, including a false impression as to law, value or intention or other
state of mind
• Prevent another person from acquiring information which would affect that person’s judgement of a
transaction.
• Fail to correct a false impression which the deceiver previously created or reinforced or which the deceiver
knows to be influencing another to whom they stand in a fiduciary or confidential relationship.
References to deception shall be construed accordingly.
“Gain and loss” is widely defined in Section 2(3) as including a temporary gain or loss.
“Ownership” includes possession or control of property for the purposes of the Act (Section 1(4)). This
will often avoid the necessity of proving the existence of corporate owners of property when the manager in
immediate possession of the stolen property can be recited as owner for the purposes of the Act in the
Charge(s)/Indictments preferred against the accused.

13.1.2. Theft - Section 4 and Section 5
The offence of theft is arguably the most important offence created by the Act. It is intended to replace a
whole range of offences under the former Larceny Act, 1916 from Larceny (including simple larceny, larceny
from the person and larceny by clerk or servant) to embezzlement and fraudulent conversion. It is also
intended to replace the former offence of obtaining by false pretences.
The offence is defined as the dishonest appropriation of property without the consent of the owner and with
the intention of depriving the owner of it. There are five principle elements in the offence; Dishonestly,
Appropriates, Property, Consent and Intention to deprive the owner.

Dishonestly
“Dishonestly” means without a claim of right made in good faith (Section 2(1)). In the People [AG] v
Grey, 1944 I.R. 326 and R v Bernard [1938] 2 KB 264 the claim of right made in good faith was held to be one
that need not be recognised in law if the accused honestly believed that the claim was justified. In Grey the
claim of the accused was based on an unenforceable contract but this was held to negate the required
element of fraud. Similarly in the People [DPP] v O’Loughlin [1979] I.R. 85 the belief by the accused that he
was entitled to remove goods in order to enforce payment of a debt which was owed to him was held to be good.
Where it appears to the investigating member that such a defence will arise, a preliminary file should be
submitted to the Law Officers for directions. The file should include full details, supported by relevant
statements outlining where the defence put forward by the suspect will arise.
Cases of this nature can frequently arise in situations where companies incorporated under the Companies
Acts have gone into receivership or liquidation and unpaid creditors take possession of goods which were
supplied to the company while trading. Similarly, it frequently arises in other civil disputes where there is a
genuine dispute as to the ownership of the property at issue. The facts should be established in such cases as
outlined above and directions obtained before undertaking an exhaustive investigation.

Appropriates
This is defined as “… in relation to property, means usurps or adversely interferes with the
proprietary rights of the owner of the property…”(Section 4 (5)). This is a much broader definition than
under the Larceny Act, 1916. It is not necessary to prove that the accused physically ‘takes and carries away’ as
was previously required.

Consent
An essential element of the offence is that the accused appropriates the property without the consent of the
owner. Section 4(2) makes it clear that a person does not appropriate property without the consent of the
owner if they believe that they have the owner’s consent or that the owner would have consented if the
owner had known of the appropriation and the circumstances surrounding it or where the person appropriates
the property in the belief that the owner cannot be discovered by taking reasonable steps. The subsection
goes on to state that consent obtained by deception or intimidation is not consent for those purposes.
Section 4(3) contains certain statutory presumptions where property is held in trust for or on behalf or more
than one owner. The presumption is triggered where the accused appropriates some of the property, provided
that the appropriation was for their own benefit. When properly raised, the presumption is that the
appropriation was without the consent of the owner.
An important aspect of these presumptions relates to cases where it is proved that there is a deficiency in the
property or a sum representing it in the hands of the accused and they have failed to provide a satisfactory
explanation for the whole or any part of the deficiency. A presumption can arise in these circumstances that
the accused appropriated without the consent of the owner the whole or any part of the deficiency.
Under the previous law, it was necessary to prove the
ownership of mixed funds in the hands of the accused
precisely to individual owners. In cases such as
investment frauds where it was proved that ten people
invested €10,000 each but the accused was found to be
in possession of only €40,000, it was then impossible to
prove which investors had been defrauded. This
presumption was drafted to address this anomaly.

Property
“Property means money and all other property, real or personal, including things in action and other
intangible property”. This definition is contained at Section 2(1) of the Act. This definition while very wide is
limited by Section 5(2). The principal exclusion states that a person cannot steal land, or things forming part
of land and severed from it by or under their directions. An exception to that exception includes a trustee or
personal representative authorised to sell or dispose of land. The section also contains an exception in relation
to the picking of mushrooms or the stealing of wild creatures.
Intention to deprive the owner
Depriving is defined in Section 4(5) as meaning temporary or permanent deprivation.

13.1.3. Making Gain or Causing Loss by Deception
This is a new offence created by the 2001 Act. The offence is committed where the accused dishonestly with
the intention of making a gain for themselves or another or of causing a loss to another by deception induces
another to do or refrain from doing an act. The term ‘dishonest’ means, as already noted, the absence of a
claim of right made in good faith. The requirement that there be an intention of making a gain for oneself or
causing a loss to another would capture many business transactions. To induce another to do or refrain from
doing an act is also a typical feature of business transactions. Accordingly, the two critical elements of the
offence which will distinguish criminal from non-criminal conduct will be the absence of a claim of right
made in good faith and the requirement that there be a deception.
Deception is defined in Section 2(2) as meaning one of three possibilities. First, the creation or reinforcement
of a false impression, including a false impression as to law, value or intention or other state of mind.
Secondly, the prevention of another person from acquiring information which would affect that person’s
judgement of a transaction. Thirdly, failing to correct a false impression which the deceiver previously created

Whereas the 2001 Act does not create a general offence of fraud, Section 6 comes close to a general offence

There are very many business transactions where one party would prevent the other from acquiring
information which could affect the other’s judgement of that transaction and certainly the creation or
reinforcing of a false impression as to intention or state of mind would not be unusual. The application of the
concept of claim of right made in good faith to business transactions may not be easy. The expression can be
more easily applied to a circumstance where property passes from one to the other and the person receiving
the property asserts a claim of right to that property.
For example, if an auctioneer or estate agent is accused of inducing another person to purchase property,
having created a false impression as to value, where does the question of claim of right made in good faith
arise? It remains to be seen where the courts will draw the line between sharp business practice and criminal
conduct properly captured by section 6.

13.1.4. Obtaining Services by Deception - Section 7
This section creates a new offence of obtaining services by deception which addressed a major omission in the
old offence of False Pretences contained in the Larceny Act, 1916 where it was not possible to ‘steal’ a service.
The wording of Section 7 mirrors that of section 6. The offence requires that the accused acted dishonestly
with the intention of making a gain for themselves or another or of causing loss to another. The obtaining
must also be by deception. However, instead of the requirement that the accused induce another to do or
refrain from doing an act the offence occurs where the accused obtains services from another.
This divergence between the two sections is narrowed when the term ‘services’ is defined in subsection 2. For
the purpose of the section a person obtains services from another where the other is induced to confer a
benefit on some other person by doing some act or causing or permitting some act to be done on the
understanding that the benefit has been or will be paid for. So, section 6 involves the inducing of another to
do or refrain from doing an act, whereas section 7 involves inducing another to confer a benefit on
some person.
While the Irish courts have not as yet decided any cases on this section, the following services are considered
to be examples which will come within the section based on UK case-law dealing with a similar provision of
the UK Theft Act; (a) board and lodgings (b) social, sporting and entertainment amenities (c) repairs and
decorating and (d) letting of goods on hire and other situations of employment and services rendered for
payment.
The general definition of obtaining services is qualified in subsection 3, where it is stated that without
prejudice to the general words used in subsection 2 the obtaining of services includes inducing another to
make a loan or to cause or permit a loan to be made. This section could apply to cases where a suspect
provides false information to obtain loan facilities from financial institutions.

13.1.5. Making off without Payment - Section 8
Section 8 creates an offence of making off without payment. It is intended to cover situations such as a
person leaving a restaurant or hotel without paying for a meal or the hotel bill. The first requirement is that
the accused knew that payment ‘on the spot’ was required for any goods obtained or any service provided
was required or expected. The term ‘on the spot’ is somewhat colloquial but it is regarded as reasonably
well understood.

The next element of the offence is that the actions of the accused were dishonest, in that they were made
without a claim of right made in good faith. This would of course address the situation where a person left
without paying for a meal because of some genuine consumer complaint. The third element of the offence
is that the accused made off without having paid as required or expected and with the intention of
avoiding payment.
It should be remembered that Section 8(2) creates an exception where the supply of the goods or the doing of
the service is contrary to law or where the service done is such that payment is not legally enforceable.
Examples here would include prostitution services or illegal gambling debts.

13.1.6. False Accounting - Section 10
The new offence of false accounting set out in Section
10 is drawn far more widely than the offence in the
Falsification of the Accounts Act 1875 (now repealed).
The old offence was limited to clerks or servants and
related to uttering or making a false entry or the
omission of a particular materiality.
Section 10 of the 2001 Act is not restricted to an
employee – it covers anyone who does certain things (or
fails to do them). As with other sections of the Act the
omission must be done dishonestly, that is without a claim of right made in good faith and with intent to
make a gain for oneself or another or of causing a loss to another.
The section prohibits:
• The destruction, defacing, concealing or falsifying of any account or document made or required for any
accounting purpose. (See the definition of “document” referred to above.) Of particular note here is the
expression “…made or required for any accounting purpose…” This is a very wide definition and can
apply to almost any document or account used for the purpose of accounting (Attorney General’s
Reference No. 1, 1980 Court of Appeal where it was decided that a personal loan proposal form to a
finance company containing false particulars came within the ambit of a similar section in the UK Theft
Act, 1968).
• The failure to make or complete an account of such document;
• The production or use of any account or any such document which to the person’s knowledge is or may be
misleading, false or deceptive in a material particular.
There is no limitation as to who can commit the offence. A person is treated as falsifying an account or
document if they make or concur in making a false entry or omit or concur in omitting a material particular.
Therefore an accountant who presents a set of accounts which to their knowledge includes or omits such
information is guilty of the offence. The question for the prosecution is whether it is with the intention of
misleading a person or persons or for a gain for the accountant or company or for a loss to another such as
the Revenue Commissioners.

13.1.7. Suppression of documents - Section 11
This is a new section created by the Act. The elements of the offence are acting dishonestly with the intention
of making a gain for themselves or another or of causing a loss to another. It prohibits the destruction,
defacing and the concealment of:

13.1.8. Possession of certain articles – Section 15
This section covers the situation where the accused is not at their place of residence and has possession of
any article with intent to commit any of the following offences -15(1):
• Theft
• Burglary
• Robbery
• Making a gain or causing a loss by deception (Section 6)
• Obtaining services by deception (Section 7)
• Blackmail, extortion and demanding money with menaces (Section 17 Criminal Justice (Public Order) act,
1994
• Taking a vehicle without authority (Section 112 Road Traffic Act 1961 as amended)

Possession
The concept of possession has two elements; knowledge of and control over the suspect items. Manual
possession is not necessary; it is sufficient if the accused has such control over the items that they would be
forthcoming if they ordered it (Archibold 36th. ed. Para 2096). Constructive possession applies where the
suspect item is in the actual possession of some other person over whom the accused has control so that it
would be available to them if they wanted it (Minister for Posts and Telegraphs v Campbell [1966] I.R. 69 at
73). This case is also authority for the proposition that “…normally speaking, a person can properly be
said to be in possession of the contents of his own dwelling house, but only if he is aware of what
it contains…” (per Davitt P.ibid).

The Mental Element
It is necessary for the prosecution to establish the requisite intention on the part of the accused in 15(1).
Section 15(2) (amended by section 47, Criminal Justice Act, 2007) provides a defence to a charge under 15(1)
to prove that at the time of the alleged offence, the article was not in their possession for a purpose specified
at Section 15(1).
So where a suspect is found in possession of equipment adapted to ‘skim’ ATM cards an excuse put forward
that they were developing a security system for an intended business venture (swipe card security system)
could be seen as reasonable on their part if there is evidence to support the story. It should be noted also
that possession of these items in the suspect’s own residence is not an offence under this section.
Therefore, members investigating breaches of section 15 should not simply rely on the fact that the accused
was found in possession of certain suspect articles. Account should be taken of the ingredients necessary to
establish possession in law and the intention to commit one of the offences specified. The DPP has directed
(H.Q.177/03) that the onus of proof under Section 15(1) is a heavy one and while there is consent for
summary disposal without the submission of a file to the DPP, care should be taken in charging this offence to
ensure that there is evidence to establish the requisite intention.
Accordingly, the manner in which the accused was found in possession of the suspect article will be of critical
importance. If for instance, the accused produced the suspect article when questioned by Gardai, this would
establish possession on their behalf. Often the facts of individual cases will prove intention on behalf of the
accused, for example where an employee of a retail outlet can give evidence that the accused produced a
bogus credit card to pay for goods.
It is important that the full circumstances surrounding the accused’s possession of the articles in question
should be fully investigated. Of particular importance will be any evidence as to the suspect’s state of
knowledge of the suspect articles and the fact that they can be or have been designed for the criminal use
suspected. Any explanation or excuse put forward by the suspect should be carefully noted after caution and
this excuse should be fully investigated. The circumstances of each individual case will of course dictate the
nature of the enquiries required.
Members could also consider the terms of Section 30 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2007 regarding inferences
which can be drawn from a failure of an accused person to mention certain facts which are subsequently
relied upon by them in their defence. It is recommended that resort should only be made to this section after
the accused has been invited to make a voluntary statement of admission and all the indications are that the
accused intends to avail of their right to silence. The invocation of section 30 at an early stage of questioning
could cast serious doubts on the voluntariness of any admissions which might be made subsequent to the
invocation of the section.
Members should also keep the terms of section 29(4) of the Criminal Justice (Theft & Fraud Offences) Act,
2001 in mind in appropriate cases – see below at 13.1.12.

Application
This offence should be kept in mind in cases of sophisticated ATM frauds where either a ‘Lebanese Loop’ – a
device which retains an ATM card after it is introduced onto a cash machine to be retrieved by the offender
later for the purposes of committing theft, or in the case of other sophisticated devices which are placed at
ATM machines for the purposes of defrauding the bank and the relevant customers (card skimmers, false
fronts, miniature cameras and so on). Indeed, the offence has a very wide application in the investigation of
fraudulent crime generally including credit card fraud, the production of false driving licences and other
identity documents for use in the commission of fraud or the interception of a victim’s personal identity
information (identity theft) for use in fraudulently withdrawing funds from the victim’s bank account(s) or
obtaining fraudulent loans in the victim’s name.

13.1.9. Handling Stolen Property - Part 3, Criminal Justice (Theft & Fraud Offences)
Act 2001
Section 17 creates the offence of handling stolen property. There are a number of elements to this offence.
First, the prosecution must prove that there has been a handling. The most usual way in which the property
can be handled is by it being received by the accused. It is also handling if the accused undertakes or assists in
its retention, removal, disposal or realisation by or for the benefit of another person. It is also an offence if they
arrange to do all of the above.
The section contains the limitation that the handling must be otherwise than in the course of stealing. Thus,
an accused who receives property in the course of stealing cannot be guilty of handling – in other words the
handler must be other than the thief (People Ag) v Carney and Mulcahy [1955] I.R. 324). This is of course
subject to the exception that the thief may also be guilty of handling if they undertake or assist in the retention,
removal, disposal or realisation of the property on behalf of another person. This rarely happens in reality.
The second element of the offence is that the property is stolen property. Section 20 of the Act is important in
understanding what is or is not stolen property. First, the stolen property can be stolen before or after the
commencement of the Act (August 1st 2002) or in or outside the State, provided that in the latter case the
stealing constituted an offence where and at the time the property was stolen.
Section 20 also makes clear that the phrase stolen property can be applied to property which represents the
proceeds of the disposal or realisation of the original property stolen.
Finally, it should be carefully noted that
section 20(3) excludes from the concept of
stolen property, property which has been
restored to the owner or other lawful
possession or custody. This has been held to
include possession by the police. This fact
should be kept in mind when maintaining
surveillance on stolen property in order to
catch the thief recovering it. In such cases,
as the stolen property is effectively in the
possession of An Garda Siochana, it is no
longer stolen property within the meaning
of Part 3 of the Act and consequently, the
thief cannot be guilty of handling it.
The third element of the offence is that the accused knew that the property was stolen or was reckless as to
whether it was stolen. Section 17 (2) amplifies circumstances in which recklessness on the part of the accused
can be presumed. For example, if a person receives property in such circumstances that it is reasonable to
conclude that the person either knew that the property was stolen or was reckless as to whether it was stolen,
they are taken to have so known or to have been so reckless, unless the Court or jury is satisfied that there is a
reasonable doubt in the matter. No guidance on the precise scope of this presumption has been laid down by
the Superior Irish Courts to date.

13.1.10. Possession of Stolen Property - Section 18
Section 18 creates a new offence of possession of stolen property, without lawful authority or excuse,
knowing that it was stolen or being reckless as to whether it was stolen. It is to be contrasted with the offence
of handling in that it can be a continuous offence. It has some importance in relation to property stolen
abroad. If an accused person receives stolen property in England and then travels to Ireland it may not be
possible to prosecute him here for handling stolen property. One of the necessary proofs in handling is to

of an offence under section 18 of being in possession of the property at the time and place where the offence
is detected by An Garda Siochana. Section 18 (3) states that the accused may be tried and convicted whether
or not the principal offender has been previously convicted or is amenable to justice. In this context, the
principal offender means the person who actually stole the property.

13.1.11. Withholding Information Regarding Stolen Property - Section 19
This section relates to an offence of stealing property or of handling stolen property. A member acting under
this section may require a suspect to give an account of how they came by the stolen property in question.
Before making the requirement the member has to have reasonable grounds for believing that a particular
offence of stealing or handling had been committed and that the person was found in possession of the stolen
property. Prior to making the requirement the member must have reasonable grounds for believing that the
property relates to the offence of stealing or handling and has to inform the suspect of this belief.
The member must also inform the suspect in ordinary language of the effect of a failure or a refusal to comply
with the requirement.
The section creates an offence which consists of a failure or refusal, without reasonable excuse, to give such an
account or to give information that the person knows to be false or misleading. It should be noted that
subsection 4 makes it clear that any information given under the section is not admissible against the person
or their spouse in any criminal proceedings, other than proceedings for a refusal or failure or for giving false or
misleading information.
It will be noted that this is a summary offence with maximum penalties fixed appropriately to such offences.
Members intending to resort to this section should only do so at the end of any period of detention or other
questioning of the suspect. Any legal caution given to the accused should also be withdrawn. Should this
section be invoked and the suspect were subsequently to make admissions regarding theft or handling of
property (or indeed, any other offence), it is most unlikely that a court would admit any such admissions in
evidence as the suspect could claim that these admissions were not voluntary as the result of the invocation of
section 19.
Accordingly, this section should only be invoked after it has been decided not to pursue the investigation of
any other more serious offences any further and at the final stages before either the release of the prisoner or
the conclusion of questioning - See further at Appendix II Section 4.

13.1.12. Forgery - Sections 24-31
Prior to the enactment of the Criminal Justice (Theft & Fraud Offences) Act, 2001, the law on forgery was
contained in the Forgery Act, 1913 (now repealed). The 1913 Act created three offences; forgery, uttering and
obtaining. It defined forgery as the making of a false document in order that it may be used as genuine. Part
4 of the 2001 Act adopts an approach that the essence of forgery is the making of a false document intending
that it be used to induce a person to accept it and to act on the message contained in it as if it were a genuine
document. It will be noted that the 2001 Act introduced the term “instrument” with a very wide definition
which now means that there is a wide extension in the items which can be forged.
Section 25 provides:

“(1) a person is guilty of forgery if he or she makes a false instrument with the
intention that it shall be used to induce another person to accept it as genuine
and, by reason of so accepting it, to do some act, or to make some omission, to
the prejudice of that person or any other person.”

Section 25 defines forgery as the making of a false instrument. Instrument is described as including any
document whether formal or informal. Section 24 lists seventeen examples of instruments but the use of the
words “…includes any…” prior to this list indicates that this list is not exhaustive. Document is of course
widely defined in the Act to include, for example, computer records or information on microfilm, micro-fish,
magnetic tape or disc.
The documents listed at Section 24 include any money order, postal order, postage stamp, cheque,
including a traveller’s cheque or bank draft, charge card, cheque card, credit card, debit card or any
card combining two or more of the functions performed by such cards, share certificate, certified
copy of a birth certificate, marriage certificate or death certificate, a certificate of insurance, a
passport, a Garda Registration Certificate and a ticket of admission to an event.
Other documents which come within the meaning of the section include a driving licence and a bail bond. In
this regard, the DPP has directed that the act of signing a bail bond in a false name amounts to an offence
under section 25 (Forgery) of the Act.
Prejudice has a specific meaning as defined at Section 31 of the Act. An act or omission intended to be
induced shall be to a person’s prejudice if, and only if:
• Section 31 (1) (a) – will result in the temporary or permanent loss of property, the deprivation of an
opportunity to earn remuneration or greater remuneration or the deprivation of an opportunity to gain a
financial advantage otherwise than by way of remuneration
• Section 31 (1) (b) – will result in another person being given an opportunity to earn remuneration or
greater remuneration or to gain a financial advantage
• Section 31(1) (c) - will be the result of their having accepted any false instrument as genuine or any
copy of it in connection with their performance of any duty
Section 31(3) refers to inducing a person to accept a false instrument as genuine or a copy of a false
instrument as a copy of a genuine one includes references to inducing a machine to respond to the
instrument or copy as if it were a genuine instrument or copy of a genuine one. This would include the use of
a credit card to obtain money from a machine.
Section 26 prohibits the use of a false instrument with the intention to induce another person to accept it as
genuine and by reason of so accepting it to do some act or make some omission or provide some service to
the prejudice of that person or any other person. As noted above ‘prejudice’ includes a situation where a
person accepts a false instrument in the performance of any duty. Cases such as the production of a false
driving licence to a Garda on road traffic duties or a false passport to a member on immigration duties are
examples in this context. Another example is using a false passport, driving licence or other identity document
to open a bank account.
Section 27 prohibits the making of a copy of a false instrument, which a person knows or believes to be a
false instrument with the intention that it shall be used to induce another person to accept it as a copy of a
genuine instrument.
Section 28 prohibits the use of a copy of a false instrument with the intention of inducing another person
to accept it as a copy of a genuine instrument and to do some act or make some omission or to provide some
service to the prejudice of that person.
Section 29 (1) prohibits a person having in their custody or in their control certain false instruments intending
to pass them as genuine thereby causing loss to another.
Subsection (2) creates an offence where a person has custody or control of such false instruments without
lawful authority or excuse.

Subsection (3) creates an offence where a person has in their custody or under their control any machine,
stamp, implement, paper or any other material, which has been specially designed or adapted for use in

Application
Section 29 has been found to be a most useful provision in the investigation of fraud-related crime. The
section not only provides for the offence of possession of the instruments themselves but also of items used to
make or procure such an instrument. So, the possession of computers used in the making of counterfeit credit
cards or the possession of cloned credit cards falls within the meaning of this section. Similarly, articles used in
the production of counterfeit admission tickets, counterfeit passports or driving licences also come within the
meaning of the section.

13.2. Fraud
Experience in Ireland and elsewhere has shown that fraudsters come from all walks of life. There are no
indications that family background or personality type have a bearing on who commits fraud. The fraudster is
often a plausible and likeable person who gains the confidence of their victim.
Experience also shows that the biggest frauds are committed by trusted people. Persons of dubious character
draw upon themselves a degree of attention which means that they are often subject to close scrutiny,
particularly in the work environment. Trusted people and employees are generally not subject to such scrutiny.
The Garda Bureau of Fraud Investigation (GBFI) is mentioned throughout this chapter. For further details of the
role and function of the Bureau see Section 13.20 below.

13.4. Preventing Fraud within Organisations
Awareness that fraud can happen is a significant first step in preventing fraud. Like any other business risk, it
requires a plan to identify risk areas and for controls to be put in place to address these risks. There are three
broad areas of risk which need to be addressed:
• Personnel
• Structural/Physical
• Operational/Financial

Personnel
Many frauds are committed by persons employed within the organisation. Often the fraud is only discovered
when the fraudster is called away unexpectedly.
The initial opportunity to reduce the risk of fraud being committed by employees is at the recruitment stage.
Appendix 1 to this chapter sets out a list of steps that can be taken at the recruitment stage to reduce the risk
of fraud by employees.

12

Structural/Physical
The structure of the organisation may lead to an increased risk of fraud. For example, if an organisation has a
number of warehouses or remote locations away from central management this may increase the risk of fraud
as often these locations are given greater autonomy to enable them operate effectively.
In particular, physical access controls play an important role in fraud prevention. Restricting access to cash,
goods, stores, computers, documents and the premises are a critical element in reducing the risk of fraud. For
example, physical access may be controlled by means of swipe cards.

Operational/Financial Risks
Some organisations are more susceptible to fraud than others. Cash based businesses, for example, generally
carry a higher risk of misappropriation or fraud.
A robust and comprehensive system of controls over the accounting and financial transactions of an
organisation is an essential element in fraud prevention. Controls provide the framework within which the
organisation operates as well as protection against abuses of its profit-making system. For example, if an
organisation deals in stock which is widely marketable, control measures should be put in place to ensure that
both the physical stock and the corresponding accounting records relating to them are protected. Ideally, avoid
having one person responsible for both the management of physical stock and for updating the stock records.

13.5. Warning Signs that the Risk of Fraud Could be High
Management:
• Not knowing your business partner; for example where a mobile phone number or an email address
is your only point of contact
• ‘Larger than life’ personalities with a tendency towards autocratic behaviour
• Frequent changes of professional advisers
Staff:
• Lavish lifestyle out of keeping with the person’s known resources
• Customers who, for no apparent reason, won’t deal with anyone other than a particular member of staff
• Employees not taking holidays
• Sudden changes in work habits / lifestyle
• Extensive and unnecessary foreign travel
• Unexplained swings in cash business completed by salesmen or agents
• High turnover of staff
Financial:
• Unexpected drop in margins, or increase in expenses, that cannot be explained
• Unexplained differences between accounting records and third party documentation, for example
suppliers’ statements
• Payments for services (for example to agents or consultants) that appear excessive in respect of
the service provided

Records:
• The same staff keeping records and being responsible for safeguarding the assets recorded.
• Loss of accounting records and / or unavailability of financial information for no good reason
• Inadequately documented cash transactions

13.6. Types of Fraud-related Crime
The expression ‘fraud’ is used as a collective name for a number of statutory and common law offences,
there being no specific offence of ‘fraud’, though there is a common law offence of conspiracy to defraud.
For investigation purposes, fraud is a very relevant term, constantly in use, and very descriptive of the specific
activities whereby an advantage is gained by unfair means - the advantage usually being of a pecuniary
nature and obtained by deceitful means.
Frauds are as varied as human ingenuity and imagination. Schemes by which to defraud are constantly being
thought up and practised by the professional fraudster. The measure of their success is dependent upon the
extent of their victim’s gullibility. Their essential weapons are a combination of confidence, false
representations, pretences, deceits and omissions, all of which they use to exploit the greed, compassion,
sympathy and trust of their victims.
The successful fraudster must have considerable powers of imagination, know how to lie well, have the
ability to seize any opportunity that arises and, above all, must be able to inspire confidence.
Many criminologists believe that fraudsters have no superior intelligence but are endowed with a certain
facility for trickery, deceit, cunning and hypocrisy. They depend on speed of thought, tongue and action to
assist them in their misdeeds. The most successful frauds are not perpetrated by strangers but by persons
well known and trusted by the victim.
Usually more difficulty is experienced in establishing a breach of the law than identifying the culprit. The
most common breaches of the law encountered in the investigation of fraud are:
• Theft
• Handling/Possession of stolen property
• Forgery
• Deception
• Possession of certain articles for use in the commission of offences
• False Accounting
• Money Laundering
• Counterfeiting
• Breaches of the Companies Acts
• Breaches of the Competition Acts
Further details on the various offences outlined are set out elsewhere in this chapter.

13.7. The Investigation of Fraud
Fraud investigation is more an art than a science. Although many of the usual investigative techniques are
helpful, only by experience – on the job training – can one learn to do a really effective job in this very
complicated field.
On initial complaint, the investigating member should obtain an overview of the account being given by the
complainant. In some cases this will require that enquiries are conducted by the Gardai to facilitate a proper
assessment of the complaint being made. At GBFI, a dedicated Assessment Section is in place for this purpose
and the assistance of the members of this unit may be sought if required.
The key issues to determine are whether a breach of the criminal law is disclosed and that evidence is available
to establish this breach against a particular individual. The more serious cases are referred to the GBFI for
investigation.
It is a function of the Detective Chief Superintendent attached to the Bureau to decide on the venue for
investigation in any given case. This may involve the case being investigated exclusively within the Bureau or
alternatively, it may be referred for local investigation with assistance from GBFI personnel. The criteria which
will determine the venue for investigation include the complexity of the complaint made, the value of money
or other property defrauded, the geographical spread of the criminal offences alleged and whether extensive
international enquiries are necessitated.

13.8. Statement of complaint from injured parties
At the outset of an investigation, members should take a detailed statement, with backup documentation,
from the victim. Action should not be taken against a suspect until such a statement is available as it is not
unknown for victims to alter their report after the commencement of proceedings.
Frequently a suspect will reimburse the victim and plead with them for leniency using the same powers of
persuasion as they used to defraud at the outset. Should a member have any doubts about the truthfulness of
a victim’s account, then the victim’s statement should be evaluated carefully and checked for accuracy.

This will help prevent the frequently encountered incidence of injured parties attempting to use Gardai to
influence suspects to reimburse them in cases which, if the complete truth was known, would not be matters
for Garda investigation at all.
However, regard should also be had at the initial stage of the investigation to any urgent action which may be
necessary. For example, where it is suspected that evidence will be destroyed or that the offender will abscond.
All available evidence should be secured at the earliest possible opportunity. This includes evidence from
computers, phones, bank statements or financial records which will now most frequently be maintained in
electronic format. Where urgent action of this nature is contemplated, confirmation at least of the general
nature of the complaint should be put in writing and signed by an appropriate person on behalf of the injured
party or organisation.
Normally, it will be a matter for the financial controller within an organisation to identify what monies have
been stolen by conducting an internal investigation and audit. In smaller organisations, an accountant or
auditor should be in a position to conduct these enquiries. It is not a matter for Garda investigators to conduct
an internal audit of an organisation to determine if and how a fraud has occurred.

As a matter of best practice, an organisation will usually have clearly defined roles and responsibilities for all
staff employed and have appropriate control measures in place to ensure that all monies, stock and other
property is properly accounted for. Elements of internal control include the following:
• Segregation of duties
• Structure of Organisation
• Authorisation and approval
• Physical security
• Supervision;
• Personnel
• Arithmetical and accounting procedures
• Management
The standard of proof in criminal cases is beyond a reasonable doubt and this standard applies to all aspects of
the evidence presented by the prosecution. It is critical that the complainant can establish precisely what has
been stolen and can produce the evidence trail to support the allegations. If control procedures are weak
within an organisation, it may not be possible to establish what monies were defrauded and by whom. Weak
control procedures may create serious impediments in proving the alleged offences. Where it is considered
that such weaknesses exist, it is advisable to submit a preliminary file for directions of the DPP outlining the
nature of the weaknesses identified.
The investigating member should maintain a progress log where all action taken in the investigation is
recorded. Details should be recorded promptly in this log to act as an effective aide memoir when making a
statement of evidence or to address alleged delays in conducting the investigation should judicial review
proceedings be instituted.
When a particular aspect of a fraud is being included in a statement, it will be found helpful to cover:
• When
• Where
• Who was present
• What occurred
This will help to keep matters in proper sequence by pinpointing locations, obtaining names of witnesses so as
to verify the account given together with details of the incident. Experienced personnel find it convenient to
use a check list, tailored to a particular case in interviewing a witness so that essential matters are not
overlooked. In addition, a check list will make note-taking of interviews much simpler.
In addition to listing the key ingredients of the crime under investigation, the check list should remind the
member to note all the basic data, such as full name and address, telephone numbers (business and
residence), occupation, age, background, experience and an appraisal of the victim as a potential
court witness.

It is important for the investigator to remember that ultimately the fraud or breach of the law will have to be
established, not just to the investigator’s satisfaction but to the satisfaction of the Courts, and therefore,
evidence of the essential ingredients of the crime must be obtained and included in the statements taken. An
index should be created at the commencement of the investigation which will record all statements taken in
the course of the investigation.
Where handwriting will be an issue, it is advisable to obtain a control sample of handwriting from the witness
for comparison later at the Documents Section, Garda Headquarters.
Most fraud cases concern the movement of monies in bank accounts which are the subject of the crime under
investigation. In these cases, it is not sufficient to show that a particular cheque has been drawn. Evidence
must be obtained to prove the actual movement of the funds where one account was debited and another
account was credited to the benefit of the accused. This proof will often require the obtaining of Orders from
the Courts to permit the examination of the relevant accounts. Banks will not release this information to
investigating Gardai without such an Order as to do so would breach their duty of confidentiality to their
customers. Additionally, the relevant bank mandate should be examined to confirm the identity of the persons
authorised to operate the account as this may be required in evidence.
Section 52 of the Criminal Justice (Theft & Fraud Offences) Act, 2001 as amended authorises an application to
court for Production Orders in respect of offences created by the Act which carry a minimum five year penalty.
Production Orders are also available under the Bankers’ Books Evidence Act, 1879 as amended and under
Section 63 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1994 as amended. Members must ensure that they comply with the
requirements of the relevant legislative provisions in applying to court for such orders.

13.9. Handling of Documents and Exhibits
The handing over of each document/exhibit should
be included in the witness statement. An identifying
reference number should be allocated to each
document and it should be noted whether the
document is a copy or an original.
If the witness is retaining the original, this fact
should be stated. It should be impressed on the
person retaining possession of the document the
strict rules regarding continuity of evidence and that
the document must be retained in safe custody. An
undertaking should also be obtained to the effect that the witness will produce the original document in court
if and when required. If handing over a copy or if the copy is from a microfiche or computer, it should be
stated that the witness has compared the copy with the original and that it is a true copy.
Investigators should also bear in mind Section 30 of the Criminal Evidence Act, 1992 in relation to the use of
copy documents in prosecutions. Sections 5, 6 and 7 of this Act are also relevant regarding the admissibility of
documentary evidence. It is vital that the continuity of evidence is maintained from the commencement of the
investigation.
Documents are frequently crucial in supporting the evidence of a witness and should be initialled and dated by
the witness for identification purposes in court, bearing in mind that this may only occur some years later.
Investigators should also make a separate note of all documents received, together with time, date and from
whom received. The usefulness of such notes will be apparent should the original document be mislaid or the
witness producing it is unavailable at a trial. It should be remembered that a document cannot ‘speak’ for
itself, so the best witness should be identified to speak about a document.

13.10. Role of Exhibits Officer
Where it is likely that a substantial number of documents and exhibits will be used in evidence in an
investigation, an Exhibits Officer should be appointed. This member will have the following responsibilities:

Maintain a list of all exhibits recovered
The Exhibits Officer must record details of all exhibits recovered in the investigation. The record should include
the Garda exhibit number, a description of the exhibit, the date found, by whom and where found. It is best
practice to appoint an Exhibits Officer at the commencement of each investigation so that correct procedures
are applied from the beginning to the conclusion of the investigation.

Record the movement of all exhibits
Recording the continuity of all exhibits is one of the most important roles of the Exhibits Officer. Any improper
procedures adopted could result in the trial judge directing an acquittal because the exhibits were not
maintained correctly. The Exhibits Officer must know and record the full movement of each exhibit. It is
recommended that all exhibits should be copied. The copies can then be used as working copies. This will
reduce the need for unnecessary handling of exhibits.

Ensuring that all exhibits are correctly labelled
Each exhibit should be assigned an identifying reference number and placed in a clear cellophane envelope. In
the case of original documents or documents required for forensic analysis, the exhibit number should written
on the envelope before inserting the exhibit inside. The common method used is to refer to the initials of the
person handing over the exhibit with a number following - so if John Murphy were referring to the first exhibit
in his statement it would be referred to as JM1. If the exhibit is not an original document or not required
for fingerprint identification, the exhibit number can be written on the top right of the page. If
there are a number of pages, use one identifying number and the pages can be numbered later for ease of
reference.

That the safe custody of all exhibits is secured
It is the Exhibits Officer’s responsibility to ensure that all exhibits are secured safely. The Exhibits Officer will
have to explain in court if there are any unexplained marks or alterations or damage to an exhibit, or indeed, if
the exhibit was lost. For example, this would include marks or alterations discovered through ESDA testing
carried out by the Forensic Science Laboratory at Garda Headquarters. Great care must be exercised at all times
in the custody and continuity of exhibits and if necessary a locked secure cabinet should be obtained to store
the exhibits. The Exhibits Officer alone should have access to this cabinet.

To ensure that the continuity of all exhibits is correct and included in
the relevant statements
Exhibits will be recovered from a number of sources including complainants, injured parties, witnesses and
suspects as well as from searches.
In the case of complainants, injured parties and witnesses the handing over of documents should be included
in the statement of the witness/injured party and an identifying number should be included. It should be
stated whether the document is an original or a copy. If the original is being maintained, a named individual
should be directed to retain the document in safe custody and the strict rules of evidence which apply in such
cases should be explained to them. If the witness is handing over a copy or the document is from a microfiche
or computer, they should state that the copy has been compared with the original and it is a true copy of
the original.
Searches will normally be carried out on foot of warrants or search orders obtained in the course of the
investigation. An Exhibits Officer should be appointed for each search and they should focus exclusively on this
role. It is important that the exact location of each document recovered is recorded. If the document is found

in an envelope, it should be stated whether the envelope was opened or closed. It should also be noted if
there is internet access to the search site.
Relevant documents only should be seized during the course of these searches and a full inventory should be
provided to the responsible person/suspect at the search site. This inventory should be agreed and signed by
both the senior investigating Garda and the responsible person/suspect before concluding the search
operation. This process will avoid any disputes later in regard to the documents taken possession of by the
Garda investigation team.

Presenting exhibits in court
Only documents and exhibits which have been obtained in strict conformity with the rules of evidence will be
admitted in evidence in a criminal trial. So each document or exhibit must be proved. The role of the Exhibits
Officer in court requires careful planning and organisation.
The Exhibits Officer must consult with prosecuting counsel to establish in what order the witnesses will be
called. All original statements taken and exhibits obtained during the course of the investigation must be
available in court and the Exhibits Officer should know which exhibits should be put to each witness. A witness
exhibits list should be maintained and the Exhibits Officer must be in a position to confirm exactly which
exhibits have been produced in evidence in the event of any dispute arising. In some cases, hundreds of
exhibits will be produced in the course of the trial and the exhibits officer is expected to have a precise record
of which exhibits were ultimately produced to the judge and jury.

Proving documents in evidence
Before a document is accepted in evidence, the following must be proven:
• The source of the document
• Prove all entries in the document – best evidence rule
• The history of the document
• Location of the original – if not available the witness must give evidence to the effect that after a
comprehensive search the original cannot be found and is believed to be irretrievably lost
• If the document was stored in non-legible form, that it has been reproduced in permanent legible form
and that this reproduction was effected in the course of the normal operation of a specified computer or
other system
• Where appropriate, state that the person who supplied the information cannot reasonably be expected
to have any recollection of the matters dealt with – having regard to the time elapsed or other
specified circumstances
• If the date is not shown on document, state date, or if not known, approximate date on which it
was compiled
• Any other matter which is relevant to the admissibility of the information

13.11. Disclosure
Failure to make full and complete disclosure can have fatal consequences at a criminal trial. In general, all
information and evidence in the possession of the Garda investigation team relevant to the trial of the accused
is subject to disclosure to the defence. Exceptions to this include covering reports on Garda investigation files

claimed, the basis for the claim must be set out together with a description of the documents or other
information concerned. Investigating members should be vigilant in this regard and ensure that all information
