the accused. See also Appendix II, Section 5.

13.12. Protecting Documentary Evidence
In recent years great advances have been made in the field of forensic science and it is now possible to glean
valuable evidence from an expert forensic examination of documents. Forensic tests include finger print
analysis, identification of handwriting, comparison of inks, identifying alterations, identifying indentations and
computer file back-ups. (see chapter 14 “Examination of Handwriting and Documents).
Original documents will be required whenever possible. Documents should be preserved in the same state
that they were found. No pencil or other marks should be made to the original document. Avoid unnecessary
handling. Write up a label noting where they were found, by whom and to whom they were passed. Place
each document in a clear plastic envelope. To avoid damaging the evidence only put the label on the plastic
envelope after the details have been written on the label. This will facilitate the subsequent examination of the
document without the possibility of compromising the integrity of the exhibit.
Everything, no matter how insignificant it may appear should be documented. Details can be crucial – you can
never take too many notes but you can often take too few.
The safe custody of documents and exhibits is vital – it must be proved that no unauthorised persons gained
access to them.

13.13. Fraudulent Insurance Claims
Garda Code Chapter 23.16, Volume I, sets out the procedures to be adopted on receipt of a report of
insurance fraud. An Garda Siochana and the Irish Insurance Federation (IIF) have agreed on the matters to be

The formal report should contain all relevant information surrounding the case. This should include the nature

reports, each individually numbered and identified
• Names and addresses of witnesses identified

13.14. Money laundering
The Money Laundering Investigation Unit is attached to the Garda Bureau of Fraud Investigation and includes
the national Financial Intelligence Unit which was established in conformity with EU Regulations on the matter.
The Criminal Justice Act of 1994 created the offence of Money Laundering. The definition of Money
Laundering was amended by the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001.

Definition of Money Laundering
Money Laundering as a concept may be
described in simple terms as the process by
which criminals attempt to conceal the true
origin and ownership of the proceeds of their
criminal activities. If undertaken successfully
money laundering enables criminals to
legitimise ‘dirty’ money by mingling it
with ‘clean’ money, ultimately providing
a legitimate cover for the source of
their income.
A person is guilty of money laundering if,
knowing or believing that property is or
represents the proceeds of criminal conduct or
being reckless as to whether it is or represents
such proceeds, the person, without lawful
authority or excuse (the proof of which shall
lie on them) • Converts, transfers or handles the property, or removes it from the State with the intention of:
• Concealing or disguising its true nature, source, location, disposition, movement or ownership or
any rights with respect to it, or
• Assisting another person to avoid prosecution for the criminal conduct concerned, or
• Avoiding the making of a confiscation order or a confiscation co-operation order (within the
meaning of section 46 of this Act) or frustrating its enforcement against that person or
another person.
• Conceals or disguises its true nature, source, location, disposition, movement or ownership or any rights
with respect to it, or
• Acquires, possesses or uses the property.
‘Criminal Conduct’ means conduct which constitutes an indictable offence.
In the case of DPP v McHugh, Court of Criminal Appeal (2002) the Court ruled that in order to
sustain a conviction for Money Laundering, the prosecution must prove as a fact the criminal origin
of the property in question. In this case there was no dispute that there was sufficient evidence
from which the jury were entitled to infer that the defendant knew the property, a large sum of

Chapter 13 Fraud

money, was or represented the proceeds of drug trafficking. However, it was held that the quality
of the proof offered was insufficient to establish that the money was actually of criminal origin. The
presence of traces of diamorphine on the banknotes did not establish that the notes themselves
represented the proceeds of drug trafficking, and admissions made by the accused of his suspicions
and his placement of the banknotes in the attic was proof of his suspicion or belief but not proof of
the fact that the money did represent the proceeds of drug trafficking. The conviction was
accordingly quashed.

Stages of Money Laundering
Money laundering is usually accomplished in three stages, each of which has its own characteristics and each
of which may comprise numerous transactions by the launderer. These stages are commonly referred to as:
• Placement
• Layering
• Integration

Placement
This occurs when the launderer makes the initial placement of cash proceeds derived from criminal conduct.
This initial placement can be with a financial institution, solicitor, auctioneer, estate agent, accountant, casino
or a dealer in high value goods such as jewellery, cars, expensive paintings and so on which are then resold.

Layering
This stage of money laundering occurs when the proceeds of criminal conduct are distanced from their source
by creating complex layers of financial transactions. It is also achieved by the passing of money through an
international web of legitimate businesses and ‘shell’ companies. All of this is designed to disguise the audit
trail and provide anonymity therefore making it extremely difficult for the investigator.

Integration
This is the provision of apparent legitimacy to wealth derived from criminal conduct. Where placement and
layering have succeeded the laundered proceeds now re-enter the economy with the appearance of normal
business funds.
The objective of most crimes committed is for profit, therefore almost every serious crime will have a parallel
financial crime. When investigating any serious crime consideration should always be given to the financial
activities that may be associated. This is for the purpose of tracing the profits from crime. This should be
particularly borne in mind when investigating a drug trafficking offence as Section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act
1994 as amended by Section 25 of the Criminal Justice Act 1999 states that:

“Where a person has been sentenced or otherwise dealt with by a court in
respect of one or more drug trafficking offences of which he has been convicted
on indictment, the Court ‘shall’, subject to subsection 2 and 3 of the act,
determine whether the person has benefited from drug trafficking.”
The Court then makes a confiscation order for the amount determined. This puts a responsibility on the
prosecution to determine if the suspect has gained financially from their drug trafficking and to present the
facts/proofs to the Court.
Other benefits of conducting an investigation into the financial element of the crime is the intelligence gleaned
including details of the suspect’s travel history, associates, links to other crimes and criminals and so on.

Investigation of Suspicious Transaction Reports
Section 57 of the Criminal Justice Act 1994 places a statutory obligation on all designated bodies to report
suspicious transactions such as suspected money laundering (section 31) or identification details (section 32) to
the Gardai and Revenue Commissioners.
All suspicious transaction reports by financial institutions and other designated bodies are made to the Money
Laundering Investigation Unit at GBFI, Harcourt Square, Harcourt Street, Dublin 2. On receipt of the disclosures
they are inputted into a computer database. They are then analysed and disseminated for attention either
within GBFI or to Detective Sergeants in each Garda Division who have been trained in the investigation of
suspicious transaction reports.

Investigation Rules
No approach to a suspect should be made unless:
• A three-way link is established between the money, the crime and the suspect – evidence must exist to link
all three
• Evidence is legally obtained on foot of a court order pursuant to Section 63 of the Criminal Justice Act
1994 or other lawful authority
• Existence of the crime (predicate offence) must be established
A progress report on each investigation should be forwarded to Chief Superintendent, Garda Bureau of Fraud
Investigation on a three monthly basis.

Money Laundering Restraint and Confiscation Orders
Section 24 Criminal Justice Act 1994
This section provides for a Restraint Order granted by the High Court on application of the DPP. The following
criteria apply:
• The High Court must be satisfied that proceedings have commenced or that proceedings are to be
instituted
• A direction from the DPP to prosecute is sufficient
This applies to a drug trafficking or other indictable offence. Proceedings do not have to be concluded.
Where a restraint order has been granted, Gardai or Customs Officers may seize property to prevent its
removal from the State.
Section 4 Criminal Justice Act, 1994 as amended by Section 25 Criminal Justice Act 1999
This applies only to the proceeds of drug trafficking. The accused must be convicted on indictment. The DPP
makes application to have the Court conduct an assessment of the benefit from the crime. The Court then
determines the benefit and makes an order.
Section 9 Criminal Justice Act, 1994
This applies to proceeds of all forms of criminal activity where a monetary gain has been made with the
exception of drug trafficking. The accused must be convicted on indictment. An application for a confiscation
order for the amount ‘benefited’ only is made by the DPP to the Court.
The difference between both confiscation orders is that under Section 4 the Court determines the amount to
be confiscated whereas under Section 9 the order must be for the amount benefited only.

Orders to make material available
An order pursuant to Section 63 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1994 may be obtained for the purpose
of uplifting financial information and other documentary evidence believed to be connected to a
criminal investigation.
To obtain an order under this section the Judge of a Court must be satisfied that the investigation involves:
• An offence under Section 31 Criminal Justice Act, 1994 as amended (a money laundering offence) or
• A drug trafficking offence or
• The commission of an offence of financing terrorism
• Whether a person holds funds subject to confiscation
• An investigation into whether a person has benefited from a drug trafficking offence or
• An offence in respect of which a confiscation order might be made under Section 9 of the Criminal Justice
Act, 1994 as amended.
In the case of Dylan Creaven –v- Judge Patrick Clyne and the Minister for JELR [2006] IEHC 290, Clarke J. held
that in order to justify the making of a production order under the final ground listed above (confiscation order
under section 9 CJA 1994), there must be an investigation into whether a person has benefited from an
offence in respect of which a confiscation order might be made. The Judge also stated

“…It seems to me that the natural meaning of the provision as drafted
concentrates on the investigation into the benefit. It is, of course the case that
certain types of offences lend themselves more naturally to the possibility of an
application for a confiscation order being made post conviction. Cases of fraud
are an obvious example. Assuming the fraud to have been successful then it is
likely that the persons guilty of fraudulent activity would have benefited and
will, therefore, be open to the possibility of a confiscation order…”
It is essential that one of the conditions outlined above exists before applying to the Court for an order under
this Section.

Warrant pursuant to Section 64 Criminal Justice Act 1994 as amended.
It must be noted that the same conditions required for application of a Section 63 Order exist for the
application to a Court for a search warrant pursuant to Section 64 Criminal Justice Act, 1994.
Before granting a warrant under Section 64 Criminal Justice Act, 1994 as amended the judge must be satisfied
that it would not be appropriate to make an order pursuant to Section 63, Criminal Justice Act, 1994 as
amended in relation to the material sought because:
• It is not practicable to communicate with any person entitled to produce the material sought or
• It is not practicable to communicate with any person entitled to grant access to the material or entitled to
grant entry to the premises in which the material is held or
• That the investigation for which the warrant was sought might be seriously prejudiced unless immediate
entry and access to the material is secured or
• Entry to the premises will be denied unless a warrant is produced.

Tipping off Offence
Section 58 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1994 as amended provides for various offences of prejudicing an
investigation under the Act including the offence of ‘tipping off’.
A person shall be guilty of an offence where they, knowing or suspecting that an investigation is taking place,
makes any disclosure which is likely to prejudice that investigation. This applies in relation to an investigation
into drug trafficking or an investigation into whether a person has benefited from an offence in respect of
which a confiscation order might be made, or an order under Section 63 of the Act has been made or applied
for, or a warrant under Section 64 of the Act has been issued, or a report under Section 57 of the Act is made.
This offence carries a five year prison sentence on conviction on indictment.

13.15. Terrorist Financing
Terrorist financing came into sharp focus in the law enforcement community following the 9/11 attacks in the
US in 2001. The ease with which the suspects were able to move money through the banking system to fund
the attacks was noted. In response to this threat, the United States and its allies decided to enact laws to
prevent the use of the banking system by international terrorists and terrorist groups. The European Union
adapted what is known as the Framework Decision on Combating Terrorism. This took place on June 13th
2002 in Luxembourg. In response, Ireland enacted the Criminal Justice (Terrorist Offences) Act, 2005 on the
March 8th 2005.
This Act is designed to combat international terrorism and terrorist financing. The Act provides a legal
definition of ‘terrorism’ and ‘terrorist activity’ and a ‘terrorist group’. The Act also creates various offences
in which terrorist groups may be involved.
This section focuses only on the offence of terrorist financing.
Terrorist financing is the means by which these groups fund their operations. This includes everything from day
to day running costs, through funding the families of terrorists killed, wounded or captured in action, to the
running of an operation in their own native country or abroad.
Essentially, terrorist funding is the means by which terrorist groups raise funds as well as how they move funds
from one jurisdiction to another.
Terrorist financing has similarities to money laundering. The major difference between the two offences is
that criminals launder money in order to make it appear legitimate, thus enabling them to ‘live the high life’
while the terrorist uses these funds to further a cause. The money launderer is attempting to ‘clean’ so-called
dirty money whereas the terrorist intends to use what is often ‘clean’ money for the commission of t
errorist offences.
The terrorist group, however, has different reasons for disguising its funds; it does this in order to prevent the
authorities from disrupting its operations and to prevent the identification of persons moving funds on
their behalf.
The methods by which terrorist groups fund themselves are many and varied. They are known to raise
donations from supporters – this activity has coined the phrase laundering of clean funds. Another method is
by the misuse of Non-Governmental Organisations (NGO’s). Terrorist groups are also involved in fraud,
extortion, kidnapping, smuggling and the production of counterfeit goods. Funds have also been donated to
these groups by wealthy individuals who have sympathies to their various causes and objectives.
Section 13 of the Criminal Justice (Terrorist Offences) Act, 2005 is the section which creates the offence of
financing terrorism. It provides that a person is guilty of an offence if, within or outside the State, the person
by any means directly or indirectly, unlawfully and wilfully provides, collects or receives funds, intending that

25

10:58

Page 26

Chapter 13 Fraud

they be used or knowing that they will be used in whole or in part in order to carry out the various activities
described in the section which amount to a terrorist offence.
In order to establish an offence of terrorist financing, it is necessary to prove that the suspect, by whatever
means, collected funds with the intent that these funds be used to perpetrate any of the acts described in the
section. This requirement can place a heavy onus of proof on the prosecution in that it is necessary to link the
funds identified to a specific terrorist action in this or another country.
What also makes this a difficult offence to prosecute is the fact that the action taken by a terrorist group may
not fit within terms of the Act. An example of this would be if a terrorist group was to carry out construction
work for a particular community with a view to obtaining that community’s assistance at some stage in the
future. The terrorist group will offer service far in excess of anything which the Government of the country will
provide and the terrorist group will ensure that this fact is widely known within the community concerned.
It is suspected that terrorist groups are using Irish couriers to move funds from one country to another. The
amounts of funds at issue in these cases are usually quite small and may involve an individual carrying as little
as a few hundred euro.
Indeed, it has been revealed that the amounts of money involved in mounting a terrorist operation can be
quite insignificant in comparison to the carnage and loss of life caused. The September 11 attacks in the US
cost an estimated US$250,000. The Madrid attacks cost approximately €10,000 while the London bombings
cost between stg£1,000 to stg£8,000, the higher figure being accounted for by reason of travel to Pakistan.
Terrorist groups are also suspected of using underground banking systems and money remittance companies
to move money.
The use of mainstream banking systems can be exploited by terrorists by transferring funds by electronic funds
transfer (EFT) from one country to another and passing it to an individual who is not known or suspected of
involvement in terrorist funding The funds can be moved in this way without coming to the notice of the
relevant authorities.
It is the low cost of these operations that make the movement of money in terrorist related-related offences so
difficult to spot. Financial Institutions are obliged to report transactions which they deem suspicious. They can
spot unusual activity in an account when large unexplained sums of money are lodged. It is a lot more difficult
to spot €100 or €200 lodged and transferred out.
Another method of fundraising is through obtaining relatively small loans of between €10,000 and €15,000
and claiming the funds are to be used to purchase a car. When the funds are approved and lodged into the
customers account they are then transferred from that account to an account in another country. They will
then usually give excuses such as that they are repaying a relative who has already supplied the money to
purchase the car. In a lot of cases these loans are then paid off properly.

13.16. Cheque, Payment Card Fraud and Counterfeiting
Members are referred to the “An Garda Siochana - Best Practice in Crime Prevention and Reduction” Manual –
chapter 5 for further information under this heading.
Cheque and payment card fraud may be dealt with by one or a combination of offences under the Criminal
Justice (Theft & Fraud) Offences Act, 2001 including theft, deception or forgery depending on the
circumstances of each case. The provisions of section 15 of the Act should also be kept in mind in respect of
sophisticated ATM frauds and credit card ‘skimming’ equipment (see discussion above under the Criminal
Justice [Theft & Fraud Offences] Act, 2001. Members are also referred to the counterfeiting provisions
contained within this Act).

Cheques
The Irish Court of Criminal Appeal in the case of People [AG] v Thompson alias Morrison, CCA 13 Oct, 1960,
1 Frewen 201 at 207 has held that the following representations are implicit in writing a cheque:
• That the drawer has an account at the bank on which the cheque is drawn
• That they have authority to draw on it for the amount of the cheque
• That the cheque as drawn is a valid order for the payment of the amount for which it is drawn in that the
state of affairs then existing is such that, in the ordinary course of events, the cheque will be duly
honoured on the date appearing on it or subsequently
• Unless indications are otherwise, that it will be presented for payment immediately
Where an accused writes a cheque having no funds but
genuinely anticipating being able to make a sufficient
lodgement before the cheque is presented, they are guilty
of making a false representation but would be held not to
have the necessary intent to defraud under the old offence
of obtaining by false pretences. It is unlikely that such
conduct would now come within the present offences
without some evidence of an intention at the time of
writing the cheque of both deceiving and obtaining the
property without a claim of right made in good faith. Such
cases can come within the current definition of Theft
contrary to Section 4 of the Criminal Justice (Theft & Fraud
Offences) Act, 2001.
Members investigating such crimes should look for evidence as to the suspect’s intention at the time of
the transaction. Enquiries with the suspect’s bank may reveal that they had been communicated with and
advised that no further payments would be honoured on their account or other similar evidence may also be
obtained. It will depend on the individual circumstances surrounding each case. Members seeking guidance
may contact the Cheque & Payment Card Section at GBFI for advice on individual cases.

Chip and Pin Payment Cards
‘Chip & Pin’ technology combines two effective security features. The first is a microchip on payment cards
that stores card data more securely than the current magnetic stripe so it is much harder to counterfeit (clone)
or skim. The second is the four-digit PIN which is known only by the cardholder and is much harder to copy
than a signature.

Counterfeit Cheques & Drafts
Recent trends have shown an increase in the number of counterfeit cheques and bank drafts in circulation.
Technical advances and inexpensive home computers and scanners have allowed the criminal to create good
quality counterfeits in a home environment. The most frequent method of production is by scanning a
legitimate cheque or draft into a computer and manipulating the payee details to create a counterfeit. If a
hologram is required this can be added by using special inks or secondary fixings such as stuck on holograms.
In the genuine draft, the hologram is manufactured into the paper of the draft whereas on the counterfeit
they are placed on top of the paper and will be obvious to the touch on running a fingernail across the draft.
The counterfeit holograms created with special inks may appear or feel flush but they are easily scratched and
less durable than the legitimate hologram.
Criminals tend to present counterfeit cheques/drafts outside banking hours when purchasing goods so that
the draft cannot be checked immediately. To frustrate prosecutions or to give themselves an alibi they
masquerade as or use couriers to collect the purchased items and hand over the counterfeit in an envelope.

While each case differs the possession of such counterfeits can amount to offences under the Criminal Justice
(Theft & Fraud Offences) Act, 2001. (See earlier discussion on the various offences within this Act.)

Card-not-present Fraud
Criminals commit this form of payment card fraud by obtaining the genuine cardholder’s details surreptitiously
and then using these details to purchase goods over the phone or on the Internet. Criminals may obtain these
details by intercepting post addressed to the victim or by obtaining the victim’s discarded payment card
receipts (bin raiding). Criminals have also been known to listen to the victim while they purchase goods over
the telephone or watching while the victim enters personal identification numbers at cash machines or similar
cash outlets (shoulder surfing). The victim is often unaware that their account has been compromised until
receipt of their next bank statement.

Counterfeit Card Fraud
A counterfeit (cloned) or skimmed card is one that has been printed, embossed or encoded without permission
from the issuer or one that has been validly issued and then altered or re-encoded.
Most cases of counterfeit fraud involve skimming; a process where the genuine data on a card’s magnetic
stripe is electronically copied onto another without the legitimate cardholder’s knowledge.

‘Skimming’
‘Skimming’ occurs when the genuine data from the magnetic stripe on one card is copied without the
cardholder’s knowledge and placed on another card. Skimming may occur when a cardholder hands over their
card to make a purchase, for example in bars, restaurants and petrol stations, and where a corrupt employee
skims a customer’s card before handing it back. Skimming may also occur when the cardholder uses their card
at an Automated Teller Machine (ATM). In both circumstances the card’s details are secretly copied during the
transaction and in some cases the cardholder’s Personal Identification Number (PIN) is compromised.
The information obtained may then be used to produce counterfeit cards or in other cases, the details are used
to carry out fraudulent card-not-present transactions. Most often, cardholders are unaware of the fraud until a
statement is received showing purchases they did not make. (See also above under ‘Chip & Pin Payment
Cards’.)
What to look for when conducting a search in investigating cheque/payment card fraud.
The following list of items is typically found in the possession of criminals engaged in this type of crime.
• Cards
• Point-of-sale terminals (technically card readers)
• Card readers
• Card writers/encoders (which have a slot to swipe the cards and
generally look like card readers)
• Computers e.g. PC, laptop, notebook, palmtop, PDA (Personal
Data Assistant) and USB devices
• Parts of computers
• Floppy disks/CD-ROMs (both storage devices may contain
valuable evidence)
• Cables/electronics/moulds/spray paint

• Do NOT remove any batteries - you may destroy the evidence
• Seize all cables, floppy disks, computers etc, we need ALL the evidence;
• Photograph/video the scene if possible
• Call immediately for expert assistance – some units are battery operated and vital evidence can be lost
when the battery expires or is incorrectly examined

13.17. Identity theft
ID theft occurs when a criminal uses fraudulently obtained personal information to open or access bank
accounts in someone else’s name. There are two types – application fraud and account take-over.

Application Fraud
Application fraud involves criminals using stolen or false
documents to open an account in someone else’s name.
Criminals steal documents such as utility bills and bank
statements to build up usable information. Alternatively, they
may use counterfeit documents for identification purposes.
This constitutes breaches of the Criminal Justice (Theft & Fraud
Offences) Act, 2001 - including section 26 using a false
instrument (the false documents). A breach of Section 32(12)
Criminal Justice Act 1994 (opening a bank account in a false
name) may also be disclosed.

Account take-over
This involves criminals taking over another person’s account by first gathering personal information about the
intended victim (bin raiding, Dumpster-diving and so on). The criminal then contacts the card issuer,
masquerading as the genuine cardholder, requesting mail be redirected to a new address. The criminal then
reports the card lost and requests a replacement to be sent to the new address. In some cases redirection of
mail is done through the local post office.

13.18. Cash machine fraud (ATM)
Criminals commit fraud at a cash machine in a number of ways.

Shoulder Surfing
This involves criminals looking over a cardholder’s shoulder to watch the PIN being entered then stealing the
card using distraction techniques or pick pocketing.

Card-rapping Devices (i.e. Lebanese Loop)
This is a device which is inserted into a cash machine’s card slot and will retain the card inside the cash
machine for subsequent recovery by the criminal. The criminal tricks the victim into re-entering the PIN while
the criminal watches. After the cardholder gives up and leaves, the criminal removes the device, with the card,
and withdraws cash.

Skimming at Cash Machines
A skimming device is attached to the card entry slot. A false keypad may be used or a miniature camera may
be hidden overlooking the PIN pad to capture the user’s PIN. The normal transaction takes place and the user
does not lose their card. (See photo page 28). The criminal can then produce a counterfeit card and withdraw
money at a cash machine using the legitimate PIN. (Any plastic card such as club cards and store loyalty cards
with a magnetic stripe can be recoded with the information.
Possession of such devices can constitute a breach of the Criminal Justice (Theft & Fraud Offences) Act, 2001 –
including section 15(1) covering possession of certain articles.

13.18.1. Counterfeit Currency
When a seizure of counterfeit currency is made the member finding the notes or coins should treat all notes as
exhibits and apply the correct handling procedure. All seizures of counterfeit currency must be reported to
Detective Chief Superintendent, Garda Bureau of Fraud Investigation. See Garda Code for the procedure to
be followed.
Where currency believed to be counterfeit is found and a suspect is identified, all of the notes should be
exhibited and labelled and transferred as soon as possible to the Garda Technical Bureau. Notes should first be
brought to Document and Handwriting Section for examination so that a statement can be prepared for any
subsequent prosecution. On completion of this examination the notes should then be brought to Fingerprint
Section for examination there.
Security features
The following is a list of some of the security features that can be easily checked to help members decide
whether the notes should be treated as suspect or not. Further information on security features is available at
www.garda.ie, www.contralbank.ie and www.euro.ecb.int.
Firstly the Euro banknotes are printed on special banknote paper made mainly from cotton fibres. It feels crisp
– not limp or waxy. Counterfeiters should not be able to get hold of such paper.
On euro banknotes you can feel the initials of the European Central Bank (i.e. the letters BCE, ECB, EZB, EKT,
EKP), the value numerals and the window and gateway motifs. Please be aware that age and wear can
gradually reduce some of these properties.
Look for the watermark, the security thread and the ‘see-through’ feature. Hold the banknote up to a light.
The watermark is visible on both sides of the same unprinted area. You can therefore see both the main
architectural motif and the value numeral. The watermark is created inside the paper by varying the paper
